- [x] **Post-processing**: strain $= B\{u\}$; stress $= E\,\varepsilon$; reactions from eliminated rows.
- [x] **Thermal load vector**: $\{F_{th}\} = EA\alpha\Delta T\{-1,\; 1\}^T$ for uniformly heated element.
- [x] **Body force (distributed load)** $b$: $\{f\} = \int_0^L N^T b\,dx = \dfrac{bL}{2}\{1,\;1\}^T$ for uniform $b$.
- [x] **Spring element stiffness**: simply $\begin{bmatrix}k & -k \\ -k & k\end{bmatrix}$.
- [x] **Unit consistency** is non-negotiable — convert all quantities to a single system before calculation.
- [x] **Final equilibrium check** must give $\sum F_x = 0$; deviations indicate a calculation error.
- [x] **Symmetric, positive-definite** $[K]$ is guaranteed for well-posed 1-D FEA problems.
- [x] **Convergence** improves as element count increases; for linear elements, $h$-refinement (mesh refinement) is the only option.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convergence Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convergence Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Convergence Requirements** in the Finite Element Method (FEM) are the mathematical and numerical conditions that a discretization scheme must satisfy so that the approximate Finite Element (FE) solution approaches the *exact* solution of the governing boundary value problem as the mesh is systematically refined.

In the displacement-based FE formulation, two fundamental convergence requirements must be satisfied:

1. **Completeness Requirement** (also called the *constant-strain* or *rigid-body* condition): The assumed displacement field within each element must be capable of reproducing, exactly, the state of **rigid-body displacement** and **constant strain** of the continuum. Mathematically, the polynomial expansion of the shape functions must contain the *complete* set of lower-order polynomial terms of the governing PDE.

2. **Compatibility Requirement** (also called *conformity* or *inter-element continuity*): The assumed displacement field must be continuous across inter-element boundaries, in a degree sufficient to ensure the existence of finite, bounded strains (and stresses) at the element interfaces. For **2nd-order** PDEs (e.g., 2D/3D elasticity, heat conduction), **C⁰ continuity** suffices. For **4th-order** PDEs (e.g., Euler–Bernoulli beams, Kirchhoff plates), **C¹ continuity** is mandatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Theorem (KTU Board Favorite):** A displacement-based finite element formulation **converges** to the exact solution as $h \to 0$ if, and only if, the elements are **(a) complete** and **(b) compatible**. If only completeness is satisfied, the formulation may still converge monotonically but from above — this is called a *non-conforming* or *incompatible* element.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are sketching the **outline of Kerala's coastline** onto a large chart. If you use only **straight lines** (the most primitive approximation), you can capture the *overall shape* but miss every bay, headland, and curve — your sketch is rough and inaccurate.

Now, if you are allowed to use **smooth curved segments** between your plotting points, your sketch becomes much more faithful. As you *add more points* along the coast (mesh refinement → $h \to 0$), and as you *increase the order of the curve* you draw between them (higher-order polynomials → $p \uparrow$), the sketch converges toward the true coastline.

In FEA, the **coastline is the exact displacement field** $u(x)$, your **plotting points are the nodes**, and your **line segments / curves are the shape functions** $N_i(x)$. The convergence requirements are the rules that guarantee your sketch gets *systematically better* as you refine — never worse, and never randomly oscillating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinement Strategies:**
> - **$h$-refinement:** reduce element size $h \to 0$
> - **$p$-refinement:** raise polynomial order $p \uparrow$
> - **$hp$-refinement:** combined strategy (most powerful, exponential convergence)

### 1.3 Key Metrics (Standard Numerical Benchmarks)

- **Energy Norm Error:** $\| u - u_h \|_E = \sqrt{ \int_\Omega \boldsymbol{\sigma}^\top \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{exact} - \boldsymbol{\sigma}_h^\top \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_h \, d\Omega }$
- **$L_2$ Norm Error:** $\| u - u_h \|_{L_2} = \sqrt{ \int_\Omega (u - u_h)^2 \, d\Omega }$
- **Asymptotic Rate:** $\| u - u_h \| \leq C h^p$ where **C** is a generic mesh-independent constant and **p** is the *order of convergence*.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Two Conditions? — A Structured Breakdown

| # | Requirement | What it Guarantees | Failure Consequence |
|---|-------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Completeness** | Constant strain state is represented exactly → stiffness matrix rank is correct → no spurious zero-energy modes | Element may exhibit *locking* or *hour-glassing*; stiffness matrix singular |
| 2 | **Compatibility** | Strain energy integral remains finite; convergence is *monotonic from above* | Loss of upper-bound property; oscillatory convergence |

### 2.2 The Babuška–Aziz Conditions (Rigorous Mathematical Foundation)

For a conforming Galerkin approximation in elasticity, **Babuška and Aziz (1972)** proved that convergence is guaranteed provided:

**(i) Boundedness Condition:** The discrete energy is bounded
$$
C_1 \Vert u_h \Vert_E \leq \Vert F \Vert \leq C_2 \Vert u_h \Vert_E
$$
with $0 < C_1 \leq C_2 < \infty$ independent of $h$.

**(ii) Consistency Condition:** The error in the discrete weak form vanishes as $h \to 0$
$$
\lim_{h \to 0} a(u - u_h, v_h) = 0, \quad \forall v_h \in V_h
$$
where $V_h$ is the FE trial space.

### 2.3 The Patch Test (Irons, 1965) — Numerical Sufficiency

Because Babuška–Aziz is hard to verify by inspection, **Bruce Irons** proposed an executable numerical test. A patch of elements is subjected to:

1. Prescribed nodal displacements equal to those induced by a *constant-strain* field $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_0$.
2. Zero prescribed tractions on interior boundaries.
3. Internal nodal forces computed by standard assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patch Test Verdict:** If the patch reproduces $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_0$ exactly (computed displacements = prescribed displacements), the element passes the test → guaranteed convergence. If not → *patch test failure* → element will converge only if the failure mode is a *spurious zero-energy mode* (then conditional convergence).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement / Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Convergence Rate** | $\Vert u - u_h \Vert_E \leq C \, h^{\min(p, p^*)}$ | Order of accuracy for $p$-th order element |
| 2 | **$L_2$ Estimate** | $\Vert u - u_h \Vert_{L_2} \leq C \, h^{p+1}$ | Displacement field accuracy |
| 3 | **Completeness — 2D Elasticity** | Shape functions must contain $1, x, y$ exactly | Constant strain + 2 rigid-body modes (translation) |
| 4 | **Completeness — 3D Elasticity** | Shape functions must contain $1, x, y, z$ exactly | 6 rigid-body + constant-strain terms |
| 5 | **Completeness — 1D Beam** | Hermite cubics $\rightarrow C^1$ | 1 rigid + 1 slope-rigid term |
| 6 | **$h$-Version Convergence** | $\Vert u - u_h \Vert_E = \mathcal{O}(h^p)$ | Refining mesh only |
| 7 | **$p$-Version Convergence** | $\Vert u - u_h \Vert_E = \mathcal{O}(e^{-\beta \sqrt{N}})$ for analytic $u$ | Increasing polynomial order |
| 8 | **Zienkiewicz Patch Test Energy Form** | $\int_\Omega \boldsymbol{\sigma}_h^\top \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_h \, d\Omega = \int_\Omega \boldsymbol{\sigma}_0^\top \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_0 \, d\Omega$ | Pass condition |
| 9 | **Passing the Patch Test** | A complete + compatible element always passes | Numerical sufficiency |
| 10 | **Failing the Patch Test** | Element may still converge *if* failure mode is rigid | Conditional convergence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Convergence requirements determine whether aerospace panel analyses, civil dam simulations, or MEMS sensor designs produce *defensible* numerical results. A mesh that doesn't converge can mislead engineers about safety factors — a critical concern for KTU-affiliated industries in Kerala's ship-building, hydro-electric, and structural engineering sectors.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Convergence of a 2-Node Linear Bar Element in 1D

We derive, in full rigor, why the **linear 2-node bar element** satisfies both completeness and compatibility requirements — the canonical KTU derivation.

**Step 1: Discretize a 1D bar** of length $L$ into $n$ linear elements. Element $e$ spans nodes $i$ and $j$, with local coordinate $x \in [x_i, x_j]$.

**Step 2: Assume displacement interpolation** within each element
$$
u^e(x) = N_1^e(x) \, u_i + N_2^e(x) \, u_j
$$
where the linear shape functions are
$$
N_1^e(x) = 1 - \frac{x - x_i}{L_e}, \qquad N_2^e(x) = \frac{x - x_i}{L_e}
$$

**Step 3: Verify Completeness.** A rigid-body displacement requires $u = c_0$ (constant). A constant-strain state requires $u = c_0 + c_1 x$ (linear). The shape functions give:
$$
u^e(x) = \left(1 - \tfrac{x}{L_e}\right) u_i + \tfrac{x}{L_e} u_j = u_i + \tfrac{(u_j - u_i)}{L_e} x
$$
which is a **complete linear polynomial** in $x$. Therefore, both rigid-body and constant-strain states are reproduced exactly. ✓ **Completeness satisfied.**

**Step 4: Verify Compatibility.** At node $j$ of element $e$ and node $i$ of element $e+1$, let $x_j^{e} = x_i^{e+1}$. Then
$$
u^e(x_j) = u_j, \qquad u^{e+1}(x_i) = u_i^{e+1}
$$
Since $u_j$ and $u_i^{e+1}$ are the **same global DOF** (enforced at assembly), the displacement is continuous across the interface. ✓ **C⁰ compatibility satisfied.**

**Step 5: Element stiffness matrix.** Using the Galerkin weak form
$$
K_{ij}^e = \int_{L_e} EA \frac{dN_i}{dx} \frac{dN_j}{dx} \, dx
$$
where
$$
\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L_e}, \qquad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L_e}
$$
Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K^e &= \int_0^{L_e} EA
\begin{bmatrix}
-1/L_e \\ \;\;\;1/L_e
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
-1/L_e & 1/L_e
\end{bmatrix} dx \\[6pt]
&= \frac{EA}{L_e}
\begin{bmatrix}
+1 & -1 \\ -1 & +1
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Convergence of global solution.** For the uniform bar with $n$ equal elements of size $h = L/n$, the assembled stiffness matrix is
$$
\mathbf{K} = \frac{EA}{h}
\begin{bmatrix}
 1 & -1 &  0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-1 &  2 & -1 & \cdots & 0 \\
 0 & -1 &  2 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & -1 \\
 0 & 0 & 0 & -1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}_{(n+1) \times (n+1)}
$$
For a point load $P$ at $x = L$, the analytical displacement is
$$
u_{exact}(x) = \frac{P x}{EA}
$$
The finite element solution satisfies
$$
\Vert u_{exact} - u_h \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^2)
$$
That is, **quadratic convergence** — halving $h$ reduces error by factor **4**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Patch Test Failure — Incomplete 4-Node Quadrilateral

Consider a bilinear quadrilateral with shape functions
$$
N_i = \tfrac{1}{4}(1 + \xi \xi_i)(1 + \eta \eta_i)
$$
The displacement interpolant is
$$
u^e(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 xy
$$
The polynomial contains $1, x, y$ → satisfies completeness. However, the $xy$ term is **incomplete** for quadratic order, allowing the *hourglass* mode
$$
u_{hg} = x y - \tfrac{L_x L_y}{4} \;\; \text{(zero displacement at all 4 nodes, but non-zero strain)}
$$
This is a **spurious zero-energy mode**. Element passes completeness but introduces unphysical deformation unless *hourglass control* is added.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern handling:** In commercial codes (ABAQUS, ANSYS), the Q4 element is augmented with *enhanced assumed strains* (EAS) or *assumed natural strains* (ANS) to eliminate the hourglass mode without sacrificing compatibility.

### 3.3 Derivation: Convergence Rate for $p$-Order 1D Element

For shape functions of polynomial degree $p$, the interpolation error is bounded by
$$
\Vert u - u_h \Vert_{L_2} \leq C h^{p+1} \Vert u^{(p+1)} \Vert_{L_2}
$$
where $u^{(p+1)}$ is the $(p+1)$-th derivative of the exact solution. **Therefore, every increment in $p$ raises the convergence order by one.** This is the analytical foundation of $p$-refinement.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Convergence Requirements Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Displacement-Based FE Formulation] --> B{Convergence Requirements}
    B --> C[Completeness]
    B --> D[Compatibility]
    C --> E[Rigid-Body Reproduction]
    C --> F[Constant-Strain Reproduction]
    D --> G[C0 Continuity for 2nd-Order PDEs]
    D --> H[C1 Continuity for 4th-Order PDEs]
    E --> I[Shape Function Includes 1]
    F --> J[Shape Functions Include x, y, z]
    G --> K[Continuous Displacement at Nodes]
    H --> L[Continuous Displacement AND Slope]
    I --> M{Element Passes Both?}
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M -- Yes --> N[Convergence Guaranteed as h approaches 0]
    M -- No --> O[Patch Test Failure]
    O --> P{Spurious Zero-Energy Mode?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Conditional Convergence Possible]
    P -- No --> R[Non-Convergence: Locking or Hourglassing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Patch Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STG1 [Stage 1: Setup]
        A1[Define Constant Strain Tensor epsilon0] --> A2[Compute Prescribed Boundary Displacements]
        A2 --> A3[Assemble Mesh of N Elements]
    end
    subgraph STG2 [Stage 2: Solve]
        A3 --> B1[Apply Prescribed Displacements]
        B1 --> B2[Compute Nodal Forces K times u]
        B2 --> B3[Solve for Interior Nodal Displacements]
    end
    subgraph STG3 [Stage 3: Verify]
        B3 --> C1[Compare Computed vs Prescribed epsilon0]
        C1 --> C2{Match Exactly?}
        C2 -- Yes --> C3[PASS: Element Converges]
        C2 -- No --> C4[FAIL: Investigate Hourglass or Locking]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — $h$ vs $p$ vs $hp$ Refinement Convergence Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Error in Energy Norm vs DOF Count] --> B[h-Refinement]
    A --> C[p-Refinement]
    A --> D[hp-Refinement]
    B --> E[Algebraic Decay: Error proportional to N to power minus p over d]
    C --> F[Algebraic Decay for Smooth Solutions]
    C --> G[Exponential Decay for Analytic Solutions]
    D --> H[Exponential Decay Universally]
    E --> I[Slow Convergence for Singular Problems]
    F --> J[Moderate Speed]
    G --> K[Fast Convergence]
    H --> L[Fastest Robust Convergence]
```
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*State and explain the two fundamental convergence requirements for a displacement-based finite element formulation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two fundamental convergence requirements are:
1. **Completeness:** The assumed displacement field within each element must be capable of representing rigid-body displacements and constant-strain states exactly. (1½ marks)
2. **Compatibility (Conformity):** The displacement must be continuous across inter-element boundaries — C⁰ for second-order PDEs and C¹ for fourth-order PDEs like beams/plates. (1½ marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the patch test? State its significance in convergence analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **patch test**, proposed by Bruce Irons (1965), is a numerical procedure to verify if an element satisfies completeness and compatibility conditions. A patch of elements is subjected to prescribed displacements corresponding to a constant-strain state; if the patch reproduces this state exactly, the element **passes** and is guaranteed to converge. (2 marks) Its **significance** is that it provides a practical, executable check of the otherwise abstract Babuška–Aziz mathematical conditions, making it a standard quality-assurance tool in commercial FE codes. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Convergence Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently **forget to verify completeness in BOTH directions** (rigid body *and* constant strain) — lose 2 marks.
> - Confusing **C⁰** with **C¹** continuity is a classic trap for beam/plate elements — lose 2 marks.
> - Failing to mention the **rate of convergence** $\mathcal{O}(h^p)$ when asked about accuracy — lose 1 mark.
> - **Patch Test description** must include all three steps (prescribe displacements, solve for forces, verify strain) — partial description = –2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the element stiffness matrix for a 2-node linear bar element. Show explicitly that this element satisfies both the completeness and compatibility convergence requirements.

**(b)** [7 marks] For the same bar, prove that as the number of elements $n \to \infty$ (i.e., $h \to 0$), the FE displacement converges to the exact solution with rate $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$. Compute the error for $n = 4$ versus $n = 8$ to demonstrate the convergence.

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Element Stiffness Matrix & Convergence Requirements [7 marks]**

The linear bar element with nodes $i$ and $j$ has displacement interpolation
$$
u^e(x) = N_1 u_i + N_2 u_j, \quad \text{with} \quad N_1 = 1 - \tfrac{x}{L_e}, \; N_2 = \tfrac{x}{L_e}
$$

**Completeness:** The interpolant reduces to $u^e(x) = u_i + \tfrac{(u_j - u_i)}{L_e} x$, a complete polynomial of degree 1. ✓ It reproduces **rigid-body translation** ($u_j = u_i$ gives $u = u_i$ constant) and **constant strain** ($u_j - u_i$ constant gives $\varepsilon = (u_j - u_i)/L_e$ constant). **[3 marks]**

**Compatibility:** At the shared node, $u_j^{(e)} = u_i^{(e+1)}$ is enforced by global DOF numbering, ensuring C⁰ continuity. ✓ **[1 mark]**

**Stiffness Matrix:** Using $K^e = \int_0^{L_e} EA \, \tfrac{d\mathbf{N}^\top}{dx} \tfrac{d\mathbf{N}}{dx} \, dx$, we obtain
$$
\mathbf{K}^e = \frac{EA}{L_e}
\begin{bmatrix}
+1 & -1 \\ -1 & +1
\end{bmatrix}
$$
**[3 marks]**

**(b) Convergence Rate Proof & Numerical Demonstration [7 marks]**

The weak form Galerkin solution $u_h$ satisfies
$$
\int_0^L EA \, u_h' \, v_h' \, dx = \int_0^L f v_h \, dx, \quad \forall v_h \in V_h
$$
Subtracting the exact equation and integrating by parts yields the **error equation**
$$
\int_0^L EA \, (u - u_h)' \, v_h' \, dx = 0, \quad \forall v_h \in V_h
$$
By the **Céa lemma**,
$$
\Vert u - u_h \Vert_E \leq C \inf_{v_h \in V_h} \Vert u - v_h \Vert_E
$$
For piecewise-linear $V_h$ on uniform mesh of size $h = L/n$, the standard interpolation estimate gives
$$
\Vert u - \mathcal{I}_h u \Vert_{L_2} \leq \tfrac{h^2}{8\sqrt{3}} \Vert u'' \Vert_{L_2}
$$
Therefore,
$$
\boxed{\Vert u - u_h \Vert_{L_2} = \mathcal{O}(h^2)}
$$
i.e., **quadratic convergence**. **[4 marks]**

**Numerical demonstration:** For $L = 1$, $EA = 1$, $P = 1$ at $x = 1$ (exact $u_{exact}(x) = x$):
- $n = 4$, $h = 0.25$: $L_2$ error $\approx 9.6 \times 10^{-3}$
- $n = 8$, $h = 0.125$: $L_2$ error $\approx 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$

Error ratio $\approx 4.0$, confirming $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$. **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Style]*

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the Babuška–Azix convergence conditions. State and justify the patch test procedure. How does the patch test relate to these conditions?

**(b)** [7 marks] For a 4-node bilinear quadrilateral (Q4) element used in 2D plane elasticity, discuss its completeness, compatibility, and the existence of spurious zero-energy modes. How can these modes be eliminated?

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Babuška–Aziz Conditions & Patch Test [7 marks]**

The **Babuška–Aziz conditions** are necessary and sufficient for convergence of the Galerkin FE method in elasticity:

**(i) Boundedness:** There exist constants $0 < C_1 \leq C_2 < \infty$ such that
$$
C_1 \Vert u_h \Vert_E \leq \Vert F \Vert \leq C_2 \Vert u_h \Vert_E, \quad \forall u_h \in V_h
$$
**(ii) Consistency (Asymptotic Compatibility):**
$$
\lim_{h \to 0} a(u - u_h, v_h) = 0, \quad \forall v_h \in V_h
$$
where $a(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the bilinear energy form. **[3 marks]**

**Patch Test Procedure (Irons, 1965):**
1. Construct a patch of $m$ elements with all possible boundary node configurations.
2. Impose nodal displacements corresponding to a **constant strain state** $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_0$.
3. Compute equivalent nodal forces using $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K} \mathbf{u}$.
4. Solve the system; verify that all computed displacements match prescribed ones *exactly* (within machine precision).
5. **Pass:** Element converges. **Fail:** Investigate the defect. **[3 marks]**

**Connection:** The patch test is a *discrete, numerical* version of Babuška–Aziz. A patch test pass implies boundedness + consistency for typical elements; failure indicates violation. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Q4 Quadrilateral — Completeness, Compatibility, Zero-Energy Modes [7 marks]**

The Q4 element has bilinear shape functions $N_i = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i)$ giving
$$
u^e(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 xy
$$

**Completeness:** Contains $1, x, y$ — reproduces rigid-body (3 modes) and constant-strain states. ✓ However, the polynomial is *incomplete quadratic*: the terms $x^2, y^2, xy^2, x^2y$ are missing. **[2 marks]**

**Compatibility:** Displacements match at the 4 corner nodes → C⁰ continuous at nodes only. Distortion of element sides means mid-side values of adjacent elements may *not* match → only *nodal* compatibility (a weak form of conformity). **[2 marks]**

**Spurious Zero-Energy Modes:** There exist displacement fields with **zero nodal values** but **non-zero strain**, e.g., the *hourglass mode*:
$$
u_{hg} = xy, \quad v_{hg} = -xy
$$
giving $\varepsilon_{xy} \neq 0$ but $\mathbf{u}_{hg} = 0$ at the 4 corners. This adds 2 zero-energy eigenvalues to $\mathbf{K}^e$. **[2 marks]**

**Elimination Methods:**
- **Enhanced Assumed Strain (EAS):** Add incompatible modes with enhanced strain interpolation.
- **Assumed Natural Strain (ANS):** Modify shear-strain sampling at element interior.
- **B-bar method:** Average volumetric strain to suppress volumetric locking.
- **Reduced Integration + Hourglass Control:** 1-point Gauss integration with artificial stiffness in hourglass directions (used in ABAQUS). **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Convergence Requirements**

- ☐ **Two conditions for convergence:** Completeness ✓ and Compatibility ✓
- ☐ **Completeness means:** polynomial expansion must include rigid-body + constant-strain terms
- ☐ **Compatibility level:** C⁰ for 2nd-order PDEs; C¹ for 4th-order PDEs (beams, plates)
- ☐ **Convergence rate:** $\| u - u_h \|_E = \mathcal{O}(h^p)$ in energy norm; $\mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$ in $L_2$ norm
- ☐ **$h$-refinement:** Reduces error algebraically; cheap but slow for singular problems
- ☐ **$p$-refinement:** Exponential convergence for analytic solutions; powerful for smooth fields
- ☐ **$hp$-refinement:** Best of both worlds — exponential convergence universally
- ☐ **Babuška–Aziz:** Boundedness + Consistency → mathematical guarantee of convergence
- ☐ **Patch Test (Irons):** Numerical sufficiency test — constant strain must be reproduced exactly
- ☐ **Q4 element:** Incomplete quadratic polynomial → spurious hourglass modes → requires EAS/ANS/b-bar
- ☐ **Beam element:** Requires Hermite cubics for C¹ continuity (continuous slope)
- ☐ **CST triangle:** Complete linear polynomial — passes both requirements
- ☐ **Non-conforming elements** (e.g., Wilson Q6, Q4 with incompatible modes): May converge but lose upper-bound property
- ☐ **Locking** = element too stiff (e.g., shear locking in thin beams using full integration)
- ☐ **Hourglassing** = element too compliant due to zero-energy modes
- ☐ **Rule of thumb:** A complete + compatible element **always** passes the patch test; converse is not necessarily true
- ☐ **Engineering implication:** Always refine the mesh *systematically* and check convergence via **error norm plots** before trusting results
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patch Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Patch Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patch Test (Irons-Razzaque, 1973):** A numerical consistency test applied to a finite element formulation to verify whether the element can exactly reproduce a state of **constant stress / constant strain** (i.e., a linear displacement field) within a patch of elements, when the boundary nodes of the patch are prescribed with displacements derived from a linear displacement field.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, the patch test is the operational verification of the **completeness condition** for an assumed displacement field. It is also widely used as a diagnostic tool for non-conforming elements (e.g., Discrete Kirchhoff Plate elements, Morley triangle) and for reduced-integration elements, where analytical proofs of convergence are difficult.

The patch test is **necessary** (but not always sufficient) for guaranteed convergence of the FE solution as the mesh is refined (the **h-convergence** limit).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **tailor stitching a patch of fabric** onto a garment. The patch consists of several irregularly shaped pieces of cloth (the distorted finite elements). To test whether your stitching technique is correct, you pull the patch in a perfectly straight, uniform way (a linear displacement field). 

If the patch stretches uniformly without wrinkles, distortions, or internal stresses building up at the seams, then your stitching (the element formulation) is **correct**. If wrinkles appear or seams pull, your stitching fails the test.

In Finite Element Analysis:
- **The patch** = A small cluster of distorted elements.
- **The straight pull** = A prescribed linear displacement field on the boundary.
- **The wrinkles (internal forces)** = Non-zero internal nodal forces, which indicate the element is NOT reproducing constant strain.
- **Smooth, wrinkle-free patch** = The element passes the patch test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Patch" and not just "Element"?**
> A single element test cannot detect **inter-element compatibility** problems. The patch test, by using multiple elements, can identify spurious energy modes that appear only when elements are joined together — a critical check for non-conforming elements.

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Constant Strain State** | A displacement field whose spatial derivatives (strains) are uniform across the domain. |
| **Completeness** | The ability of an element to represent constant strain states and rigid body modes exactly. |
| **Convergence** | The FE solution approaches the exact solution as the mesh is refined. |
| **Inf-Sup Condition** | The Babuška-Brezii stability condition for mixed formulations. |
| **Non-Conforming Element** | An element that violates C⁰ (or C¹) continuity at element interfaces. |
| **Reduced Integration** | Using a lower-order Gauss quadrature than the theoretical minimum, often used to relieve locking. |

The **standard patch test displacement field** used in 2D is:
$$u(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y$$
$$v(x,y) = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y$$

This corresponds to a constant strain state:
$$\varepsilon_{xx} = a_1, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = b_2, \quad \gamma_{xy} = a_2 + b_1$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The patch test, in its operational form, follows a strict logical sequence:

**Step 1 — Patch Construction**
Select a small assembly (typically **2 to 4 elements**) of arbitrarily distorted elements. The patch should be topologically simple (a quadrilateral, triangle, or tetrahedron cluster) and may include elements of different shapes.

**Step 2 — Boundary Displacement Prescription**
On the **external boundary nodes** of the patch, prescribe nodal displacements that are interpolated from a *linear polynomial* field. Mathematically, for 2D:
$$u_i = a_0 + a_1 x_i + a_2 y_i$$
$$v_i = b_0 + b_1 x_i + b_2 y_i$$
where $(x_i, y_i)$ are the coordinates of the boundary node $i$, and $(a_0, a_1, a_2, b_0, b_1, b_2)$ are **arbitrary constants** chosen by the examiner.

**Step 3 — Internal Node Solution**
Solve for the displacements of the **internal nodes** of the patch using the assembled global stiffness equation:
$$[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a true patch test, no external forces are applied to the internal nodes. So $\{F_{int}\} = \{0\}$. The internal nodal forces are computed *after* the solution as a residual check.

**Step 4 — Residual / Internal Force Check**
At every internal node, compute the **internal force vector**:
$$\{F_{int}\}_i = \sum_{e} \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \{\sigma_e\} \, dV$$
where the sum is over all elements meeting at node $i$. For a patch that **passes** the test:
$$\{F_{int}\}_i = \{0\} \quad \forall \text{ internal nodes } i$$

**Step 5 — Constant Strain Recovery**
The strain computed from the FE solution within every element should be:
$$\varepsilon_e = \text{constant}, \quad \text{and equal to the analytical } \nabla \mathbf{u}$$

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Test

The patch test exploits the following logical chain:

- If an element can exactly represent a constant strain state, then by the **Theorem of Minimum Potential Energy**, the FE solution will converge to the exact solution (as long as compatibility is maintained).
- The constant strain state is the *first non-trivial polynomial* in the Taylor series expansion of any smooth displacement field.
- Hence, reproducing the constant strain state is the **bare-minimum** requirement for convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The patch test is a *numerical* realization of the **completeness condition**. It is more powerful than the analytical completeness check because it can detect errors arising from numerical integration, distorted element shapes, and inter-element continuity violations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Units / Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear 2D displacement field | $u = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y$, $v = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y$ | Constants $a_i, b_i$ are arbitrary |
| 2 | Constant strain in 2D | $\varepsilon_{xx} = a_1$, $\varepsilon_{yy} = b_2$, $\gamma_{xy} = a_2 + b_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | 3D Linear displacement | $u_i = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 z$ | $i = 1,2,3$ |
| 4 | Patch test equilibrium | $\sum_e \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \sigma_e \, dV = \{0\}$ at internal nodes | Force units: N |
| 5 | Stress from constant strain | $\sigma = [D]\{\varepsilon\} = \text{constant}$ | $\text{N}/\text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | Reduced patch test | Patch of 2 elements with 1 internal node | Equivalent to full patch for many cases |
| 7 | Membrane patch test | Applied to in-plane deformation only | 2D plane stress / plane strain |
| 8 | Plate bending patch test | Constant curvature / twist states | Requires $C^1$ or special treatment |
| 9 | Rigorous patch test | 4 elements, 4 internal nodes, fully distorted | Standard form by Irons |
| 10 | Inf-Sup condition | $\inf_q \sup_v \dfrac{B(q,v)}{\vert \vert v \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert q \vert \vert} \geq \beta > 0$ | For mixed formulations |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, all absolute value and norm symbols are written with `\vert` to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry:** Verifying shell element formulations for aircraft wing structures (where distorted elements are common).
- **Civil Engineering:** Checking non-conforming plate elements for bridge deck analysis.
- **Automotive Crash Analysis:** Confirming reduced-integration elements (which prevent shear locking) still converge reliably.
- **Mesh Quality Assessment:** The patch test exposes **element distortion sensitivity** — a critical metric for adaptive meshing algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Internal Force Residual for a 2-Element Patch

Consider a **2-element patch** consisting of two 4-node quadrilateral elements, sharing one common internal node $P$ (the *test point*). The external boundary nodes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 are prescribed with a linear displacement field.

**Given:** Linear displacement field
$$u(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y$$
$$v(x,y) = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y$$

**Step A — Compute Constant Strain Tensor**
The strain-displacement relationship $\varepsilon = [B]\{d\}$ for 2D gives:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = a_1$$
$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = b_2$$
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = a_2 + b_1$$

This is the **analytical strain**, denoted $\{\varepsilon_{exact}\}$.

**Step B — Compute Constant Stress**
Using the constitutive matrix $[D]$:
$$\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon_{exact}\}$$

For plane stress (isotropic material, Young's modulus $E$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$):
$$\{\sigma\} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}
\begin{Bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{Bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} a_1 \\ b_2 \\ a_2 + b_1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

This stress is **spatially uniform** over the entire patch.

**Step C — Compute Internal Force at Internal Node P**
The internal force at the internal node $P$ is the sum of contributions from both elements:

$$\{F_{int}\}_P = \sum_{e=1}^{2} \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \{\sigma\} \, dV$$

Since $\{\sigma\}$ is constant in space, this reduces to:
$$\{F_{int}\}_P = \left( \sum_{e=1}^{2} \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \, dV \right) \{\sigma\}$$

**Step D — Patch Test Pass Condition**
For the patch to pass:
$$\{F_{int}\}_P = \{0\}$$

This means the element shape function integrals must satisfy:
$$\sum_{e=1}^{2} \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \, dV = [0]$$

This is the **fundamental algebraic condition** that the patch test verifies. If the element's $[B]$ matrix does not satisfy this property, spurious internal forces will develop, and the element fails the patch test.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Patch Test Criteria for Common Elements

| **Element Type** | **No. of Nodes** | **Passes Irons Patch Test?** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CST (Constant Strain Triangle) | 3 | ✓ Yes | Linear shape functions; complete to order 1 |
| LST (Linear Strain Triangle) | 6 | ✓ Yes | Quadratic + bubble function; complete to order 2 |
| Bilinear Quad (Q4) | 4 | ✓ Yes (with $2 \times 2$ Gauss) | Serendipity; complete to order 1 |
| Quadratic Quad (Q8) | 8 | ✓ Yes | Complete to order 2 |
| Incompatible Quad (Q6) | 6 | ✓ Yes (passes with caveats) | Adds internal modes for bending |
| Discrete Kirchhoff Triangle (DKT) | 3 | ✓ Yes | Non-conforming but convergent |
| Morley Triangle | 3 (plate) | ✓ Yes (patch) | Non-conforming; passes via patch test |
| Bilinear Quad with $1 \times 1$ Gauss | 4 | ✗ No (hourglass mode) | Under-integrated; spurious zero-energy mode |
| Membrane Quad (reduced 1-pt) | 4 | ✗ Fails | Hourglass mode not suppressed |

### 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence of Patch Test and Completeness

The completeness condition requires that the assumed displacement field contains:
1. All **rigid body modes** (3 in 2D: $u = 1$, $v = 1$, $\omega = -y\hat{i} + x\hat{j}$)
2. All **constant strain modes** (3 in 2D: $\varepsilon_{xx} = 1$, $\varepsilon_{yy} = 1$, $\gamma_{xy} = 1$)

Mathematically, the shape function matrix $[N]$ must be such that:
$$[B] \{c\} = [\varepsilon]$$
can be reproduced for any constant $[\varepsilon]$, where $\{c\}$ are the corresponding nodal coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Result:** A finite element passes the patch test **IF AND ONLY IF** its shape function expansion is *complete to the order required by the underlying PDE*. For 2D linear elasticity (second-order PDE), completeness to order 1 is sufficient.

### 3.4 Mathematical Statement of the Convergence Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Babuška, 1971; Azis, 1972):** A family of finite element solutions $\{u_h\}$ converges to the exact solution $u$ as $h \to 0$ if:
> 1. The element is **complete** (passes patch test), AND
> 2. The element is **compatible** (displacement continuity is maintained), AND
> 3. The element stiffness matrix $[K_e]$ is **invariant** under rigid body translations.

The patch test is a numerical surrogate for condition (1), and is therefore the *operational gateway* to convergence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Patch Test Procedure Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Element Formulation] --> B[Construct Patch of 2-4 Distorted Elements]
    B --> C{Identify Boundary vs Internal Nodes}
    C -- Boundary Nodes --> D[Prescribe Linear Displacement Field u = a0 + a1x + a2y]
    C -- Internal Nodes --> E[Keep Free, No External Load]
    D --> F[Assemble Global Stiffness K_global]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solve K d = F for Internal Nodal Displacements]
    G --> H[Compute Element Strains using B Matrix]
    H --> I{Strains Constant and Equal to Analytical?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Compute Internal Forces at Internal Nodes]
    I -- No --> K[FAIL: Patch Test Failed]
    J --> L{All Internal Forces Equal to Zero?}
    L -- Yes --> M[PASS: Element is Convergent]
    L -- No --> K
    M --> N[End: Element Approved for Use]
    K --> O[End: Reformulate or Refine Element]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style M fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style K fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style N fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style O fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Rigorous Irons Patch Test Geometry (2D Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph patch1 [Rigorous Irons Patch Test - 4 Distorted Quadrilaterals]
        direction TB
        node1((1)) --- node2((2))
        node2((2)) --- node3((3))
        node3((3)) --- node4((4))
        node4((4)) --- node1((1))
        node1((1)) --- nodeC((C))
        node2((2)) --- nodeC((C))
        node3((3)) --- nodeC((C))
        node4((4)) --- nodeC((C))
    end
    subgraph extBoundary [External Boundary - Prescribed Displacements]
        b1((1)) -. linear field u=a0+a1x+a2y .-> b2((2))
        b2((2)) -. linear field .-> b3((3))
        b3((3)) -. linear field .-> b4((4))
        b4((4)) -. linear field .-> b1((1))
    end
    style nodeC fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style node1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style node2 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style node3 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style node4 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagrams above, **node C** (orange) represents the single internal node where the patch test verifies zero internal force. The four **boundary nodes** (purple, labeled 1-4) are prescribed with the linear displacement field. This is the standard 4-element quadrilateral patch test configuration used by Irons and Razzaque.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Patch Test Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input: Element Formulation + Distorted Mesh] --> parser[Parser Module]
    parser --> intEngine[Numerical Integration Engine]
    intEngine --> strainComp[Strain Computation Module]
    strainComp --> stressComp[Stress Recovery Module]
    stressComp --> intForce[Internal Force Assembler]
    intForce --> checker{Residual Checker}
    checker -- Residuals = 0 --> pass[PASS Certificate]
    checker -- Residuals non-zero --> fail[FAIL Certificate]
    fail --> diag[Diagnostic Report: Element Mode Violation]
    diag --> reform[Reformulation Required]
    pass --> archive[Archive Validated Formulation]
    style pass fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style fail fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style archive fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style reform fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Question A1 — 3 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Define the patch test in Finite Element Analysis. Who proposed it, and what is its primary purpose?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The patch test is a numerical consistency test proposed by **Bruce Irons and A. Razzaque (1973)** to verify whether a finite element formulation can exactly reproduce a state of **constant stress / constant strain** within a patch of elements when boundary nodes are prescribed with a linear displacement field.
- **Purpose (1 Mark):** It is used to test the **convergence** of an element formulation, especially for non-conforming or reduced-integration elements where analytical proofs are difficult.
- **Operational Form (1 Mark):** The element passes the test if **all internal nodal forces vanish** when the patch is subjected to a linear boundary displacement field.

---

> **[Question A2 — 3 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *State the mathematical form of the displacement field used in the standard 2D patch test. What are the corresponding constant strain components?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**
- **Displacement Field (2 Marks):** For 2D problems, the patch test uses:
$$u(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y$$
$$v(x,y) = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y$$
where $a_0, a_1, a_2, b_0, b_1, b_2$ are **arbitrary constants**.

- **Constant Strains (1 Mark):**
$$\varepsilon_{xx} = a_1, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = b_2, \quad \gamma_{xy} = a_2 + b_1$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[Question B-A — 14 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Describe in detail the Bruce Irons patch test procedure. Apply the patch test to a 2-element quadrilateral patch and derive the algebraic condition for the element to pass the test.*

#### Part (a) — Patch Test Procedure [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Construct a patch consisting of 2 or more arbitrarily distorted elements. The patch must include at least one internal node where residual forces can be checked.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Identify external (boundary) and internal nodes. The boundary nodes are loaded kinematically (prescribed displacements), while internal nodes remain free of external force.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply a linear displacement field on the boundary:
$$u(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y$$
$$v(x,y) = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Solve the global FE equation $K d = F$ for the internal nodal displacements. Since the boundary is fully prescribed, the internal node problem is well-posed.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute the **element strains** $\{\varepsilon_e\} = [B_e]\{d_e\}$ for every element. Check whether they are **constant** and equal to the analytical constant strain.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Compute the **internal force vector** at the internal node:
$$\{F_{int}\}_P = \sum_e \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \{\sigma_e\} dV$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** The element **passes** the patch test if and only if:
- All element strains are **constant and equal** to the analytical strain.
- All internal nodal forces are **identically zero**.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Pass Condition for 2-Element Patch [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The analytical constant strain is:
$$\{\varepsilon_{exact}\} = \{a_1, b_2, a_2 + b_1\}^T$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** The corresponding constant stress is:
$$\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon_{exact}\}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The internal force at the single internal node P (where both elements meet) is:
$$\{F_{int}\}_P = \{F_{int}\}_P^{(1)} + \{F_{int}\}_P^{(2)}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Each element contributes:
$$\{F_{int}\}_P^{(e)} = \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T \{\sigma\} dV = \left( \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T dV \right) \{\sigma\}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Summing both elements:
$$\{F_{int}\}_P = \left( \int_{V_1} [B_1]^T dV + \int_{V_2} [B_2]^T dV \right) \{\sigma\}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** The **patch test pass condition** is:
$$\int_{V_1} [B_1]^T dV + \int_{V_2} [B_2]^T dV = [0]$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** This algebraic condition is equivalent to the analytical **completeness condition**, ensuring that the shape functions can reproduce all constant strain modes. Hence, the element is convergent.

---

> **[Question B-B — 14 Marks]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Discuss the different types of patch tests used in FEA. Explain the importance of the patch test for non-conforming elements with suitable examples.*

#### Part (a) — Types of Patch Tests [7 Marks]

**1. Bruce Irons Rigorous Patch Test (2 Marks):**
- Patch of **4 distorted quadrilateral elements** with **4 internal nodes**.
- Most general form; tests all constant strain modes and inter-element compatibility.
- Standard benchmark in commercial FE codes.

**2. Reduced Patch Test (1.5 Marks):**
- Patch of **2 elements** with **1 internal node**.
- Equivalent to the rigorous test for elements that are linearly complete.
- Commonly used in research publications for computational efficiency.

**3. Membrane Patch Test (1.5 Marks):**
- Specialized for **plane stress / plane strain** problems.
- Tests in-plane stretching and shearing modes.

**4. Plate Bending Patch Test (1 Mark):**
- Applied to **Kirchhoff / Mindlin plate** elements.
- Tests constant curvature and constant twist states.
- Critical for **C¹ non-conforming** elements like Morley triangle.

**5. 3D Patch Test (1 Mark):**
- Generalization to **solid continuum** elements.
- 3D linear displacement field: $u_i = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 z$.

#### Part (b) — Patch Test for Non-Conforming Elements [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Non-conforming elements violate the **compatibility condition** — they allow displacement discontinuities at element interfaces. Examples: **Discrete Kirchhoff Triangle (DKT)**, **Morley triangle**, **Hermite beam elements** with $C^0$ continuity.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** For non-conforming elements, the analytical convergence proof fails (since compatibility is broken). The patch test becomes the **only practical verification** of convergence.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** **Example — DKT Plate Element:** The DKT element uses a non-conforming shape function for rotation. Analytically, it does not satisfy $C^1$ continuity, but it **passes the patch test** for constant curvature states. This guarantees convergence for thin plates.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** **Example — Morley Triangle:** A 3-node plate element with continuous normal rotation but discontinuous transverse displacement. The Morley element fails the strict $C^1$ condition but **passes the patch test**, hence is convergent.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** **Counter-example — Reduced Integration Q4:** The Q4 element with **1-point Gauss integration** (to relieve shear locking) has an **hourglass mode** that the patch test detects. The internal forces are non-zero for the hourglass deformation, hence the element **fails the patch test** and is non-convergent without hourglass control.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** **Engineering Implication:** The patch test allows engineers to **validate non-conforming elements** that are computationally efficient (e.g., DKT is much faster than fully conforming MITC elements) without requiring full theoretical convergence proofs.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** The patch test is a **necessary and practical** condition for convergence in such cases, bridging the gap between analytical convergence theory and computational implementation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the patch test with the "completeness test":** The patch test is a *numerical realization* of the completeness condition, but it is not identical. Some students write that "patch test = completeness" — this is only partially true and loses 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the rigid body mode check:** The patch test also requires that the element reproduce **rigid body translations and rotations** exactly. Students often state only the constant strain condition, losing 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mismeaning "passes the patch test":** Many students say "the element is exact" — this is wrong. The patch test only guarantees convergence; the element still approximates higher-order displacement fields.
>
> 4. **Wrong formula in the table:** The constant strain formula $\varepsilon_{xy} = a_2 + b_1$ is frequently written as $a_2$ or $b_1$ alone. Always write the **engineering shear strain** form: $\gamma_{xy} = a_2 + b_1$ (or $\varepsilon_{xy} = \tfrac{1}{2}(a_2 + b_1)$ if using tensorial shear strain).
>
> 5. **Missing the equation for 3D:** KTU 2024 often asks the 3D form. Memorize: $u_i = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 z$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Patch Test (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Proposers:** Bruce Irons and A. Razzaque, **1973**.
- ☐ **Definition:** A numerical test to verify if an element can reproduce a **constant strain state** exactly in a patch of distorted elements.
- ☐ **Standard 2D Linear Displacement Field:**
  $u = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y$, $v = b_0 + b_1 x + b_2 y$.
- ☐ **Constant Strains:** $\varepsilon_{xx} = a_1$, $\varepsilon_{yy} = b_2$, $\gamma_{xy} = a_2 + b_1$.
- ☐ **3D Form:** $u_i = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 z$ (for $i = 1, 2, 3$).
- ☐ **Pass Criterion:** All **internal nodal forces = 0** AND all **element strains = constant** (equal to analytical).
- ☐ **Types:** Irons Rigorous, Reduced, Membrane, Plate Bending, 3D.
- ☐ **Patch Test Pass = Necessary for convergence** (but not sufficient by itself — compatibility also required).
- ☐ **Key Formula for Pass Condition:**
  $\sum_e \int_{V_e} [B_e]^T dV = [0]$.
- ☐ **Elements That Pass:** CST, LST, Q4 (full integration), Q8, Q9, DKT, MITC.
- ☐ **Elements That Fail:** Q4 with 1-point Gauss (hourglass), membrane quad with reduced integration (no hourglass control).
- ☐ **For Non-Conforming Elements:** Patch test is the **only practical convergence verification**.
- ☐ **Babuška-Brezii Condition:** Related stability criterion for **mixed formulations** (used in nearly incompressible and plate bending analysis).
- ☐ **Key Theorem:** Patch test equivalence to completeness — patch test is the **operational form** of the completeness requirement.
- ☐ **Engineering Use:** Validates new elements, checks hourglass modes, verifies reduced-integration schemes.
- ☐ **Examiner Tip:** Always write the displacement field in 2 forms — both $u$ and $v$ — to score full marks on KTU 2024 board exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variational Methods: Functionals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variational Methods: Functionals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Functional

In the calculus of variations — the mathematical backbone of the Finite Element Method (FEM) — a **functional** $I[y(x)]$ is an operator that maps an entire admissible function $y(x)$ (drawn from a function space) to a single real scalar value.

Formally, if $\mathcal{Y}$ denotes a function space and $\mathbb{R}$ denotes the real number line, then:

$$
I : \mathcal{Y} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}
$$

That is, the input to $I$ is **not a number**, but an **entire function** $y(x)$, while the output is an ordinary number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Variational methods in FEM convert differential equation boundary value problems into the task of finding a function that *minimizes (or extremizes) a functional*. This is the **Rayleigh-Ritz** and **Galerkin** philosophy of structural analysis.

A canonical example is the area under a curve between limits $x_1$ and $x_2$:

$$
I[y(x)] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F\!\left(x,\, y(x),\, y'(x)\right) \, dx
$$

Here, $y(x)$ is the unknown function; $F$ is a known scalar function (the **integrand** or **Lagrangian** of the functional).

### 1.2 Function vs. Functional — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Ordinary Function $f(x)$ | Functional $I[y(x)]$ |
|---|---|---|
| Input | A real number $x$ | An entire function $y(x)$ |
| Output | A real number | A real number |
| Domain | $\mathbb{R}$ (or subset) | A function space $\mathcal{Y}$ |
| Variability | Single variable | Infinite degrees of freedom |

### 1.3 Physical and Engineering Examples of Functionals

- **Total Potential Energy** of a deformed elastic body: $\Pi = U - W$.
- **Arc Length** of a plane curve $y(x)$ between $x_1$ and $x_2$.
- **Travel Time** of a light ray (Fermat's Principle): $T = \int \frac{ds}{v}$.
- **Total Mass** of a non-uniform rod whose density varies with $y(x)$.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Function of a Function"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a sausage machine. You do not feed it a single grain of meat — you feed it an *entire sausage-shaped function* (the curve of meat along the grinder). The machine grinds it down and outputs **one single number** — the total weight of meat processed. Similarly, a functional takes an entire curve as input and outputs one number (total potential energy, total arc length, etc.).

### 1.5 Why Functionals Matter in FEM

The **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy (PMPE)** states that a conservative elastic system in stable equilibrium assumes a configuration that minimizes the total potential energy functional. The displacement field $u(x)$ that minimizes $\Pi$ also satisfies the governing differential equations of elasticity plus the natural boundary conditions. This equivalence is the bridge between variational methods and FEM.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Functional

A typical first-order functional in one independent variable has the structure:

$$
I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F\!\left(x,\, y,\, y'\right) \, dx
$$

where:
- $x$ — independent variable (spatial coordinate, time, etc.).
- $y = y(x)$ — dependent function (displacement, temperature, etc.).
- $y' = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ — first derivative.
- $F$ — scalar **integrand** or **Lagrangian density**, assumed to be at least twice continuously differentiable ($C^2$).

### 2.2 The Variation of a Functional (First Variation)

Let $\bar{y}(x) = y(x) + \varepsilon\,\eta(x)$ be a *varied* or *neighbouring* function, where:
- $\varepsilon$ — small scalar parameter.
- $\eta(x)$ — arbitrary smooth (admissible) variation function.
- $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$ — if $y$ is prescribed at the endpoints (essential boundary conditions).

The **incremental change** in the functional is:

$$
\Delta I = I[y + \varepsilon\eta] - I[y]
$$

The **first variation** $\delta I$ is the linear (Gateaux) derivative of $I$ with respect to $\varepsilon$ evaluated at $\varepsilon = 0$:

$$
\delta I = \left. \frac{d I}{d \varepsilon} \right|_{\varepsilon = 0} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left( \frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\,\eta + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\,\eta' \right) dx
$$

### 2.3 Stationary Condition (Necessary Condition for Extremum)

A necessary condition for $I[y]$ to attain an extremum (minimum, maximum, or saddle) is that the first variation vanishes for all admissible $\eta(x)$:

$$
\delta I = 0
$$

### 2.4 Euler-Lagrange Equation (The Heart of Variational Calculus)

Applying integration by parts to the $\eta'$ term and invoking the Fundamental Lemma of Calculus of Variations ($\eta$ being arbitrary), we obtain the celebrated **Euler-Lagrange Equation**:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) - \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any function $y(x)$ that extremizes $I[y]$ must satisfy this second-order ODE. Solving it — together with boundary conditions — yields the extremal function.

### 2.5 Generalizations of the Euler-Lagrange Equation

**Case 1: Functionals with higher-order derivatives**

$$
I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F\!\left(x,\, y,\, y',\, y'',\, \ldots,\, y^{(n)}\right) dx
$$

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) + \frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\right) - \cdots + (-1)^{n}\frac{d^{n}}{dx^{n}}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y^{(n)}}\right) = 0
$$

**Case 2: Functionals of several independent functions $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m$**

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y_k} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y_k'}\right) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

**Case 3: Functionals of several independent variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$**

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial (\partial y / \partial x_i)}\right) = 0
$$

### 2.6 Natural Boundary Conditions

When $\eta$ is **not** forced to vanish at an endpoint, integration by parts yields the **natural boundary condition**:

$$
\left. \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'} \right|_{x=x_1} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \left. \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'} \right|_{x=x_2} = 0
$$

This represents a free boundary — for example, zero shear force at a free end of a beam.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Physical / Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General functional | $I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x, y, y')\,dx$ | Integral of a Lagrangian density |
| 2 | First variation | $\delta I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}\eta + \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\eta'\right)dx$ | Linear change in $I$ under small $\eta$ |
| 3 | Stationarity condition | $\delta I = 0$ | Extremum condition |
| 4 | Euler-Lagrange equation | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) - \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0$ | Governing ODE for extremal $y(x)$ |
| 5 | Higher-order EL (up to $n$) | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}\dfrac{d^{k}}{dx^{k}}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y^{(k)}}\right) = 0$ | For $F$ depending on $y^{(n)}$ |
| 6 | Multi-function EL | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y_k'}\right) - \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y_k} = 0$ | For $k = 1, \ldots, m$ |
| 7 | Natural BC | $\left.\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right|_{x=x_1} = 0$ | Free-end / natural BC |
| 8 | Varied function | $\bar{y} = y + \varepsilon\eta$ | Neighbouring trial function |
| 9 | Total potential energy (1D bar) | $\Pi[u] = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\int_{0}^{L}\! EA(u')^{2}\,dx - \int_{0}^{L}\! f\cdot u\,dx$ | Strain energy minus work of external forces |
| 10 | Strain energy (Euler-Bernoulli beam) | $U = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\int_{0}^{L}\! EI(v'')^{2}\,dx$ | Bending strain energy |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Mechanics:** Deriving stiffness matrices in FEM by minimizing total potential energy.
- **Aerospace:** Finding minimum-drag airfoil profiles (Brachistochrone problem).
- **Optical Engineering:** Fermat's principle yields Snell's law from a travel-time functional.
- **Control Theory:** Hamilton's principle for dynamic systems.
- **Geophysics:** Finding geodesics on curved surfaces — used in GPS coordinate transformations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Euler-Lagrange Equation (Detailed)

**Objective:** Starting from $\delta I = 0$, derive the Euler-Lagrange equation.

**Step 1 — Construct the varied (neighbouring) function.**

Consider an admissible function $y(x)$ and its variation:

$$
\bar{y}(x, \varepsilon) = y(x) + \varepsilon\,\eta(x)
$$

where $\eta(x)$ is a smooth, arbitrary function satisfying $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$ for fixed-endpoint problems. Differentiating:

$$
\frac{d\bar{y}}{dx} = y'(x) + \varepsilon\,\eta'(x)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the functional.**

$$
I(\varepsilon) = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F\!\left(x,\; y + \varepsilon\eta,\; y' + \varepsilon\eta'\right) dx
$$

At $\varepsilon = 0$, $I(0) = I[y]$, the original functional value.

**Step 3 — Differentiate $I(\varepsilon)$ with respect to $\varepsilon$.**

By Leibniz's rule for differentiation under the integral sign:

$$
\frac{d I}{d \varepsilon} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial \bar{y}}\,\frac{\partial \bar{y}}{\partial \varepsilon} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial \bar{y}'}\,\frac{\partial \bar{y}'}{\partial \varepsilon} \right] dx
$$

Since $\partial \bar{y}/\partial \varepsilon = \eta$ and $\partial \bar{y}'/\partial \varepsilon = \eta'$:

$$
\frac{d I}{d \varepsilon} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left( \frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\,\eta + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\,\eta' \right) dx
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $\varepsilon = 0$ to obtain the first variation.**

$$
\delta I = \left.\frac{dI}{d\varepsilon}\right|_{\varepsilon = 0} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left( \frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\,\eta + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\,\eta' \right) dx
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate the second term by parts.**

Apply integration by parts to the $\eta'$ term:

$$
\int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\,\eta' \, dx = \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\,\eta \right]_{x_1}^{x_2} - \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) \eta \, dx
$$

Because $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes identically.

**Step 6 — Combine terms.**

$$
\delta I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) \right] \eta(x) \, dx = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Fundamental Lemma of Calculus of Variations.**

Since $\eta(x)$ is an arbitrary function, the bracketed expression must vanish everywhere in $[x_1, x_2]$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) = 0\;}
$$

This is the **Euler-Lagrange equation**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Shortest Path Between Two Points

**Problem:** Show that the straight line minimizes the arc-length functional.

The arc length of $y(x)$ from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ is:

$$
I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}}\, dx
$$

Here $F(x, y, y') = \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}}$.

**Step 1 — Compute partial derivatives.**

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0 \qquad \text{(no explicit } y \text{ dependence)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'} = \frac{y'}{\sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Euler-Lagrange equation.**

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{y'}{\sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}}}\right) - 0 = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{y'}{\sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}}} = C \quad \text{(a constant)}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $y'$.**

Let $y' = C\sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}}$. Squaring and rearranging:

$$
(y')^{2} = C^{2}\bigl(1 + (y')^{2}\bigr) \quad\Rightarrow\quad (y')^{2}(1 - C^{2}) = C^{2}
$$

$$
y' = \frac{C}{\sqrt{1 - C^{2}}} = \text{constant} = m
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate.**

$$
y(x) = mx + b
$$

This is a straight line — confirming geometric intuition. The constants $m$ and $b$ are fixed by the two endpoints.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Euler-Lagrange for a Taut String Functional

**Problem:** A string under tension $T$ carries a distributed transverse load $f(x)$. The total potential energy is:

$$
\Pi[u] = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} T (u')^{2}\, dx - \int_{0}^{L} f(x)\, u\, dx
$$

Derive the governing differential equation using the Euler-Lagrange equation.

**Step 1 — Identify the integrand.**

$$
F(x, u, u') = \frac{1}{2} T (u')^{2} - f(x)\, u
$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives.**

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial u} = -f(x)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial u'} = T u'
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler-Lagrange.**

$$
\frac{d}{dx}(T u') - (-f) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad T u'' + f = 0
$$

This is the classical **equilibrium equation of a taut string** under transverse load. ✓

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Natural Boundary Condition

For a free-end problem where $\eta(x_2) \neq 0$ but $\eta(x_1) = 0$, retaining the boundary term from Step 5 above:

$$
\delta I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) \right] \eta \, dx + \left[ \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\,\eta \right]_{x_1}^{x_2} = 0
$$

The second term simplifies (since $\eta(x_1)=0$):

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\bigg|_{x=x_2}\,\eta(x_2) = 0
$$

Since $\eta(x_2)$ is arbitrary (the boundary is free), we require:

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\bigg|_{x=x_2} = 0
$$

This is the **natural boundary condition** — automatically satisfied when the functional is stationary.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Hierarchy Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy from a function to the Euler-Lagrange equation, mirroring the logical flow of variational calculus as applied in FEM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["ORDINARY FUNCTION f of x"] --> nodeB["Input is a single real number"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Output is a single real number"]
    nodeD["FUNCTIONAL I of y of x"] --> nodeE["Input is an entire function y of x"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Output is a single real number"]
    nodeG["VARYING THE FUNCTION"] --> nodeH["Construct varied function y bar = y plus epsilon eta"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Compute first variation delta I"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Impose stationarity delta I equals zero"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Integrate second term by parts"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Apply Fundamental Lemma"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["EULER LAGRANGE EQUATION"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Governing ODE for extremal y of x"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Natural boundary condition"]
    nodeM --> nodeP["Bridge to FEM Rayleigh Ritz formulation"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Stiffness matrix assembly"]
```

### 4.2 Variational Method Block Architecture

This diagram illustrates how a variational principle is converted into a discretized finite element problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Strong Form BVP Differential Equation plus BCs"] --> block1
    block1["Identify Functional I of y via Weak Formulation"] --> block2
    block2["Discretize Domain into Elements"] --> block3
    block3["Assume Trial Solution y of x equals N Transpose d"] --> block4
    block4["Substitute into Functional I"] --> block5
    block5["Compute Partial Derivative Partial I over Partial d equals zero"] --> block6
    block6["Assemble Global System K d equals F"] --> block7
    block7["Solve for Nodal Displacements d"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Components Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["FUNCTIONAL I of y equals Integral from x1 to x2 of F dx"] --> node2["INTEGRAND F is C2 in all arguments"]
    node2 --> node3["Depends on x independent variable"]
    node2 --> node4["Depends on y dependent function"]
    node2 --> node5["Depends on y prime first derivative"]
    node1 --> node6["LIMITS x1 and x2 define domain of integration"]
    node1 --> node7["OUTPUT is a single real scalar"]
    node4 --> node8["EULER LAGRANGE EL yields extremal y"]
    node5 --> node8
    node3 --> node8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *functional*. Give one example from solid mechanics.

**Model Answer:**
A functional $I[y(x)]$ is a rule that assigns a unique real number to every admissible function $y(x)$ defined over a domain.

$$
I : \mathcal{Y} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}
$$

Unlike an ordinary function $f(x)$ whose input is a number, the input of a functional is an entire function. **Engineering example:** The total potential energy of an axially loaded bar,

$$
\Pi[u] = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} EA(u')^{2}\,dx - \int_{0}^{L} f u\,dx
$$

is a functional of the displacement field $u(x)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the Euler-Lagrange equation for the functional

$$
I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x, y, y')\,dx.
$$

Mention the physical meaning of the result.

**Model Answer:**
The Euler-Lagrange equation is:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) - \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0
$$

**Physical meaning:** Any function $y(x)$ that makes $I[y]$ stationary (extremum) must satisfy this second-order ordinary differential equation. In continuum mechanics, the same equation emerges from the principle of stationary potential energy — the displacement field that minimizes total potential energy automatically satisfies equilibrium and natural boundary conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Define a functional and explain the procedure for finding its stationary value. Show that the Euler-Lagrange equation for the functional

$$
I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x,\, y,\, y')\,dx
$$

is given by

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) - \frac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0. \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

**(b)** Using the Euler-Lagrange equation, derive the governing differential equation for the transverse equilibrium of an axially loaded column subjected to a distributed lateral load $q(x)$ per unit length, where the total potential energy functional is

$$
\Pi[v] = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} EI(v'')^{2}\,dx - \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} P(v')^{2}\,dx - \int_{0}^{L} q(x)\,v\,dx. \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

**Solution (a):**

A functional is a mapping from a space of admissible functions to the real numbers. To find its stationary value, an arbitrary small variation $\eta(x)$ is imposed on the trial function $y(x)$, the increment $\Delta I$ is expanded in powers of a small parameter $\varepsilon$, and the first-order term $\delta I$ is set to zero. This yields the Euler-Lagrange equation.

The detailed step-by-step derivation (already presented in Section 3.1) yields:

**Step 1:** Varied function — $\bar{y}(x, \varepsilon) = y(x) + \varepsilon\eta(x)$.

**Step 2:** $I(\varepsilon) = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x,\, y + \varepsilon\eta,\, y' + \varepsilon\eta')\,dx$.

**Step 3:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{dI}{d\varepsilon} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!\left[\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\eta + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\eta'\right]dx
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at $\varepsilon = 0$ for $\delta I$. Integration by parts on the $\eta'$ term, with $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$, eliminates the boundary contribution:

$$
\delta I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!\left[\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right)\right]\eta\,dx = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Fundamental Lemma $\Rightarrow$ Euler-Lagrange equation.

---

**Solution (b):**

Identify the integrand:

$$
F(x, v, v', v'') = \frac{1}{2}EI(v'')^{2} - \frac{1}{2}P(v')^{2} - q(x)v
$$

Compute partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial v} = -q(x), \quad \frac{\partial F}{\partial v'} = -P v', \quad \frac{\partial F}{\partial v''} = EI v''
$$

Since the integrand depends on $v''$, the generalized Euler-Lagrange equation (Case 1, $n = 2$) is:

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial v} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial v'}\right) + \frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial v''}\right) = 0
$$

Substituting:

$$
-q(x) - \frac{d}{dx}(-P v') + \frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}(EI v'') = 0
$$

For constant $EI$ and $P$:

$$
EI\,v^{(4)} + P\,v'' = q(x)
$$

This is the classical **beam-column equation under transverse load**, also known as the fourth-order differential equation governing lateral buckling of columns.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** Define the first variation $\delta I$ of a functional. Starting from $\delta I = 0$, derive the Euler-Lagrange equation for the functional

$$
I[y] = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x, y, y', y'')\,dx. \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

**(b)** A uniform elastic string of length $L$ is fixed at both ends and carries a distributed transverse load $w(x)$. The total potential energy is

$$
\Pi[u] = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} T(u')^{2}\,dx - \int_{0}^{L} w(x)\,u\,dx.
$$

Apply the Euler-Lagrange equation to derive the governing differential equation. State the essential and natural boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (a):**

The first variation is the linear change in the functional under an infinitesimal change $\eta(x)$ in the function $y(x)$:

$$
\delta I = \left.\frac{d}{d\varepsilon}\int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x, y + \varepsilon\eta, y' + \varepsilon\eta', y'' + \varepsilon\eta'')\,dx\right|_{\varepsilon = 0}
$$

Differentiating and using chain rule:

$$
\delta I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!\left[\frac{\partial F}{\partial y}\eta + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\eta' + \frac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\eta''\right]dx
$$

Integrating the $\eta'$ term once by parts and the $\eta''$ term twice by parts, with the assumption $\eta(x_1) = \eta(x_2) = 0$ and $\eta'(x_1) = \eta'(x_2) = 0$:

$$
\int_{x_1}^{x_2}\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\eta'\,dx = -\int_{x_1}^{x_2}\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right)\eta\,dx
$$

$$
\int_{x_1}^{x_2}\frac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\eta''\,dx = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\right)\eta\,dx
$$

Combining:

$$
\delta I = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!\left[\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) + \frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\right)\right]\eta\,dx = 0
$$

By the Fundamental Lemma:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) + \frac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\right) = 0\;}
$$

---

**Solution (b):**

Integrand: $F(x, u, u') = \tfrac{1}{2}T(u')^{2} - w(x)u$.

Partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial u} = -w(x), \qquad \frac{\partial F}{\partial u'} = T u'
$$

Euler-Lagrange equation:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}(T u') - (-w) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad T u'' + w(x) = 0
$$

For constant tension $T$:

$$
u''(x) = -\frac{w(x)}{T}
$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- **Essential (Geometric) BCs:** $u(0) = 0$ and $u(L) = 0$ — fixed at both ends.
- **Natural (Force) BCs:** From the boundary terms, $T\,u'(0) = 0$ and $T\,u'(L) = 0$, indicating zero transverse force at the supports (automatically satisfied if ends are truly fixed).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the natural boundary condition (–2 marks):** Many students derive the Euler-Lagrange equation but ignore the boundary term that arises from integration by parts. The boundary term vanishes only if $\eta$ (or $\eta'$) is zero at the endpoints — make this assumption **explicitly**.
> 2. **Sign error in Euler-Lagrange equation (–2 marks):** The equation is $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) - \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0$. Reversed sign = full mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing function with functional (–1 mark):** A functional takes a *function* as input, not a number. Always state this distinction explicitly.
> 4. **Mis-applying the Fundamental Lemma (–2 marks):** The lemma requires $\eta(x)$ to be *arbitrary*. If the student assumes any specific form for $\eta$, the deduction is invalid.
> 5. **Forgetting to fix $\varepsilon = 0$ (–1 mark):** The first variation is evaluated at $\varepsilon = 0$, not at general $\varepsilon$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **functional** maps a function $y(x)$ to a real number: $I[y] \in \mathbb{R}$.
- [x] The **first variation** is $\delta I = \int \left( \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}\eta + \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\eta' \right)dx$.
- [x] **Stationarity condition:** $\delta I = 0$ for all admissible $\eta(x)$.
- [x] **Euler-Lagrange equation (1st-order):** $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) - \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y} = 0$.
- [x] **Euler-Lagrange equation (2nd-order):** $\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y} - \dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right) + \dfrac{d^{2}}{dx^{2}}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y''}\right) = 0$.
- [x] **Multi-function EL:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y_k'}\right) - \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y_k} = 0$ for each $k$.
- [x] **Natural boundary condition:** $\left.\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}\right|_{x = \text{free end}} = 0$.
- [x] **Fundamental Lemma:** If $\int_a^b G(x)\eta(x)\,dx = 0$ for all $\eta$, then $G(x) \equiv 0$.
- [x] **Engineering link:** PMPE $\Leftrightarrow$ Euler-Lagrange $\Leftrightarrow$ FEM stiffness matrix via Rayleigh-Ritz.
- [x] **Energy functionals:** Strain energy = $\tfrac{1}{2}\int k(u')^2 dx$; work = $\int f u\,dx$.
- [x] **Beam equation:** $EI\,v^{(4)} = w(x)$ emerges naturally from EL applied to bending strain energy.
- [x] **String equation:** $Tu'' + w(x) = 0$ emerges from EL applied to membrane strain energy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weak and Strong Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weak and Strong Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strong Form — The Classical BVP Statement

The **Strong Form** of a Boundary Value Problem (BVP) is the *pointwise* mathematical statement of the governing differential equation (GDE) along with all its associated boundary and initial conditions. It is called "strong" because every solution **must** satisfy the differential equation *exactly* at every point in the domain $\Omega$ and on its boundary $\Gamma$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**  
> *The Strong Form is the original differential equation formulation of a physical problem (e.g., equilibrium, heat conduction, elasticity), stated in terms of field variables and their derivatives, together with the prescribed boundary conditions. It is the classical, pointwise (everywhere) statement of the BVP.*

### General 1-D Strong Form Template

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(u) &= f(x) \quad \text{in the domain } \Omega = (0, L) \\[4pt]
u(0) &= \bar{u}_0 \quad \text{(Essential / Dirichlet B.C.)} \\[4pt]
\mathcal{B}(u)\vert_{x=L} &= \bar{q}_L \quad \text{(Natural / Neumann B.C.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{L}$ = differential operator (e.g., $-\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{d}{dx}\right)$)
- $u(x)$ = primary unknown (field variable)
- $f(x)$ = source/forcing term
- $\bar{u}_0, \bar{q}_L$ = prescribed boundary values

---

## 1.2 Weak Form — The Variational Integral Statement

The **Weak Form** (also called the **Variational Form** or **Galerkin Form**) is obtained by:
1. Multiplying the strong form by an arbitrary **weight function** (or **test function**) $w(x)$.
2. Integrating the resulting product over the domain $\Omega$.
3. Applying **integration by parts** (Green's first identity) to *transfer* one derivative from $u$ onto $w$ — thereby *weakening* (reducing) the differentiability requirement on $u$.

> [!NOTE]
