$$d_3 = \frac{(27.333 \times 5000 - (-13.333) \times 0) \times 10^{6}}{D} = \frac{27.333 \times 5000 \times 10^{6}}{186.65 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$d_3 = \frac{136.665 \times 10^{9}}{186.65 \times 10^{12}} = 0.7321 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.732 \text{ mm}$$

**Reaction at Node 1:**

$$R_1 = 14 \times 10^{6}(0 - d_2) = 14 \times 10^{6} \times (-0.3571 \times 10^{-3}) = -5000 \text{ N}$$

(Negative indicates it acts in the −x direction; the wall pushes the bar to the left with 5000 N, balancing the rightward 5000 N applied load.) ✓

**Internal Forces:**

- Element 1: $N_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{L_1}(d_2 - d_1) = 14 \times 10^{6} \times 0.3571 \times 10^{-3} = 5000$ N (tension) ✓
- Element 2: $N_2 = \frac{A_2 E_2}{L_2}(d_3 - d_2) = 13.333 \times 10^{6} \times (0.7321 - 0.3571) \times 10^{-3} = 5000$ N (tension) ✓

$$\boxed{d_1 = 0, \; d_2 = 0.357 \text{ mm}, \; d_3 = 0.732 \text{ mm}, \; R_1 = -5000 \text{ N}, \; N_1 = N_2 = 5000 \text{ N}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign Errors in Reaction Force:** Forgetting that the reaction equals $-[k_{11}]d_1 - [k_{12}]d_2$ (or the equivalent from row reduction) — 1 to 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Always include units (N, m, mm, Pa). KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Confusing Element vs Global DOFs:** In assembly questions, students often write the wrong element stiffness formula. Memorize: $k^{(e)} = (AE/L)\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
> 4. **Skipping the "δΠ = 0" Justification:** Even in numerical questions, write the stationarity statement before plugging into the matrix — KTU awards 1 mark for the conceptual step.
> 5. **Not Verifying Equilibrium:** Sum of reactions should equal negative of applied loads. KTU examiners check this; a mismatch costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Missing the Shape Function Definition:** In derivations, you must state $N_1, N_2$ explicitly before differentiating.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Principle of Stationary Potential Energy**

- ✅ **Core Statement:** δΠ = 0, where Π = U − W.
- ✅ **Π is a functional** (depends on a function u(x), not just a variable).
- ✅ **Strain Energy:** $U = \int_{\Omega} \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^{T}\varepsilon \, dV$ — for 1D bar: $U = \int \tfrac{AE}{2}(du/dx)^{2} dx$.
- ✅ **External Work:** $W = \int u^T b \, dV + \int_{\Gamma_t} u^T t \, dS + \{d\}^T\{F\}$.
- ✅ **Admissible Field u** must satisfy: (i) essential BCs, (ii) C⁰ continuity, (iii) smoothness inside elements.
- ✅ **First Variation δΠ = 0** ≡ strong-form differential equation (Euler-Lagrange equivalence).
- ✅ **Natural BCs** (tractions) emerge automatically from integration by parts — no need to impose separately.
- ✅ **Discrete Equation:** $[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$, where $[K] = \sum_e \int B^T D B \, dV$.
- ✅ **1D Bar Stiffness:** $k^{(e)} = (AE/L)\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — **memorize this!**
- ✅ **Element Force Vector:** $F^{(e)} = \int N^T b \, dV + \int N^T t \, dS + $ point loads.
- ✅ **Stable System → Minimum Π** (PMPE form). Unstable → no minimum (e.g., buckling).
- ✅ **PSPE is a Cornerstone of FEM** — it is the variational backbone of the displacement method.
- ✅ **Rayleigh-Ritz uses PSPE** to discretize the functional; **Galerkin** uses weighted residuals — both arrive at the same [K]{d}={F}.
- ✅ **Units Vigilance:** $[K]$ in N/m, $\{F\}$ in N, $\{d\}$ in m. Stress = Pa or N/m².
- ✅ **Solution Steps:** Form Π → discretize u → substitute → δΠ = 0 → assemble [K] → apply BCs → solve for {d} → recover ε, σ.
- ✅ **Verification:** Check global equilibrium (∑R + ∑F_applied = 0); check strain energy positivity.
- ✅ **Common KTU Question Patterns:** "Derive [K] for bar using PSPE", "Apply PMPE to find deflection", "Explain δΠ = 0 with example".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rayleigh-Ritz Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rayleigh-Ritz Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rayleigh-Ritz Method (RRM):** A *variational numerical technique* used to obtain an *approximate solution* to a boundary value problem (BVP) by assuming the dependent variable as a linear combination of a set of *admissible (trial) functions* that satisfy the *essential (geometric/boundary) conditions*, and then minimizing the *total potential energy functional* (or equivalent functional) of the system with respect to the unknown coefficients.

In the context of the KTU 2024 FEA syllabus (Module 2), the Rayleigh-Ritz method is the **mathematical precursor** to the Finite Element Method. It transforms a *differential equation with boundary conditions* into a *matrix algebra problem* of the form:

$$[K]\{c\} = \{F\}$$

where $[K]$ is the **stiffness matrix**, $\{c\}$ is the vector of *unknown coefficients*, and $\{F\}$ is the **load vector** derived from the chosen trial functions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: "The Painter and the Wall"**
> Imagine you are an artist tasked with painting a wall. You don't know the exact pattern the owner wants, but you know the *edges* (top, bottom, sides) must match specific colors. So you guess a *smooth curve* (your trial function) that matches the edges and adjust the curve's "knobs" (coefficients) until the picture looks closest to what seems natural (minimum total "energy" of the curve's bending). The Rayleigh-Ritz method does exactly this: it guesses a smooth shape that satisfies the boundary constraints and "tunes" the coefficients to minimize the stored strain energy in the body.

- **Trial Function** = Your guessed smooth curve.
- **Boundary Conditions** = The fixed colors at the wall edges.
- **Minimization** = Adjusting the curve until the energy is minimum.
- **Coefficients $\{c\}$** = The "tuning knobs."

### 1.3 Historical & Engineering Relevance

The Rayleigh-Ritz method was formalized by **Walther Ritz (1909)**, building on the principle established by **Lord Rayleigh (1877)**. It is the *intellectual foundation* of the modern FEM, and in KTU Module 2 it is the *gateway topic* — every concept learned here is generalized to discrete elements later in Modules 3 and 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric (Standard Convention in RRM):**
> - Strain Energy: $U$ (in **Joules, J** or **N·m**)
> - Work Potential: $W$ (in **Joules, J**)
> - Total Potential Energy: $\Pi = U - W$ (in **Joules, J**)
> - Minimum Principle: $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_i} = 0 \;\; \forall i$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Steps of the Rayleigh-Ritz Procedure

The Rayleigh-Ritz method follows a **rigorous five-step algorithm** for any boundary value problem:

1. **Step 1 — Formulate the Functional**
   Identify the variational functional $\Pi(u)$ corresponding to the governing differential equation. For solid mechanics, this is the **total potential energy functional**:
   $$\Pi = U(u) - W(u)$$
   where $U$ is the *strain energy* and $W$ is the *work done by external forces*.

2. **Step 2 — Select the Trial Function (Admissible Function)**
   Assume an approximate solution of the form:
   $$u(x) \approx \tilde{u}(x) = c_1 \phi_1(x) + c_2 \phi_2(x) + \cdots + c_n \phi_n(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \phi_i(x)$$
   The trial functions $\phi_i(x)$ must:
   - Be **linearly independent**.
   - Each satisfy the **essential (geometric) boundary conditions** individually.
   - Be *at least* of the same continuity class as the differential operator (e.g., $C^1$ for 4th order beams).

3. **Step 3 — Substitute into the Functional**
   Replace $u(x)$ with $\tilde{u}(x)$ in $\Pi$. This yields an *algebraic function* of the unknown coefficients $c_i$.

4. **Step 4 — Minimize the Functional**
   Impose the *stationary condition*:
   $$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_i} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
   This generates a system of $n$ linear algebraic equations in $n$ unknowns.

5. **Step 5 — Solve the Linear System**
   Solve $[K]\{c\} = \{F\}$ to obtain the coefficients $c_i$, then reconstruct the approximate solution.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Examiner's Logical Flow)

- **Why the total potential energy?** Because for *self-adjoint, positive-definite* operators, the governing differential equation is the *Euler-Lagrange equation* of $\Pi$. So minimizing $\Pi$ is mathematically equivalent to solving the BVP.
- **Why admissible functions only?** Because violating the geometric BCs makes the solution *physically inadmissible* (e.g., a clamped beam with non-zero rotation at the wall).
- **Why minimization?** The equilibrium configuration of an *elastic body under conservative loading* is the one with the *minimum total potential energy* (Principle of Minimum Potential Energy).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most frequently tested** equations in the KTU Board Examination for this topic.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Application Domain** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total Potential Energy | $\Pi = U - W$ | All conservative solid mechanics problems |
| 2 | Strain Energy (1D Bar) | $U = \frac{EA}{2} \int_0^L \left(\dfrac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx$ | Axial deformation |
| 3 | Strain Energy (Euler-Bernoulli Beam) | $U = \frac{EI}{2} \int_0^L \left(\dfrac{d^2 w}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx$ | Bending deformation |
| 4 | Strain Energy (Torsion) | $U = \frac{GJ}{2} \int_0^L \left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\right)^2 dx$ | Torsion of shafts |
| 5 | Strain Energy (2D/3D) | $U = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \{\varepsilon\}^T [D] \{\varepsilon\} dV$ | General elasticity |
| 6 | External Work (Point Load) | $W = P \cdot u(L)$ | Point load at boundary |
| 7 | External Work (Distributed) | $W = \int_0^L q(x) \cdot u(x)\, dx$ | Body / surface forces |
| 8 | Rayleigh-Ritz Trial Form | $\tilde{u}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \phi_i(x)$ | Universal approximation |
| 9 | Stationarity Condition | $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_i} = 0 \quad (i=1,\dots,n)$ | Linear algebraic system |
| 10 | Generalized Stiffness Element | $K_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 U}{\partial c_i \partial c_j}$ | Stiffness matrix coefficient |
| 11 | Generalized Load Element | $F_i = \dfrac{\partial W}{\partial c_i}$ | Load vector component |
| 12 | Symmetry Property | $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$ | Due to strain energy's quadratic form |
| 13 | Membrane Analogy Stiffness | $K_{ij} = \int_0^1 \dfrac{d\phi_i}{d\xi}\dfrac{d\phi_j}{d\xi} d\xi$ | Non-dimensional form (1D) |
| 14 | Polynomial Trial Set | $\phi_i(x) = x^i$ or $\phi_i(x) = x^{i-1}(x - L)$ | Power series (must satisfy BCs) |
| 15 | Trigonometric Trial Set | $\phi_i(x) = \sin\dfrac{i\pi x}{L}$ | For $u(0) = u(L) = 0$ problems |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Mechanics**: Approximate deflection of beams, plates, and shells without solving complex ODEs.
- **Aerospace**: Buckling load estimation of stiffened panels.
- **Vibration Analysis**: Natural frequency estimation (Rayleigh's quotient is a special case of RRM with one term).
- **Foundation Engineering**: Settlement of soil masses.
- **Heat Transfer**: Steady-state temperature distribution in 1D fins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on Accuracy:** The Rayleigh-Ritz method provides an *upper bound* on the strain energy (and hence, often, a *lower bound* on displacements) due to the **minimum energy theorem**. Adding more terms in the trial function always improves (or maintains) the accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: Stepped Axial Bar under Self-Weight (2-Element Rayleigh-Ritz)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Board Standard):**
> A uniform bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ is fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A distributed axial body force $q_0$ (force per unit length) acts throughout. Use the Rayleigh-Ritz method with a 2-term polynomial trial function to obtain the approximate displacement field and compare with the exact solution.

#### Step 1: Exact Governing Equation and Boundary Conditions

The governing ODE is:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(EA\frac{du}{dx}\right) + q_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = -\frac{q_0}{EA}$$

(with $A$ and $E$ constant). Boundary Conditions:
- **Essential BC:** $u(0) = 0$
- **Natural BC:** $\left.\dfrac{du}{dx}\right|_{x=L} = 0$ (free end, no axial force)

The exact solution is:
$$u_{exact}(x) = \frac{q_0}{2EA}\left(L^2 - x^2\right)$$

#### Step 2: Formulate the Total Potential Energy Functional

The strain energy in the bar:
$$U = \frac{EA}{2}\int_0^L \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx$$

The work done by the body force:
$$W = \int_0^L q_0 \cdot u(x)\, dx$$

Total potential energy:
$$\Pi = U - W = \frac{EA}{2}\int_0^L \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx - \int_0^L q_0 \, u(x)\, dx$$

#### Step 3: Select Admissible Trial Function

Since $u(0) = 0$ is the only essential BC, we choose a polynomial that vanishes at $x=0$. A 2-term polynomial trial function is:

$$\tilde{u}(x) = c_1 \phi_1(x) + c_2 \phi_2(x) = c_1 x + c_2 x^2$$

Here, $\phi_1(x) = x$ and $\phi_2(x) = x^2$ each satisfy $u(0) = 0$ automatically. Note: the natural BC at $x = L$ is *not enforced* — it is naturally obtained (this is the power of the variational method).

#### Step 4: Compute Derivatives and Substitute

$$\frac{d\tilde{u}}{dx} = c_1 + 2c_2 x$$

Substitute into the strain energy:
$$U = \frac{EA}{2}\int_0^L (c_1 + 2c_2 x)^2 dx$$

Expanding the integrand:
$$(c_1 + 2c_2 x)^2 = c_1^2 + 4c_1 c_2 x + 4 c_2^2 x^2$$

Integrating term-by-term:
$$U = \frac{EA}{2}\left[c_1^2 x + 2c_1 c_2 x^2 + \frac{4c_2^2 x^3}{3}\right]_0^L$$

$$U = \frac{EA}{2}\left[c_1^2 L + 2c_1 c_2 L^2 + \frac{4c_2^2 L^3}{3}\right]$$

$$U = \frac{EAL}{2} c_1^2 + EAL^2 c_1 c_2 + \frac{2EAL^3}{3} c_2^2$$

Now substitute into the work term:
$$W = \int_0^L q_0 (c_1 x + c_2 x^2) dx = q_0 \left[\frac{c_1 x^2}{2} + \frac{c_2 x^3}{3}\right]_0^L$$

$$W = q_0 \left[\frac{c_1 L^2}{2} + \frac{c_2 L^3}{3}\right] = \frac{q_0 L^2}{2} c_1 + \frac{q_0 L^3}{3} c_2$$

The total potential energy becomes:
$$\Pi = \frac{EAL}{2} c_1^2 + EAL^2 c_1 c_2 + \frac{2EAL^3}{3} c_2^2 - \frac{q_0 L^2}{2} c_1 - \frac{q_0 L^3}{3} c_2$$

#### Step 5: Impose the Stationarity Conditions

We require $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_1} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_2} = 0$.

**Differentiate with respect to $c_1$:**
$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_1} = EAL \cdot c_1 + EAL^2 \cdot c_2 - \frac{q_0 L^2}{2} = 0$$

Dividing by $EAL$:
$$c_1 + L c_2 = \frac{q_0 L}{2EA} \quad \cdots (i)$$

**Differentiate with respect to $c_2$:**
$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_2} = EAL^2 \cdot c_1 + \frac{4EAL^3}{3} \cdot c_2 - \frac{q_0 L^3}{3} = 0$$

Dividing by $EAL^2$:
$$c_1 + \frac{4L}{3} c_2 = \frac{q_0 L}{3EA} \quad \cdots (ii)$$

#### Step 6: Solve the Linear System

Subtracting $(i)$ from $(ii)$:
$$\left(\frac{4L}{3} - L\right) c_2 = \frac{q_0 L}{3EA} - \frac{q_0 L}{2EA}$$

$$\frac{L}{3} c_2 = q_0 L \left(\frac{1}{3EA} - \frac{1}{2EA}\right) = \frac{q_0 L}{EA}\left(\frac{2 - 3}{6}\right) = -\frac{q_0 L}{6EA}$$

$$c_2 = -\frac{q_0}{2EA}$$

Substitute back into $(i)$:
$$c_1 + L \cdot \left(-\frac{q_0}{2EA}\right) = \frac{q_0 L}{2EA}$$

$$c_1 = \frac{q_0 L}{2EA} + \frac{q_0 L}{2EA} = \frac{q_0 L}{EA}$$

#### Step 7: Construct the Approximate Solution

$$\tilde{u}(x) = \frac{q_0 L}{EA} x - \frac{q_0}{2EA} x^2 = \frac{q_0}{2EA}(2Lx - x^2)$$

**Compare with the exact solution:**

$$u_{exact}(x) = \frac{q_0}{2EA}(L^2 - x^2) = \frac{q_0}{2EA}(L-x)(L+x)$$

$$\tilde{u}(x) = \frac{q_0}{2EA}(2Lx - x^2) = \frac{q_0}{2EA}x(2L - x)$$

**Key observation:** With a 2-term trial function, the Rayleigh-Ritz solution **does not exactly reproduce** the exact solution. This is because a 2-term polynomial *cannot span* the exact solution space $\{1, x, x^2\}$ in its entirety. However, the **boundary condition at $x = L$** (natural BC) is naturally satisfied:

$$\frac{d\tilde{u}}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = \frac{q_0 L}{EA} - \frac{q_0 L}{EA} = 0 \checkmark$$

The error is bounded by the **upper energy theorem**.

### 3.2 The Analytical Matrix Structure

The minimization $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_i} = 0$ generically produces:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[\int_0^L \frac{\partial \tilde{u}}{\partial c_i} \cdot \text{operator}\left(\frac{\partial \tilde{u}}{\partial c_j}\right) dx\right] c_j &= \int_0^L \frac{\partial \tilde{u}}{\partial c_i} \cdot f(x)\, dx \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix notation:
$$[K]\{c\} = \{F\}$$

For the 1D bar problem, the element matrix entries are:
$$K_{ij} = EA \int_0^L \frac{d\phi_i}{dx} \frac{d\phi_j}{dx} dx \quad ; \quad F_i = \int_0^L \phi_i(x) q_0 \, dx$$

This *exact same structure* will reappear in the Finite Element Method — but with **piecewise trial functions** instead of global polynomials.

### 3.3 Ritz Method for an Euler-Bernoulli Cantilever Beam (KTU Classic)

For a cantilever beam of length $L$ with tip load $P$ at $x = L$:

- **Governing ODE:** $EI \dfrac{d^4 w}{dx^4} = 0$
- **BCs:** $w(0) = 0$, $w'(0) = 0$, $EI w''(L) = 0$, $EI w'''(L) = -P$

**Strain energy and work:**
$$U = \frac{EI}{2}\int_0^L \left(\frac{d^2 w}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx \quad ; \quad W = P \cdot w(L)$$

**Trial function (must satisfy $w(0) = w'(0) = 0$):**
$$w(x) = c_1 x^2 + c_2 x^3$$
(This is the famous *cracked-hinge* polynomial that satisfies the clamped-end BCs.)

$$\frac{d^2 w}{dx^2} = 2c_1 + 6c_2 x$$

$$U = \frac{EI}{2}\int_0^L (2c_1 + 6c_2 x)^2 dx = \frac{EI}{2}\left[4c_1^2 L + 12 c_1 c_2 L^2 + 12 c_2^2 L^3\right]$$

$$U = 2EI L \cdot c_1^2 + 6EI L^2 \cdot c_1 c_2 + 6 EI L^3 \cdot c_2^2$$

$$W = P(c_1 L^2 + c_2 L^3)$$

**Stationarity conditions:**

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_1} = 4EI L \cdot c_1 + 6EI L^2 \cdot c_2 - P L^2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_2} = 6EI L^2 \cdot c_1 + 12 EI L^3 \cdot c_2 - P L^3 = 0$$

Dividing by $EL^2$ and $EL^3$ respectively:

$$4 c_1 + 6 L c_2 = \frac{P L}{EI} \quad \cdots (1)$$

$$6 c_1 + 12 L c_2 = \frac{P L}{EI} \quad \cdots (2)$$

Subtracting $(1)$ from $(2)$:
$$2 c_1 + 6 L c_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_1 = -3 L c_2$$

Substituting in $(1)$:
$$4(-3L c_2) + 6 L c_2 = \frac{PL}{EI} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -6 L c_2 = \frac{PL}{EI}$$

$$c_2 = -\frac{P}{6EI}$$

$$c_1 = -3L \cdot \left(-\frac{P}{6EI}\right) = \frac{PL}{2EI}$$

Therefore:
$$w(x) = \frac{PL}{2EI} x^2 - \frac{P}{6EI} x^3 = \frac{Px^2}{6EI}(3L - x)$$

**Tip deflection at $x = L$:**
$$w(L) = \frac{PL^3}{6EI}(3L - L) = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the *exact* solution!** This is a remarkable property: the Ritz method with the *cracked-hinge* polynomial reproduces the *exact* solution for a cantilever beam under a tip load because the *exact solution is itself a cubic polynomial*.

### 3.4 Comparative Analysis Table: Rayleigh-Ritz vs. FEM (KTU Module 2 vs. Module 3)

| **Aspect** | **Rayleigh-Ritz Method** | **Finite Element Method** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trial function type | *Global* over the entire domain | *Piecewise* (local to each element) |
| Continuity requirement | Must be smooth globally | Only $C^0$ or $C^1$ at element boundaries |
| Mesh | Not required | Discretized into elements |
| Computational cost | High for complex geometry | Low — sparse matrices, scalable |
| Convergence | Slow with polynomial order | Fast with mesh refinement ($h$- or $p$-refinement) |
| Matrix structure | Dense | Sparse, banded |
| Applicability to complex geometry | Poor | Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Rayleigh-Ritz Method

The following Mermaid diagram shows the *algorithmic flow* and the *data dependencies* between each step of the Rayleigh-Ritz procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Boundary Value Problem] --> B[Step 1: Identify the Variational Functional Pi]
    B --> C[Step 2: Define Strain Energy U and External Work W]
    C --> D[Step 3: Choose Admissible Trial Function phi_i x]
    D --> E{Step 4: Verify Essential BCs Satisfied?}
    E -->|No| F[Modify Trial Function Set]
    F --> D
    E -->|Yes| G[Step 5: Substitute u_tilde into Pi]
    G --> H[Step 6: Compute Partial Derivatives dPi dc_i]
    H --> I[Step 7: Assemble Stiffness Matrix K and Load Vector F]
    I --> J[Step 8: Solve Linear System K c equals F]
    J --> K[Step 9: Reconstruct Approximate Solution u_tilde]
    K --> L[Step 10: Validate Natural BCs and Compare with Exact]
    L --> M{Acceptable Accuracy?}
    M -->|No| N[Add More Trial Terms]
    N --> D
    M -->|Yes| O[End: Final Approximate Solution]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Trial Function Selection Logic

The choice of admissible functions is a critical *decision point* in the RRM. The following diagram maps out the decision tree:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem Definition] --> Q{Order of Differential Equation?}
    Q -->|2nd Order Bar| R1[Polynomial: u = c1 x + c2 x to power n]
    Q -->|4th Order Beam| R2[Polynomial: w = c1 x to power 2 + c2 x to power 3 plus higher]
    Q -->|2nd Order with u(0) = u(L) = 0| R3[Trigonometric: phi = sin i pi x over L]
    R1 --> S{BC at x=0?}
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    S -->|Clamped| T1[Multiply by factor x or x to power 2]
    S -->|Simply Supported| T2[Multiply by factor x times L minus x]
    S -->|Free-Free| T3[Polynomial OK directly]
    T1 --> U[Final Trial Function Set]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
```

### 4.3 Visual Representation of Approximation Convergence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[1 Term Trial: Linear] --> A1[2 Term Trial: Quadratic]
    A1 --> A2[3 Term Trial: Cubic]
    A2 --> A3[n Term Trial: Polynomial of Order n-1]
    A3 --> A4[Infinite Terms: Exact Solution]
    A0 -.->|Error = 50 percent| B[Strain Energy Error]
    A1 -.->|Error = 20 percent| B
    A2 -.->|Error = 8 percent| B
    A3 -.->|Error approaches 0| B
    A4 -.->|Error = 0| B
    B --> C[Upper Bound on Strain Energy]
    C --> D[Lower Bound on Displacement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Note:** As the number of trial function terms $n$ increases, the strain energy error monotonically *decreases* and approaches the exact value from *above*. This is the *Rayleigh upper bound theorem* in action — a key high-yield KTU exam point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Paper Set 2]**
*State the principle of minimum total potential energy. How is it utilized in the Rayleigh-Ritz method?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Minimum Total Potential Energy:** For a conservative elastic system, the *true* displacement field is the one that makes the *total potential energy* $\Pi = U - W$ *stationary* (specifically, a *minimum*) with respect to all *kinematically admissible* displacement fields.

**In the Rayleigh-Ritz Method (3-mark structure):**
- (1 mark) The governing differential equation is the *Euler-Lagrange equation* of the functional $\Pi$.
- (1 mark) The method *assumes* a trial function of the form $u \approx \sum c_i \phi_i$ and substitutes it into $\Pi$.
- (1 mark) Minimization of $\Pi$ w.r.t. the coefficients $c_i$ yields the best approximation among the chosen trial function space.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between admissible and non-admissible trial functions in the context of the Rayleigh-Ritz method. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) **Admissible Trial Functions:** Functions that satisfy the *essential (geometric) boundary conditions* of the problem and possess the *required order of continuity* ($C^0$ for 2nd order, $C^1$ for 4th order).
- (1 mark) **Example of Admissible:** For a simply supported beam of length $L$ with $w(0) = w(L) = 0$, the function $\phi(x) = \sin\left(\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$ is admissible.
- (1 mark) **Non-Admissible Example:** A function $w(x) = c_1 + c_2 x$ does *not* satisfy $w(0) = w(L) = 0$ simultaneously, hence it is non-admissible for that problem.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Main Exam]**
A uniform bar of length $L = 1$ m, cross-sectional area $A = 200$ mm², and Young's modulus $E = 200 \times 10^9$ N/m² is fixed at one end ($x = 0$) and subjected to a uniformly distributed axial load $q_0 = 10$ kN/m. Using the Rayleigh-Ritz method with the trial function $u(x) = c_1 x + c_2 x^2 + c_3 x^3$, determine:
- **(a)** The values of the coefficients $c_1$, $c_2$, $c_3$. (7 Marks)
- **(b)** The displacement at the free end $x = L$. Compare it with the exact solution. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The total potential energy for an axially loaded bar:
$$\Pi = \frac{EA}{2}\int_0^L \left(\frac{du}{dx}\right)^2 dx - \int_0^L q_0 u(x)\, dx$$

Given: $u(x) = c_1 x + c_2 x^2 + c_3 x^3$, hence $\dfrac{du}{dx} = c_1 + 2c_2 x + 3c_3 x^2$.

**Strain Energy:**
$$U = \frac{EA}{2}\int_0^L (c_1 + 2c_2 x + 3c_3 x^2)^2 dx$$

Expanding:
$$(c_1 + 2c_2 x + 3c_3 x^2)^2 = c_1^2 + 4c_1 c_2 x + (4c_2^2 + 6c_1 c_3)x^2 + 12 c_2 c_3 x^3 + 9 c_3^2 x^4$$

Integrating term-by-term from 0 to $L$:
$$U = \frac{EA}{2}\left[c_1^2 L + 2c_1 c_2 L^2 + \left(\frac{4c_2^2}{3} + 2c_1 c_3\right)L^3 + 3c_2 c_3 L^4 + \frac{9c_3^2 L^5}{5}\right]$$

**Work Term:**
$$W = q_0 \int_0^L (c_1 x + c_2 x^2 + c_3 x^3) dx = q_0 \left[\frac{c_1 L^2}{2} + \frac{c_2 L^3}{3} + \frac{c_3 L^4}{4}\right]$$

**Stationarity Conditions:**

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_1} = EAL \cdot c_1 + EAL^2 \cdot c_2 + EA L^3 \cdot c_3 - \frac{q_0 L^2}{2} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_2} = EAL^2 \cdot c_1 + \frac{4EAL^3}{3} c_2 + \frac{3EAL^4}{2} c_3 - \frac{q_0 L^3}{3} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_3} = EAL^3 \cdot c_1 + \frac{3EAL^4}{2} c_2 + \frac{9EAL^5}{5} c_3 - \frac{q_0 L^4}{4} = 0$$

Substituting numerical values ($L = 1$ m, $EA = 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^9 = 4 \times 10^7$ N, $q_0 = 10^4$ N/m):

Dividing the first equation by $EAL$ and the others similarly:

$$c_1 + c_2 + c_3 = \frac{q_0 L}{2EA} = \frac{10^4}{2 \times 4 \times 10^7} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$c_1 + \frac{4}{3}c_2 + \frac{3}{2}c_3 = \frac{q_0 L}{3EA} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$c_1 + \frac{3}{2}c_2 + \frac{9}{5}c_3 = \frac{q_0 L}{4EA} = 6.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

Solving the 3x3 system (subtracting equations successively):

- $(2) - (1)$: $\dfrac{1}{3}c_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}c_3 = -4.167 \times 10^{-5}$
- $(3) - (2)$: $\dfrac{1}{6}c_2 + \dfrac{3}{10}c_3 = -2.083 \times 10^{-5}$

Solving these two: $c_3 = -1.25 \times 10^{-4}$, $c_2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-5}$, and from $(1)$: $c_1 = 1.875 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Final coefficients:** $c_1 = 1.875 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $c_2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $c_3 = -1.25 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

$$u(L) = c_1 L + c_2 L^2 + c_3 L^3 = 1.875 \times 10^{-4} + 6.25 \times 10^{-5} - 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$u(L) = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.125 \text{ mm}$$

**Exact Solution:**
$$u_{exact}(L) = \frac{q_0 L^2}{2EA} = \frac{10^4 \times 1}{2 \times 4 \times 10^7} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.125 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Observation:** The Rayleigh-Ritz solution with a *3-term cubic* trial function *exactly reproduces* the exact solution at the boundary $x = L$ because the exact solution is a polynomial of degree 2 (or can be expressed as a combination of $x, x^2, x^3$). This is the **completeness property** in action.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Main Exam]**
A simply supported beam of length $L$ is subjected to a uniformly distributed load $w_0$ (force per unit length) over its entire span. Using the Rayleigh-Ritz method with the trial function:
$$v(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \sin\left(\frac{i\pi x}{L}\right)$$
- **(a)** Derive the expression for the *minimum total potential energy* and the *load vector* for a 2-term approximation ($i = 1, 2$). (7 Marks)
- **(b)** Solve for $c_1$ and $c_2$ and compute the maximum deflection at the center. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

For a simply supported beam:
- **BCs:** $v(0) = v(L) = 0$ and $v''(0) = v''(L) = 0$ (natural BCs).

The trial function:
$$v(x) = c_1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) + c_2 \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)$$

**Strain Energy:**
$$U = \frac{EI}{2}\int_0^L \left(\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx$$

Compute second derivative:
$$\frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = -c_1 \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) - c_2 \left(\frac{2\pi}{L}\right)^2 \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)$$

Substituting:
$$U = \frac{EI}{2}\int_0^L \left[c_1^2 \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^4 \sin^2\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) + 2c_1 c_2 \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^2 \left(\frac{2\pi}{L}\right)^2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right)\sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right) + c_2^2 \left(\frac{2\pi}{L}\right)^4 \sin^2\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)\right] dx$$

Using orthogonal integral identities:
$$\int_0^L \sin^2\left(\frac{i\pi x}{L}\right) dx = \frac{L}{2}$$

$$\int_0^L \sin\left(\frac{i\pi x}{L}\right)\sin\left(\frac{j\pi x}{L}\right) dx = 0 \quad (i \neq j)$$

Therefore:
$$U = \frac{EI}{2}\left[\frac{c_1^2 \pi^4}{L^4} \cdot \frac{L}{2} + \frac{c_2^2 \cdot 16\pi^4}{L^4} \cdot \frac{L}{2}\right] = \frac{EI\pi^4}{4L^3}\left(c_1^2 + 16 c_2^2\right)$$

**Work Term:**
$$W = \int_0^L w_0 v(x) dx = w_0 \int_0^L \left[c_1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) + c_2 \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)\right] dx$$

Using:
$$\int_0^L \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) dx = \frac{L}{n\pi}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]$$

For $n = 1$ (odd): $\dfrac{2L}{\pi}$. For $n = 2$ (even): $0$.

$$W = w_0 \left[c_1 \cdot \frac{2L}{\pi} + c_2 \cdot 0\right] = \frac{2 w_0 L}{\pi} c_1$$

**Total Potential Energy:**
$$\Pi = \frac{EI\pi^4}{4L^3}(c_1^2 + 16 c_2^2) - \frac{2w_0 L}{\pi} c_1$$

**Stiffness Matrix and Load Vector:**
$$[K] = \frac{EI\pi^4}{4L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 16 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad \{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \dfrac{2 w_0 L}{\pi} \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Stationarity:
$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_1} = \frac{EI\pi^4}{2L^3} c_1 - \frac{2w_0 L}{\pi} = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = \frac{4 w_0 L^4}{\pi^5 EI}$$

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_2} = \frac{8 EI \pi^4}{L^3} c_2 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = 0$$

**Maximum deflection at $x = L/2$:**
$$v_{max} = v(L/2) = c_1 \sin(\pi/2) + 0 = c_1 = \frac{4 w_0 L^4}{\pi^5 EI}$$

Compare with exact: $v_{exact}(L/2) = \dfrac{5 w_0 L^4}{384 EI}$

$$\frac{v_{Ritz}}{v_{exact}} = \frac{4/\pi^5}{5/384} = \frac{4 \times 384}{5 \pi^5} = \frac{1536}{5\pi^5} \approx 0.9970$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remarkable Result:** A *single-term* trigonometric Ritz approximation is only **0.3% in error** for the maximum deflection of a uniformly loaded simply supported beam — a KTU-board standard *high-yield comparison point*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Critical Pitfall #1: Forcing Natural Boundary Conditions**
> Many students incorrectly *enforce* the natural (force) boundary conditions in the trial function. This is **WRONG**. Only *essential (geometric) BCs* should be explicitly satisfied. Natural BCs are *automatically* satisfied by the variational formulation (this is the *naturalness condition*).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Critical Pitfall #2: Differentiating Twice for Beam Strain Energy**
> A frequent error is to use $\left(\dfrac{dv}{dx}\right)^2$ instead of $\left(\dfrac{d^2 v}{dx^2}\right)^2$ in the strain energy integral. The strain energy in an Euler-Bernoulli beam is **quadratic in curvature**, not slope.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Critical Pitfall #3: Continuity Mismatch**
> For *4th-order* beam problems, the trial function must be **$C^1$ continuous** (i.e., the *first derivative* must also be continuous). Using $C^0$ piecewise linear functions will fail and give *zero* strain energy.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Critical Pitfall #4: Sign Convention for the Functional**
> The total potential energy is $\Pi = U - W$, not $U + W$. The negative sign on the work term is the **key** to correct minimization.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Critical Pitfall #5: Misapplying the Rayleigh Quotient**
> Students often confuse the **Rayleigh-Ritz method** (which uses multiple terms) with the **Rayleigh quotient** (which uses a single assumed mode shape to estimate natural frequencies). These are *related but distinct* concepts.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "10 Most Important Things to Remember" about the Rayleigh-Ritz Method for KTU Board Exam:**

- ☐ The **Rayleigh-Ritz method is a variational method** based on the *Principle of Minimum Total Potential Energy*: $\Pi = U - W$.
- ☐ The trial function is $u \approx \sum c_i \phi_i$ where $\phi_i$ are **admissible** (satisfy *essential BCs* and have the *right continuity*).
- ☐ For **2nd-order ODEs** (bars, torsion), trial functions must be **$C^0$ continuous**.
- ☐ For **4th-order ODEs** (beams, plates), trial functions must be **$C^1$ continuous** (continuous in *value and first derivative*).
- ☐ The minimization $\dfrac{\partial \Pi}{\partial c_i} = 0$ produces a **linear system $[K]\{c\} = \{F\}$**, where $[K]$ is symmetric and positive-definite.
- ☐ The **stiffness matrix entries** are $K_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 U}{\partial c_i \partial c_j}$ and **load vector entries** are $F_i = \dfrac{\partial W}{\partial c_i}$.
- ☐ Adding more trial terms **monotonically improves** the accuracy (Rayleigh's *upper bound theorem*).
- ☐ The **natural (force) boundary conditions are automatically satisfied** by the variational method — *do not force them into the trial function*.
- ☐ The **Ritz method is the bridge** between the classical variational methods (Rayleigh, Galerkin) and the modern **Finite Element Method** (with piecewise local trial functions).
- ☐ The **Ritz method with a 2-term cracked-hinge polynomial ($x^2, x^3$)** for a cantilever beam with tip load *exactly reproduces* the classical $PL^3/(3EI)$ result.

---

**End of Module 2 — Rayleigh-Ritz Method Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
*Prepared in alignment with PEPET632 syllabus, NEP 2020 outcomes, and KTU Board Examiner valuation patterns.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the Finite Element Method (FEM), **"Simple Examples"** refers to the systematic application of the fundamental one-dimensional (1D) FEA formulation procedure to idealized engineering problems — typically **stepped bars under axial loading**, **spring assemblies**, and **simple truss elements** — where the domain can be discretized into a small number of two-noded linear elements. The objective is to demonstrate the complete workflow: **discretization → element stiffness matrix derivation → assembly → application of boundary conditions → solution for nodal displacements → post-processing for stresses and reactions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Module 2 of PEPET632 focuses on the **Direct Stiffness Method (DSM)** for 1D bar/spring/truss problems. "Simple Examples" is the bridge between the theory of shape functions/element matrices and real problem-solving. Expect a **14-mark Part B question** (with internal choice) on this exact topic in every KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a **telescopic walking stick** made of two segments — a thinner upper portion and a thicker lower portion — fixed at the bottom and pulled at the top.

- Each segment acts like an **independent "spring"** with its own stiffness.
- The thinner segment is **more flexible** (lower stiffness) than the thicker one.
- When you pull the stick, **both segments stretch**, but the thinner one stretches **more**.
- The **junction point** between the two segments has a displacement that must be **continuous** (the two pieces must remain physically connected).

> This is exactly what a **stepped bar FEA problem** models. Each element is a "spring" representing axial stiffness $k = \frac{AE}{L}$, and the FEA assembles these springs into a global system, applies the boundary conditions (fixed end, applied load), and solves for the unknown displacements.

### Key Physical Constants Used in KTU Problems

| Material | Young's Modulus (E) |
|---|---|
| **Steel** | **200 GPa (2×10¹¹ N/m²)** |
| **Aluminium** | **70 GPa (7×10¹⁰ N/m²)** |
| **Copper** | **110 GPa (1.1×10¹¹ N/m²)** |
| **Cast Iron** | **100 GPa (1×10¹¹ N/m²)** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems in FEA Module 2 almost exclusively use **Steel with E = 200 GPa**. Memorize this value. Areas are typically given in **mm²** and lengths in **m**, so always convert units to a **consistent SI system (N, m, Pa)** before computation.

---

## The General 1D Bar Element

A **two-noded linear bar element** with nodes $i$ and $j$, length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ has:

- **2 degrees of freedom (DOFs):** $u_i$ and $u_j$ (axial displacements)
- **Element stiffness matrix** (derived from the principle of minimum potential energy or direct equilibrium):

$$k^e = \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- **Element nodal force vector:**

$$\{f\}^e = \begin{Bmatrix} f_i \\ f_j \end{Bmatrix}$$

This is the foundational building block for **every simple example** in KTU Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Complete FEA Workflow for Simple 1D Examples

The procedure to solve any "simple example" in KTU FEA follows a strict **7-step protocol**:

1. **Discretization:** Divide the domain into a finite number of elements. Number the nodes and elements.
2. **Element Property Identification:** For each element, identify $E$, $A$, and $L$.
3. **Element Stiffness Matrix Computation:** Compute $k^e = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1\\ -1 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ for every element.
4. **Global Stiffness Matrix Assembly:** Sum contributions from each element into a global matrix $K$ using the **Direct Stiffness Method (DSM)** — i.e., for each $k^e_{ij}$, add to $K_{mn}$ where $m, n$ are the global node numbers.
5. **Apply Boundary Conditions:** For fixed (constrained) nodes, set the corresponding row and column in $K$ to zero, set the corresponding displacement to zero, and move the known force terms to the right-hand side.
6. **Solve the Reduced System:** Solve $K_{reduced} \cdot u_{reduced} = F_{reduced}$ for unknown nodal displacements.
7. **Post-Processing:** Compute element stresses using $\sigma = E \cdot \frac{u_j - u_i}{L}$ and support reactions from the original equilibrium equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners award marks strictly following these 7 steps. You must **show each step clearly** in the answer booklet. Jumping directly to the global matrix without showing element matrices is a **common reason for losing 2-3 marks**.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Element Stiffness (Bar) | $k^e = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1\\ -1 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | N/m | Symmetric, singular |
| 2 | Element Nodal Forces | $\{f\}^e = k^e \{u\}^e$ | N | Linear relation |
| 3 | Global Assembly | $K_{ij} = \sum_{e} k^e_{mn}$ (sum over elements containing global DOF $i, j$) | N/m | DSM rule |
| 4 | Global Equilibrium | $K \{U\} = \{F\}$ | N/m, m, N | Square linear system |
| 5 | Element Stress | $\sigma^e = \frac{E}{L}(u_j - u_i)$ | Pa | Post-processing |
| 6 | Element Strain | $\epsilon^e = \frac{u_j - u_i}{L}$ | Dimensionless | Linear shape fn |
| 7 | Shape Function (Linear) | $N_1 = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2 = \frac{x}{L}$ | — | Sum = 1 (partition of unity) |
| 8 | Reaction Force | $R_i = \sum_j K_{ij} u_j - F_i$ | N | From constrained rows |
| 9 | Consistency Check (Equilibrium) | $\sum R_i + \sum F_{applied} = 0$ | N | Must verify |
| 10 | Spring Stiffness Analogy | $k_{bar} = \frac{AE}{L}$ | N/m | Treats bar as axial spring |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** $E$ in GPa must be multiplied by $10^9$, $A$ in mm² must be multiplied by $10^{-6}$, and $L$ in m is used directly. Forgetting this conversion is the **#1 mistake** in KTU FEA numerical problems. The final stiffness $k^e$ should be in **N/m**, and final stresses in **Pa** (or MPa = $10^6$ Pa).

## Real-World Engineering Utility

The stepped bar problem directly models:
- **Bolted joints** in pressure vessels (sections of different cross-sections)
- **Tapered structural columns** under axial load
- **Reinforcing bars** in concrete (composite axial stiffness)
- **Telescopic hydraulic cylinders** in heavy machinery
- **Drive shafts** with stepped diameters for transmission systems

Mastering this simple example builds the foundation for **2D truss analysis, plane stress/strain, and 3D solid mechanics** in higher semesters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Exhaustive Worked Example: Stepped Bar (The KTU Favorite)

### Problem Statement

> A stepped bar is fixed at Node 1 and loaded with an axial force **P = 10 kN** at Node 3, as shown. The bar consists of two segments:
> - **Element 1:** $L_1 = 0.1$ m, $A_1 = 100$ mm², $E = 200$ GPa
> - **Element 2:** $L_2 = 0.1$ m, $A_2 = 200$ mm², $E = 200$ GPa
>
> **Find:** (a) Nodal displacements $u_2$ and $u_3$, (b) Stresses in both elements, (c) Reaction force at Node 1.

**Schematic:** Node 1 (Fixed) → Element 1 → Node 2 → Element 2 → Node 3 (Free, Load P = 10 kN)

### Step 1: Unit Conversion (to SI)

$$A_1 = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = 200 \text{ mm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$$

### Step 2: Element Stiffness Matrices

**For Element 1** (between nodes 1 and 2):

$$k_1 = \frac{A_1 E}{L_1} = \frac{(10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{11})}{0.1} = \frac{2 \times 10^7}{0.1} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

$$[k^{(1)}] = 2 \times 10^8 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \times 10^8 & -2 \times 10^8 \\ -2 \times 10^8 & 2 \times 10^8 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

**For Element 2** (between nodes 2 and 3):

$$k_2 = \frac{A_2 E}{L_2} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{11})}{0.1} = \frac{4 \times 10^7}{0.1} = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

$$[k^{(2)}] = 4 \times 10^8 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \times 10^8 & -4 \times 10^8 \\ -4 \times 10^8 & 4 \times 10^8 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

### Step 3: Global Stiffness Matrix Assembly (Direct Stiffness Method)

The global system has **3 nodes** and **3 DOFs** ($u_1, u_2, u_3$). The global matrix $K$ is **3×3**, initialized to zero. We add element contributions based on their global node numbers.

**Element 1** connects nodes (1, 2) — its $2 \times 2$ matrix maps to global positions (1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2):

**Element 2** connects nodes (2, 3) — its $2 \times 2$ matrix maps to global positions (2,2), (2,3), (3,2), (3,3):

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 & 0 \\ -k_1 & k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting values:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \times 10^8 & -2 \times 10^8 & 0 \\ -2 \times 10^8 & 6 \times 10^8 & -4 \times 10^8 \\ 0 & -4 \times 10^8 & 4 \times 10^8 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

**Verification of $K_{22}$:** Element 1 contributes $+k_1 = 2 \times 10^8$, Element 2 contributes $+k_2 = 4 \times 10^8$. Sum = $6 \times 10^8$ ✓

### Step 4: Global Force Vector and Boundary Conditions

The applied loads and reactions at each node:

$$\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} R_1 \\ 0 \\ 10000 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ N}$$

**Boundary condition:** Node 1 is fixed, so $u_1 = 0$.

The global system $K\{U\} = \{F\}$ becomes:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 \times 10^8 & -2 \times 10^8 & 0 \\ -2 \times 10^8 & 6 \times 10^8 & -4 \times 10^8 \\ 0 & -4 \times 10^8 & 4 \times 10^8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} R_1 \\ 0 \\ 10000 \end{Bmatrix}$$

### Step 5: Apply Boundary Condition (Elimination Method)

Setting $u_1 = 0$ eliminates the first row and column. The **reduced system** for unknowns $u_2$ and $u_3$ is:

$$\begin{aligned} 6 \times 10^8 \cdot u_2 - 4 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 &= 0 \quad \text{(Equation A)} \\ -4 \times 10^8 \cdot u_2 + 4 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 &= 10000 \quad \text{(Equation B)} \end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Solve the Reduced System

**From Equation A:**

$$6 \times 10^8 \cdot u_2 = 4 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3$$

$$u_2 = \frac{4 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^8} \cdot u_3 = \frac{2}{3} u_3$$

**Substitute into Equation B:**

$$-4 \times 10^8 \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3} u_3\right) + 4 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 = 10000$$

$$-\frac{8}{3} \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 + 4 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 = 10000$$

$$\left(4 - \frac{8}{3}\right) \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 = 10000$$

$$\left(\frac{12 - 8}{3}\right) \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 = 10000$$

$$\frac{4}{3} \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 = 10000$$

$$u_3 = \frac{10000 \times 3}{4 \times 10^8} = \frac{3 \times 10^4}{4 \times 10^8} = 0.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{u_3 = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.075 \text{ mm}}$$

**Back-substitute to find $u_2$:**

$$u_2 = \frac{2}{3} \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5} = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{u_2 = 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.050 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 7: Compute Element Stresses (Post-Processing)

**Stress in Element 1** (between nodes 1 and 2):

$$\sigma_1 = E \cdot \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L_1} = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-5}) - 0}{0.1}$$

$$\sigma_1 = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot (5.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 100 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}}$$

**Stress in Element 2** (between nodes 2 and 3):

$$\sigma_2 = E \cdot \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot \frac{(7.5 \times 10^{-5}) - (5.0 \times 10^{-5})}{0.1}$$

$$\sigma_2 = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 5.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_2 = 50 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}}$$

### Step 8: Compute Reaction Force at Node 1

Using the first row of the original (un-reduced) equilibrium equation:

$$R_1 = 2 \times 10^8 \cdot u_1 - 2 \times 10^8 \cdot u_2 + 0 \cdot u_3$$

$$R_1 = 0 - 2 \times 10^8 \cdot (5.0 \times 10^{-5}) + 0$$

$$R_1 = -1.0 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = -10 \text{ kN}$$

**The negative sign** indicates the reaction acts in the **negative x-direction** (opposing the applied load), as expected physically.

$$\boxed{R_1 = -10 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(i.e., 10 kN compressive reaction)}}$$

### Step 9: Global Equilibrium Check (Mandatory in KTU Exams!)

$$\sum F_x = R_1 + F_{applied} = -10 \text{ kN} + 10 \text{ kN} = 0 \checkmark$$

This validates the solution.

### Step 10: Cross-Verification Using Force Method

Total elongation under load P = 10 kN should equal $u_3$ (since node 1 is fixed):

$$\delta_{total} = \frac{P L_1}{A_1 E} + \frac{P L_2}{A_2 E} = \frac{10000 \times 0.1}{10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{11}} + \frac{10000 \times 0.1}{2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\delta_{total} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^7} + \frac{1000}{4 \times 10^7} = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

This matches $u_3 = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m ✓ — **The FEA solution is verified.**

## Summary Table of Results

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $u_1$ | **0** | m (fixed) |
| $u_2$ | **5.0 × 10⁻⁵** | m (0.05 mm) |
| $u_3$ | **7.5 × 10⁻⁵** | m (0.075 mm) |
| $\sigma_1$ | **100** | MPa |
| $\sigma_2$ | **50** | MPa |
| $R_1$ | **−10,000** | N (−10 kN) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## A. Mermaid Flowchart — Complete FEA Solution Procedure for Simple Examples

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Problem Definition] --> B[Discretize Domain into Elements]
    B --> C[Number Nodes 1 to N]
    C --> D[Identify E, A, L for Each Element]
    D --> E["Compute k^e = AE/L x Matrix 1,-1;-1,1"]
    E --> F[Map Element DOFs to Global DOFs]
    F --> G[Assemble Global K Matrix using DSM]
    G --> H[Form Global Force Vector F]
    H --> I[Apply Boundary Conditions: u_fixed = 0]
    I --> J[Reduce System: Remove Fixed Rows/Cols]
    J --> K["Solve K_red x U_red = F_red"]
    K --> L[Back-substitute to Find All u_i]
    L --> M[Compute Element Stresses sigma = E x du/L]
    M --> N[Compute Support Reactions R_i]
    N --> O[Verify Global Equilibrium Sum F = 0]
    O --> P[END: Final Results]
```

## B. Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Flow in Stepped Bar FEA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Input Data]
        A1[E1: L1, A1, E]
        A2[E2: L2, A2, E]
        A3[BC: u1 = 0]
        A4[Load: P = 10 kN at Node 3]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS [Computation]
        B1[k1 Matrix 2x2]
        B2[k2 Matrix 2x2]
        C[Global K Matrix 3x3]
        D[Reduced System 2x2]
        E[Linear Solver]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [Results]
        F1[u2 = 0.050 mm]
        F2[u3 = 0.075 mm]
        F3[sigma1 = 100 MPa]
        F4[sigma2 = 50 MPa]
        F5[R1 = -10 kN]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    A3 --> D
    A4 --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F1
    E --> F2
    E --> F3
    E --> F4
    E --> F5
```

## C. Mermaid Schematic — Stepped Bar Discretization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node 1: Fixed, u1=0] -->|Element 1: L1=0.1m, A1=100mm2| N2[Node 2: Unknown u2]
    N2 -->|Element 2: L2=0.1m, A2=200mm2| N3[Node 3: P=10kN, u3 unknown]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The diagram shows the 3-node, 2-element discretization. Each arrow represents a finite element with its own geometric and material properties. Node 1 is the boundary (fixed), Node 3 is the loaded end, and Node 2 is the internal junction whose displacement must be solved.

## D. Assembly Visualization (How Element Matrices Build Global K)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Element 1 Matrix 2x2] -->|Maps to global rows 1,2| K11[K11 gets k1]
    M1 -->|Maps to global rows 1,2| K12[K12 gets -k1]
    M1 -->|Maps to global rows 1,2| K21[K21 gets -k1]
    M1 -->|Maps to global rows 1,2| K22a[K22 gets k1]
    M2[Element 2 Matrix 2x2] -->|Maps to global rows 2,3| K22b[K22 also gets k2]
    M2 -->|Maps to global rows 2,3| K23[K23 gets -k2]
    M2 -->|Maps to global rows 2,3| K32[K32 gets -k2]
    M2 -->|Maps to global rows 2,3| K33[K33 gets k2]
    K22a --> SUM[K22 = k1 + k2 = 6x10^8]
    K22b --> SUM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**For a 1D bar element of length L, cross-sectional area A, and Young's modulus E, derive the element stiffness matrix using the direct equilibrium method.**

**Model Answer:**

Consider a bar element with nodes $i$ and $j$ subjected to axial forces $f_i$ and $f_j$ producing displacements $u_i$ and $u_j$.

The axial deformation of the element is $(u_j - u_i)$. By Hooke's law, the internal force is:

$$F_{internal} = \frac{AE}{L}(u_j - u_i)$$

Equilibrium at each node gives:

$$f_i = \frac{AE}{L}(u_i - u_j), \quad f_j = \frac{AE}{L}(u_j - u_i)$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} f_i \\ f_j \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ u_j \end{Bmatrix}$$

Therefore, the element stiffness matrix is:

$$[k^e] = \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix is **symmetric** and **singular** (determinant = 0), reflecting that the element cannot undergo rigid body motion analysis in isolation — it requires boundary conditions.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the Direct Stiffness Method (DSM) for assembly of the global stiffness matrix in a 1D FEA problem.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Direct Stiffness Method (DSM)** assembles the global stiffness matrix $K$ by **superimposing** the element stiffness matrices $[k^e]$ in their correct global positions. The procedure is:

1. Initialize $K$ as a zero matrix of size $N_{DOF} \times N_{DOF}$ where $N_{DOF}$ is the total number of nodal DOFs.
2. For each element $e$ connecting global nodes $p$ and $q$:
   - Compute $[k^e]$ (a $2 \times 2$ matrix for a 1D bar element).
   - Map each entry $k^e_{ij}$ to the global position $K_{m,n}$ where $m$ and $n$ are the global node numbers corresponding to local nodes $i$ and $j$.
   - **Add** the value: $K_{m,n} \leftarrow K_{m,n} + k^e_{ij}$.
3. The diagonal entry $K_{ii}$ (where two elements share node $i$) receives contributions from **all elements meeting at node $i$**, while off-diagonal entries $K_{ij}$ (where $i \neq j$) are non-zero only when nodes $i$ and $j$ are **directly connected** by an element.

The assembled $K$ is **symmetric**, **banded** (bandwidth depends on node numbering), and **positive semi-definite** before applying boundary conditions.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** For the stepped bar shown below, fixed at Node 1 and loaded with P = 20 kN at Node 3, determine the **nodal displacements** $u_2$ and $u_3$. Take E = 200 GPa for both segments.

**Data:**
- Element 1: $L_1 = 200$ mm, $A_1 = 400$ mm²
- Element 2: $L_2 = 300$ mm, $A_2 = 200$ mm²

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **stresses** in both elements and the **reaction force** at Node 1. Verify global equilibrium.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Displacements:**

Unit conversion:
- $L_1 = 0.2$ m, $A_1 = 400 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $L_2 = 0.3$ m, $A_2 = 200 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m²

Element stiffnesses:

$$k_1 = \frac{A_1 E}{L_1} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{11})}{0.2} = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

$$k_2 = \frac{A_2 E}{L_2} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{11})}{0.3} = 1.333 \times 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

Global stiffness matrix (3 DOFs):

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \times 10^8 & -4 \times 10^8 & 0 \\ -4 \times 10^8 & 5.333 \times 10^8 & -1.333 \times 10^8 \\ 0 & -1.333 \times 10^8 & 1.333 \times 10^8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Force vector: $\{F\} = \{R_1, 0, 20000\}^T$. Apply $u_1 = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 5.333 \times 10^8 \cdot u_2 - 1.333 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 &= 0 \\ -1.333 \times 10^8 \cdot u_2 + 1.333 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 &= 20000 \end{aligned}$$

From equation 1: $u_2 = 0.25 \cdot u_3$.

Substitute: $-1.333 \times 10^8 \times 0.25 u_3 + 1.333 \times 10^8 u_3 = 20000$

$$0.75 \times 1.333 \times 10^8 \cdot u_3 = 20000$$

$$u_3 = \frac{20000}{10^8} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$u_2 = 0.25 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Stresses and Reaction:**

$$\sigma_1 = E \cdot \frac{u_2 - u_1}{L_1} = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.2} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = E \cdot \frac{u_3 - u_2}{L_2} = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot \frac{(2 \times 10^{-4} - 5 \times 10^{-5})}{0.3} = (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} = 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

Reaction at Node 1: $R_1 = k_1 (u_1 - u_2) = 4 \times 10^8 \times (-5 \times 10^{-5}) = -20000$ N $= -20$ kN

**Equilibrium check:** $R_1 + P = -20 + 20 = 0$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Two springs are connected in series between two rigid walls. Spring 1 has stiffness $k_1 = 1000$ N/m and Spring 2 has stiffness $k_2 = 2000$ N/m. A force F = 600 N is applied at the junction node 2. Model each spring as a 1D bar/axial element.**

**(a)** Assemble the **global stiffness matrix** and **solve for the displacement** of the loaded node and the wall-junction node displacements.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **internal forces** in both springs and verify the **compatibility of displacements** (junction displacement = sum of individual spring elongations).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Assembly and Solution:**

3 nodes total. Nodes 1 and 3 are walls (fixed), Node 2 is the loaded junction.

Element stiffnesses:
- $k_1 = 1000$ N/m (between nodes 1 and 2)
- $k_2 = 2000$ N/m (between nodes 2 and 3)

Global stiffness matrix (3×3):

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & -1000 & 0 \\ -1000 & 3000 & -2000 \\ 0 & -2000 & 2000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Force vector: $\{F\} = \{R_1, 600, R_3\}^T$. Apply $u_1 = u_3 = 0$.

The reduced system is a single equation (only $u_2$ is unknown):

$$K_{22} \cdot u_2 = F_2$$

$$3000 \cdot u_2 = 600$$

$$\boxed{u_2 = 0.2 \text{ m}}$$

**(b) Internal Forces and Compatibility:**

Force in Spring 1: $F_1 = k_1 (u_2 - u_1) = 1000 \times 0.2 = 200$ N

Force in Spring 2: $F_2 = k_2 (u_3 - u_2) = 2000 \times (-0.2) = -400$ N

The negative sign indicates the spring is in **compression** (it's being pushed from the wall side).

**Compatibility check:** Individual elongations:
- $\delta_1 = F_1/k_1 = 200/1000 = 0.2$ m
- $\delta_2 = F_2/k_2 = -400/2000 = -0.2$ m
- Net displacement of node 2: $\delta_1 = 0.2$ m ✓ (matches $u_2$)

**Reactions:** $R_1 = -200$ N, $R_3 = +400$ N. Check: $R_1 + R_3 + F = -200 + 400 + 600$... wait, applied force is +600 N. Sum = $-200 + 400 + 600 = 800$ N. **Error — recheck:**

The applied force is at node 2: $F_2 = 600$ N. Reactions: $R_1 + R_3 + 600 = 0$ → $R_1 + R_3 = -600$ N.

From $K$ rows: $R_1 = 1000 u_1 - 1000 u_2 = 0 - 200 = -200$ N. $R_3 = -2000 u_2 + 2000 u_3 = -400 + 0 = -400$ N.

Sum: $-200 + (-400) + 600 = 0$ ✓ Equilibrium satisfied.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Simple Examples" Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing GPa with mm² and m without proper conversion. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Skipping Element Stiffness Matrices (–2 marks):** Jumping directly to the global matrix. KTU examiners want to see the individual $[k^{(1)}]$ and $[k^{(2)}]$ matrices.
> 3. **Wrong Sign Convention for Reactions (–1 mark):** Reaction direction must be consistent with the assumed positive coordinate axis. A negative reaction is **not an error** — it means the actual direction is opposite to the assumed one.
> 4. **Missing Equilibrium Check (–1 mark):** Always verify $\sum F = 0$ at the end. This is a free 1-mark bonus.
> 5. **Confusing Stress Formula:** Use $\sigma = E(u_j - u_i)/L$ (with sign), not $\sigma = E \cdot |u_j - u_i|/L$ (which loses sign information).
> 6. **Bandwidth Ignorance:** Always number nodes sequentially along the bar to minimize bandwidth. KTU problems often test this implicitly by choosing "inefficient" numbering and asking you to optimize.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Simple Examples** in FEA Module 2 = **1D bar/spring problems** solved using the **Direct Stiffness Method (DSM)**.
- The **element stiffness matrix** for a 1D bar is $k^e = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1\\ -1 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ — **memorize this**.
- **Global assembly rule:** $K_{ij}$ accumulates contributions from **all elements connecting global nodes $i$ and $j$**.
- **Boundary conditions:** For fixed nodes, set displacement = 0 and eliminate the corresponding row/column.
- **Stress computation:** $\sigma^e = \frac{E}{L}(u_j - u_i)$ — this is the **post-processing step**.
- **Reaction force:** Computed from the constrained rows of the original (un-reduced) equilibrium equation.
- **Always verify:** $\sum R_i + \sum F_{applied} = 0$ (global equilibrium).
- **Always convert units** to SI: $E$ in Pa, $A$ in m², $L$ in m, $F$ in N, $u$ in m, $\sigma$ in Pa.
- **Standard E for steel** in KTU problems = **200 GPa = 2×10¹¹ N/m²**.
- The "simple" examples in KTU are **never trivial in evaluation** — they test the **complete workflow** (discretization → assembly → BCs → solve → post-process).
- **Internal choice** in Part B means you can choose between stepped-bar (Question A) or spring-assembly/truss (Question B) — practice **both types**.
- The FEA result should **match the analytical (strength-of-materials) solution** — always cross-check as a validation step.
- **Bandwidth minimization** is implicit in 1D problems when nodes are numbered sequentially; for 2D, it requires careful numbering strategy.
- The **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy** and **Galerkin Method** give the same element stiffness matrix — examiners may ask for either derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Shape Function # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shape Functions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)** > *Shape functions* are a set of interpolation (or basis) functions $N_i$ used in the Finite Element Method (FEM) to approximate the spatial variation of an unknown field variable $u(x)$ across an element as a weighted sum of the nodal values. For an element with $n$ nodes, the field variable is approximated as: > > $$u(x) \approx \hat{u}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) \, u_i$$ > > where $u_i$ are the nodal unknowns and $N_i(x)$ are the **shape functions** associated with node $i$. Mathematically, the shape function $N_i$ satisfies the **Kronecker delta property** at every node $j$ of the element: $$N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i = j \\ 0 & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$$ They also satisfy the **partition of unity** condition: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) = 1 \quad \forall \, x \in \Omega_e$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Volume Knob" of a Sound System** > Imagine a 3-node long rope stretched between two walls with a person pulling at the middle node. The displacement at any point along the rope is not uniform — it smoothly transitions from **0 at the left wall**, to **maximum at the middle**, and back to **0 at the right wall**. The shape function for each node acts like a "weighting dial" that tells you: *"How much does the value at this particular node contribute to the displacement at this point?"* > > - At the **left node** itself → the left shape function $N_1 = 1$, others $= 0$. > - At the **middle of the element** → the middle shape function $N_2$ is largest. > - Far from a node → its shape function smoothly fades to **0**. Another analogy: **building a digital picture with LEGO bricks**. Each brick (shape function) is anchored to a pixel (node). Together, they reconstruct a smooth, continuous image (the field variable) from discrete pixel values. ## 1.3 Why Are Shape Functions the "Heart" of FEM? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — Why Shape Functions Matter** > The choice of shape function determines the **accuracy**, **convergence**, and **computational cost** of the FEM solution: > > | Polynomial Order | Element Type | Convergence | Cost | > |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| > | **Linear (1st order)** | 2-node bar, CST triangle | Monotonic, slow | Low | > | **Quadratic (2nd order)** | 3-node bar, LST (6-noded triangle) | Faster | Moderate | > | **Cubic / Higher** | 4-node bar, 8/9-noded quadrilateral | Very fast | High | A higher-order shape function gives a more accurate field approximation, but at the cost of more DOFs and a larger stiffness matrix. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation The shape function approximation can be viewed as projecting a continuous field $u(x)$ onto a **finite-dimensional polynomial subspace** $P^k$ of degree $\le k$. The general polynomial form for a 1D element of order $k$ is: $$\hat{u}(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_k x^k$$ with $k+1$ unknown coefficients. Since these coefficients must be solved for, we rewrite the polynomial in **Lagrange (nodal) form**: $$\hat{u}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) \, u_i, \quad n = k+1$$ This reparametrization converts the unknown *coefficients* $a_j$ into the unknown *nodal values* $u_i$ — which is far more convenient because nodal values have **direct physical meaning**. ## 2.2 Properties of Shape Functions — The Six Cardinal Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Properties (High-Yield for KTU Boards)** > > 1. **Kronecker Delta Property:** $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ — each shape function equals **1 at its own node** and **0 at all other nodes**. > 2. **Partition of Unity:** $\sum N_i = 1$ — essential for representing rigid-body translation. > 3. **Polynomial Completeness:** Together they must exactly reproduce polynomials of order up to the element's order (a linear element must exactly reproduce constants and linear fields). > 4. **Continuity:** $C^0$ continuity is mandatory across element boundaries; for beams/shells, $C^1$ continuity is required (Hermite shape functions). > 5. **Non-negativity inside the element:** $N_i \ge 0$ (ensures bounded interpolation). > 6. **Compact Support:** $N_i \neq 0$ only inside elements adjacent to node $i$ — yields sparse global matrices. ## 2.3 Generalized Coordinate Transformation (Isoparametric Mapping) A shape function in **natural (parent) coordinates** $\xi$ is mapped to a **global coordinate** $x$ via: $$x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi) \, x_i \quad \text{(coordinate mapping)}$$ $$u(\xi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi) \, u_i \quad \text{(field approximation)}$$ This is called the **isoparametric formulation** because the same shape functions are used for both geometry and field variables. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Table — Master These for Board Exams** | Element Type | Nodes ($n$) | Shape Function Formula | Domain | |:---|:---:|:---|:---:| | **1D Linear Bar** | 2 | $N_1 = 1-\xi, \quad N_2 = \xi$ | $\xi \in [0, 1]$ | | **1D Quadratic Bar** | 3 | $N_1 = 2(\xi-0.5)(\xi-1)$
$N_2 = 4\xi(1-\xi)$
$N_3 = 2\xi(\xi-0.5)$ | $\xi \in [0, 1]$ | | **1D Cubic Bar** | 4 | $N_1 = \dfrac{(1-\xi)(1-2\xi)(1+2\xi+\xi)}{-6}$ (and similar cyclic) | $\xi \in [-1, 1]$ | | **2D Linear Triangle (CST)** | 3 | $N_i = L_i = A_i / A$ (area coordinates) | $\xi, \eta$ | | **2D Linear Quadrilateral (Q4)** | 4 | $N_i = \dfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi\xi_i)(1+\eta\eta_i)$ | $\xi, \eta \in [-1,1]$ | | **2D Quad Serendipity (Q8)** | 8 | Mixed corner + mid-side terms | $\xi, \eta \in [-1,1]$ | | **2D Lagrangian (Q9)** | 9 | $N_i = L_i(\xi)L_j(\eta)$ tensor product | $\xi, \eta \in [-1,1]$ | **Lagrange Polynomial Form (generalized 1D form for $n$ nodes):** $$N_i(\xi) = \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} \frac{\xi - \xi_j}{\xi_i - \xi_j}$$ ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Are Shape Functions Used? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Applications** > - **Structural Mechanics:** Displacement field in beams, plates, and 3D solids. > - **Heat Transfer:** Temperature distribution $T(x,y,z)$ across a discretized body. > - **Fluid Dynamics:** Velocity and pressure fields in CFD (Navier–Stokes). > - **Electromagnetics:** Scalar/vector potential fields in antennas, waveguides. > - **Biomechanics:** Stress analysis of bones, implants, and soft tissues. > > The choice of shape function order directly influences the **rate of convergence** of the FEM solution to the true analytical solution (per the **Céa Lemma** and the **Patch Test**). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Linear Shape Function (2-Node Bar Element) **Problem Setup:** Approximate a 1D field $u(x)$ over an element spanning $[x_1, x_2]$ using a linear polynomial. **Step 1: Assume the trial function.** $$\hat{u}(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$$ This is linear in $x$ with **2 unknown constants** $a_0$ and $a_1$, matching the 2 nodes. **Step 2: Enforce nodal boundary conditions.** At node 1 ($x = x_1$): $\hat{u}(x_1) = u_1$ At node 2 ($x = x_2$): $\hat{u}(x_2) = u_2$ $$a_0 + a_1 x_1 = u_1$$ $$a_0 + a_1 x_2 = u_2$$ **Step 3: Solve the 2×2 linear system for $a_0$ and $a_1$.** Subtracting the first equation from the second: $$a_1 (x_2 - x_1) = u_2 - u_1$$ $$a_1 = \frac{u_2 - u_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$ Substituting back: $$a_0 = u_1 - a_1 x_1 = u_1 - \frac{u_2 - u_1}{x_2 - x_1} x_1 = \frac{u_1 x_2 - u_2 x_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$ **Step 4: Substitute $a_0, a_1$ back into $\hat{u}(x)$ and group by nodal values.** $$\hat{u}(x) = a_0 + a_1 x = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1} u_1 + \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} u_2$$ **Step 5: Identify the shape functions.** $$\boxed{N_1(x) = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}}$$ **Step 6: Verify the properties.** At $x = x_1$: $N_1 = 1, N_2 = 0$ ✓ (Kronecker delta) At $x = x_2$: $N_1 = 0, N_2 = 1$ ✓ (Kronecker delta) Sum: $N_1 + N_2 = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = 1$ ✓ (Partition of unity) **Step 7: Convert to natural (parent) coordinates $\xi \in [0, 1]$ using the isoparametric map $x = x_1 + (x_2-x_1)\xi$.** $$\xi = \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}, \quad 1 - \xi = \frac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}$$ $$\boxed{N_1(\xi) = 1 - \xi, \quad N_2(\xi) = \xi}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Quadratic Shape Function (3-Node Bar) — Lagrange Interpolation Method **Step 1: With 3 nodes, the polynomial must be quadratic.** $$\hat{u}(\xi) = a_0 + a_1 \xi + a_2 \xi^2$$ **Step 2: Enforce nodal conditions at $\xi_1 = 0, \xi_2 = 0.5, \xi_3 = 1$.** $$\hat{u}(0) = u_1, \quad \hat{u}(0.5) = u_2, \quad \hat{u}(1) = u_3$$ **Step 3: Write the Lagrange interpolating polynomial directly.** For node $i$, the Lagrange basis polynomial is: $$N_i(\xi) = \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{3} \frac{\xi - \xi_j}{\xi_i - \xi_j}$$ **Step 4: Expand $N_1$ (with $\xi_1 = 0$).** $$N_1(\xi) = \frac{(\xi - 0.5)(\xi - 1)}{(0 - 0.5)(0 - 1)} = \frac{(\xi - 0.5)(\xi - 1)}{0.5}$$ $$N_1(\xi) = 2(\xi - 0.5)(\xi - 1) = 2\xi^2 - 3\xi + 1$$ **Step 5: Expand $N_2$ (with $\xi_2 = 0.5$).** $$N_2(\xi) = \frac{(\xi - 0)(\xi - 1)}{(0.5 - 0)(0.5 - 1)} = \frac{\xi(\xi - 1)}{-0.25}$$ $$N_2(\xi) = -4\xi(\xi - 1) = 4\xi(1 - \xi) = -4\xi^2 + 4\xi$$ **Step 6: Expand $N_3$ (with $\xi_3 = 1$).** $$N_3(\xi) = \frac{(\xi - 0)(\xi - 0.5)}{(1 - 0)(1 - 0.5)} = \frac{\xi(\xi - 0.5)}{0.5}$$ $$N_3(\xi) = 2\xi(\xi - 0.5) = 2\xi^2 - \xi$$ **Step 7: Compact form of the result.** $$\boxed{N_1 = 2\xi^2 - 3\xi + 1, \quad N_2 = -4\xi^2 + 4\xi, \quad N_3 = 2\xi^2 - \xi}$$ **Step 8: Verify all properties at the three nodes.** | $\xi$ | $N_1$ | $N_2$ | $N_3$ | Sum | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | **1** | 0 | 0 | 1 | | **0.5** | 0 | **1** | 0 | 1 | | **1** | 0 | 0 | **1** | 1 | All three Kronecker delta conditions ✓ and partition of unity ✓ are satisfied. ## 3.3 Derivation of Hermite Shape Function (Beam Element — $C^1$ Continuity) For Euler–Bernoulli beams, the field variable $w$ (transverse displacement) requires $C^1$ continuity, so **both the displacement AND its slope** are interpolated. Using Hermite cubics: $$w(\xi) = N_1 w_1 + N_2 \theta_1 + N_3 w_2 + N_4 \theta_2$$ where $\theta_i = \left(\dfrac{dw}{dx}\right)_i$ and the shape functions are: $$N_1(\xi) = 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3, \quad N_2(\xi) = L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3)$$ $$N_3(\xi) = 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3, \quad N_4(\xi) = L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)$$ with $\xi \in [0,1]$ and $L$ being the element length. The Hermite shape functions guarantee continuous slopes — essential for **bending elements** like beams, plates, and shells. ## 3.4 Shape Functions for 2D Linear Quadrilateral (Q4) For a 4-node quad with natural coordinates $\xi, \eta \in [-1, 1]$: $$N_i(\xi, \eta) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + \xi \xi_i)(1 + \eta \eta_i), \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$ where $(\xi_i, \eta_i)$ are the natural coordinates of the 4 corner nodes (e.g., $(-1,-1), (1,-1), (1,1), (-1,1)$). These are obtained as the **tensor product** of 1D linear shape functions: $$N_i(\xi, \eta) = L_i(\xi) \cdot L_i(\eta)$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Shape Function Behaviour for a 1D Linear Element ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PARENT["Parent Domain 0 to 1"] A1[xi equals 0]:::node1 A2[xi equals 1]:::node2 end subgraph SHAPE1["Shape Function N1 equals 1 minus xi"] S1A[Value equals 1 at xi equals 0]:::high S1B[Value equals 0 at xi equals 1]:::low end subgraph SHAPE2["Shape Function N2 equals xi"] S2A[Value equals 0 at xi equals 0]:::low S2B[Value equals 1 at xi equals 1]:::high end A1 --> SHAPE1 A2 --> SHAPE2 S1A --> S1B S2A --> S2B classDef node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef high fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef low fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Shape Function Behaviour for a 1D Quadratic (3-Node) Element ```mermaid graph LR subgraph NODES["Three Nodes in Parent Domain"] N1[Node 1 at xi equals 0]:::corner N2[Node 2 at xi equals 0.5]:::mid N3[Node 3 at xi equals 1]:::corner end subgraph N1F["N1 equals 2 xi squared minus 3 xi plus 1"] N1H[Equals 1 at Node 1]:::peak N1M[Equals 0 at Node 2]:::zero N1E[Equals 0 at Node 3]:::zero end subgraph N2F["N2 equals minus 4 xi squared plus 4 xi"] N2H[Equals 0 at Node 1]:::zero N2M[Equals 1 at Node 2]:::peak N2E[Equals 0 at Node 3]:::zero end subgraph N3F["N3 equals 2 xi squared minus xi"] N3H[Equals 0 at Node 1]:::zero N3M[Equals 0 at Node 2]:::zero N3E[Equals 1 at Node 3]:::peak end N1 --> N1F N2 --> N2F N3 --> N3F classDef corner fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef peak fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zero fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Shape Function Selection in FEA Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Problem Definition]:::start --> B{Choose Element Type}:::decision B -->|Truss or 1D Bar| C[1D Linear 2-Node]:::linear B -->|Higher Accuracy 1D| D[1D Quadratic 3-Node]:::quad B -->|Beam or Plate| E[Hermite Cubic 4-Node]:::hermite B -->|2D Plane Stress or Strain| F[2D CST or Q4]:::cst B -->|2D Curved Boundary| G[2D LST or Q8 or Q9]:::lst C --> H[Generate Lagrange Shape Functions N_i xi]:::gen D --> H E --> I[Generate Hermite Shape Functions N_i and dN_i]:::gen F --> J[Generate Area Coordinates or Tensor Product]:::gen G --> J H --> K[Verify Kronecker Delta Property]:::verify I --> K J --> K K --> L[Verify Partition of Unity]:::verify L --> M[Map to Global Coordinates x via Jacobian]:::map M --> N[Form Stiffness Matrix and Load Vector]:::assemble N --> O[Apply Boundary Conditions]:::bc O --> P[Solve and Post-Process]:::solve classDef start fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef linear fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef quad fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef hermite fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cst fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef lst fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gen fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef verify fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef map fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef assemble fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bc fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef solve fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Isoparametric Mapping of a Distorted Quad Element ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PARENT["Parent Element Reference Square"] P1[Corner minus 1 minus 1]:::pnode P2[Corner plus 1 minus 1]:::pnode P3[Corner plus 1 plus 1]:::pnode P4[Corner minus 1 plus 1]:::pnode end subgraph MAP["Isoparametric Mapping N_i xi eta times x_i y_i"] M1[Mapping Function x equals sum N_i x_i]:::map M2[Mapping Function y equals sum N_i y_i]:::map end subgraph GLOBAL["Global Element Distorted Quadrilateral"] G1[Node 1 at x1 y1]:::gnode G2[Node 2 at x2 y2]:::gnode G3[Node 3 at x3 y3]:::gnode G4[Node 4 at x4 y4]:::gnode end PARENT --> MAP MAP --> GLOBAL classDef pnode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef map fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** ### Q1. Define shape functions. State any four important properties of shape functions used in FEM. [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** A *shape function* $N_i$ is an interpolation function used to approximate the variation of a field variable across a finite element as a weighted sum of the nodal values: $$u(x) \approx \hat{u}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x) \, u_i$$ **Four important properties:** 1. **Kronecker delta property:** $N_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}$ — the function is unity at its own node and zero at all other nodes. 2. **Partition of unity:** $\sum N_i(x) = 1$ over the element. 3. **Polynomial completeness:** Together they reproduce polynomials of order up to the element's degree. 4. **Compact support:** $N_i \neq 0$ only within elements adjacent to node $i$, ensuring sparsity of the global stiffness matrix. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** ### Q2. Differentiate between Lagrange and Hermite shape functions. [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** | Feature | Lagrange Shape Functions | Hermite Shape Functions | |:---|:---|:---| | **Continuity** | $C^0$ (function continuity only) | $C^1$ (function + slope continuity) | | **Nodal DOFs** | Function values $u_i$ only | Function values $u_i$ **and** derivatives $u_i'$ | | **Typical Use** | Truss, plane stress, heat transfer | Beams, plates, shells (bending problems) | | **Example** | 2-node linear: $N_1 = 1-\xi, N_2 = \xi$ | 4-node cubic beam Hermite | | **Polynomial Degree** | Lower for the same number of nodes | Higher (cubic for beams) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]** ### Question A — 14 Marks **(a)** For a two-noded bar element of length $L$, derive the linear shape functions in natural coordinates. Show that they satisfy the Kronecker delta property and the partition of unity. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A one-dimensional quadratic element has three nodes located at $\xi_1 = 0$, $\xi_2 = 0.5$, and $\xi_3 = 1$. Using the Lagrange interpolation approach, derive the three shape functions $N_1(\xi)$, $N_2(\xi)$, and $N_3(\xi)$. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1: Assume a linear polynomial field over the element of length $L$.** $$\hat{u}(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$$ **Step 2: Apply the isoparametric map** $x = x_1 + \xi L$, so that $\xi = (x - x_1)/L$ with $\xi \in [0, 1]$.
