Differentiate between **Plane Stress** and **Plane Strain** conditions. Give the corresponding [D] matrices.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Plane Stress** | **Plane Strain** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Stresses normal to the plane are zero ($\sigma_z = 0$) | Strains normal to the plane are zero ($\varepsilon_z = 0$) |
| **Geometry** | Thin plates loaded in-plane | Long bodies with constrained ends |
| **Example** | Sheet metal bracket | Concrete dam, tunnel lining |

$$
[D]_{PS} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
[D]_{PE} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 1 for examples, 1 for matrices)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix $[k^e]$ for a **Constant Strain Triangular (CST) element** used in 2D plane stress analysis, starting from the **Total Potential Energy functional**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CST element with nodes at **(0, 0), (4, 0), (2, 3)**, thickness **t = 10 mm**, E = **200 GPa**, ν = **0.3**, determine the [B] matrix and the element stiffness matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Step 1 — Total Potential Energy for a Single Element:**

$$
\Pi^e = \frac{1}{2} \int_{A^e} \{\sigma\}^T \{\varepsilon\} \, t \, dA - \int_{A^e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, t \, dA
$$

**Step 2 — Approximated Fields:**

Displacement: $\{u\} = [N]\{d^e\}$
Strain: $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{d^e\}$
Stress: $\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\} = [D][B]\{d^e\}$

**Step 3 — Substituting into Π:**

$$
\Pi^e = \frac{1}{2} \{d^e\}^T \left( \int_{A^e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, t \, dA \right) \{d^e\} - \{d^e\}^T \left( \int_{A^e} [N]^T \{b\} \, t \, dA \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Stationarity Condition:**

$$
\frac{\partial \Pi^e}{\partial \{d^e\}} = [k^e]\{d^e\} - \{f^e\} = \{0\}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{[k^e] = \int_{A^e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, t \, dA}
$$

**Assumptions:**

1. Material is **linear, elastic, isotropic, and homogeneous**.
2. Displacement field is **linear** within each element (CST).
3. **Plane stress** conditions: $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$.
4. Body forces are **negligible** (or handled separately in $\{f^e\}$).
5. Thickness **t is constant** across the element.

*(7 marks: 1 for Π, 1 for substitutions, 1 for stationarity, 1 for final K, 1 for assumptions, 2 for derivation flow)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the Area:**

Given nodes: 1(0,0), 2(4,0), 3(2,3).

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 4 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} (1 \cdot 12 - 0 + 0) = 6 \text{ units}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Coefficients:**

$$
b_1 = y_2 - y_3 = 0 - 3 = -3, \quad c_1 = x_3 - x_2 = 2 - 4 = -2
$$

$$
b_2 = y_3 - y_1 = 3 - 0 = 3, \quad c_2 = x_1 - x_3 = 0 - 2 = -2
$$

$$
b_3 = y_1 - y_2 = 0 - 0 = 0, \quad c_3 = x_2 - x_1 = 4 - 0 = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Form the [B] Matrix (3×6):**

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{2 \times 6} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 & -2 & 0 & 4 \\ -2 & -3 & -2 & 3 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{12} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 & -2 & 0 & 4 \\ -2 & -3 & -2 & 3 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the [D] Matrix (Plane Stress, E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3):**

$$
\frac{E}{1-\nu^2} = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{1 - 0.09} = \frac{200000}{0.91} = 219780.22 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
[D] = 219780.22 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3 & 0 \\ 0.3 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.35 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Stiffness Matrix:**

Since [B] is constant for CST:

$$
[k^e] = [B]^T [D] [B] \cdot t \cdot A
$$

with t = 10 mm, A = 6 mm².

This yields a **6×6 symmetric matrix** with units of N/mm. The full numerical evaluation is done by matrix multiplication, giving the element stiffness matrix used for global assembly.

*(7 marks: 2 for coefficients, 2 for [B], 1 for [D], 2 for K setup and computation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the principle of minimum potential energy, derive the expression for the **equivalent nodal force vector** $\{f^e\}$ for a 2D element subjected to body forces and surface tractions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure to **assemble the global stiffness matrix [K] and global force vector {F}** from individual element contributions, highlighting the use of the **connectivity (incidence) matrix** and the principle of **compatibility at shared nodes**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Step 1 — Work Done by External Forces (for a Single Element):**

$$
W^e = \int_{V^e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, dV + \int_{S^e} \{u\}^T \{T\} \, dS
$$

In 2D form (with dV = t dA):

$$
W^e = \int_{A^e} \{u\}^T \{b\} \, t \, dA + \int_{S_\sigma^e} \{u\}^T \{T\} \, t \, dS
$$

where $S_\sigma$ denotes the portion of the boundary where tractions are prescribed.

**Step 2 — Substitute the FE Displacement Approximation:**

$\{u\} = [N]\{d^e\}$

$$
W^e = \int_{A^e} (\{d^e\}^T [N]^T) \{b\} \, t \, dA + \int_{S_\sigma^e} (\{d^e\}^T [N]^T) \{T\} \, t \, dS
$$

**Step 3 — Extract Constant $\{d^e\}^T$ Outside the Integral:**

$$
W^e = \{d^e\}^T \left( \int_{A^e} [N]^T \{b\} \, t \, dA + \int_{S_\sigma^e} [N]^T \{T\} \, t \, dS \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Equivalent Nodal Force Vector:**

$$
\boxed{\{f^e\} = \int_{A^e} [N]^T \{b\} \, t \, dA + \int_{S_\sigma^e} [N]^T \{T\} \, t \, dS}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

The distributed body forces and surface tractions are "lumped" into equivalent concentrated forces at the nodes, weighted by the shape functions, so that they produce the same virtual work as the original distributed loads.

*(7 marks: 1 for W expression, 1 for substitution, 1 for rearrangement, 1 for final vector, 1 for physical meaning, 2 for derivation flow)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Local vs. Global DOFs:**

Each element e has its own **local DOF vector** $\{d^e\}$ (e.g., 6 DOFs for CST). However, the global equation is in terms of the **global DOF vector** $\{D\}$ (containing all nodal DOFs of the mesh).

**Step 2 — The Connectivity (Incidence) Matrix:**

A connectivity or **Boolean location (LM) matrix** maps local element DOFs to global DOFs. For example, if element 1 connects to global nodes 4, 7, and 9, then its local DOF 1 ($u_1$) maps to global DOF 7 ($U_4$ in x-direction) — the exact mapping depends on the chosen global node numbering.

**Step 3 — Element Stiffness Expansion to Global Size:**

The element matrix $[k^e]$ is "expanded" (using zeros) to global dimensions and placed into $[K]$ at the locations dictated by the connectivity.

**Step 4 — Assembly (Summation) Process:**

$$
[K] = \sum_{e=1}^{n} [k^e]_{expanded}, \qquad \{F\} = \sum_{e=1}^{n} \{f^e\}_{expanded}
$$

At a shared node between elements e₁ and e₂, the global equation automatically sums up the stiffness contributions:

$$
K_{ii}^{global} = \sum_{\text{elements sharing node } i} K_{ii}^e
$$

**Step 5 — Compatibility at Shared Nodes:**

For the assembled system to be valid, the **displacement field must be continuous** at every shared node. The FE shape functions ensure this: when two elements share a node, they are forced to have the **same nodal displacement** in the global system.

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions and Solve:**

Impose essential (Dirichlet) BCs by eliminating rows/columns or penalty method, then solve $[K]\{D\} = \{F\}$.

*(7 marks: 1 for local vs. global, 2 for connectivity, 2 for assembly, 1 for compatibility, 1 for BC and solve)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Plane Stress vs. Plane Strain [D] matrices** — Verify whether σz = 0 or εz = 0 is assumed before writing [D].
> 2. **Sign errors in CST coefficients** — Always re-verify $b_i = y_j - y_k$ and $c_i = x_k - x_j$ (cyclic).
> 3. **Forgetting the factor t × A** in $[k^e] = [B]^T[D][B] \cdot t \cdot A$ for CST (a common 1-mark loss).
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** (linear, elastic, isotropic) — Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 5. **Mixing local and global DOFs** during assembly — Be explicit about the connectivity/incidence matrix.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Total Potential Energy:** $\Pi = U - W$; minimum (stationary) at equilibrium.
- ✅ **Element stiffness:** $[k^e] = \int_{A^e} [B]^T[D][B] \, t \, dA$ — derived from $\partial \Pi / \partial \{d^e\} = 0$.
- ✅ **CST** uses **linear shape functions** → [B] is **constant** → integration reduces to a simple product $\times$ t × A.
- ✅ **Plane Stress [D]** uses $\dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}$; **Plane Strain [D]** uses $\dfrac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$.
- ✅ **Nodal force vector** = body force integral + traction integral, both weighted by $[N]^T$.
- ✅ **Global assembly** uses the **connectivity matrix** to place element contributions into [K] and {F}.
- ✅ **Compatibility** at shared nodes is automatic when shape functions are continuous (C¹ or C⁰ as required).
- ✅ **Boundary conditions:** Essential (displacement) modify [K] and {F}; Natural (traction) are included in {F}.
- ✅ **Units:** E in MPa or GPa; lengths in mm; t in mm → $[k^e]$ in N/mm; {F} in N.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions**: linear elastic, isotropic, homogeneous, small deformations, plane stress/strain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Element Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Element Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Element Matrices** are the discretized mathematical operators that govern the local (element-level) behavior of a structure within the Finite Element Method (FEM). For a structural element, the governing equilibrium equation is:
> 
> $$[k]\{d\} = \{f\}$$
> 
> where $[k]$ is the **Element Stiffness Matrix**, $\{d\}$ is the **Element Displacement Vector**, and $\{f\}$ is the **Element Nodal Force Vector**.

An element matrix encapsulates the intrinsic mechanical, thermal, or inertial properties of a small "piece" of the continuous domain. For dynamic problems, two additional matrices arise:

- **Element Mass Matrix** $[m]$ (inertial resistance to acceleration)
- **Element Damping Matrix** $[c]$ (energy dissipation)

The general element equilibrium equation in dynamics becomes:
$$[m]\{\ddot{d}\} + [c]\{\dot{d}\} + [k]\{d\} = \{f\}$$

### 1.2 Types of Element Matrices

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Element Matrices in FEA:**
> 
> 1. **Stiffness Matrix $[k]$** — Resistance to deformation (structural rigidity)
> 2. **Mass Matrix $[m]$** — Resistance to acceleration (inertia)
> 3. **Load Vector $\{f\}$** — External excitation applied at the element nodes
> 
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 3 focuses primarily on $[k]$ and $[m]$ for **1D bar, beam, and 2D CST elements**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Element Matrices as a "Smart LEGO Brick".** 
> 
> Imagine you are building a large bridge using LEGO blocks. Each LEGO brick is small, simple, and has a clearly defined connection pattern. By itself, one brick can do very little — but its *individual property sheet* (its stiffness, its weight, its maximum load) is **known exactly**. The engineer stamps this property onto a tiny "data card" attached to the brick.
> 
> In FEM, that **"data card"** is the element matrix! The element is the LEGO brick; the matrix tells you *exactly* how it deforms, how much force it can carry, and how it talks to its neighbors. The global matrix is then assembled by connecting all these "data cards" in the right order — just like snapping LEGO bricks together to form a bridge.

### 1.4 Physical Significance (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** 
> 
> - $[k]$ has units of **N/m** (force per unit displacement)
> - $[m]$ has units of **kg** (mass)
> - $\{f\}$ has units of **N** (force)
> 
> The determinant, eigenvalues, and symmetry of $[k]$ directly determine **buckling loads, natural frequencies, and stability** of the structure. Mastering element matrices is the gateway to **assembling, solving, and interpreting** any FEA problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The General Framework for Deriving Element Matrices

Every element matrix in FEA is derived from three fundamental building blocks:

> [!NOTE]
> **The FEM Trinity (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> 
> 1. **Shape Functions $[N]$** — interpolate field variables inside the element from nodal values
> 2. **Strain-Displacement Matrix $[B]$** — relates nodal displacements to internal strains
> 3. **Constitutive Matrix $[D]$** — relates stresses to strains (material law)

### 2.2 General Formulas for Element Matrices

| **Element Matrix** | **General Integral Form** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Stiffness $[k]$ | $\int_V [B]^T [D][B]\, dV$ | Resistance to deformation |
| Consistent Mass $[m]$ | $\int_V \rho [N]^T[N]\, dV$ | Inertia based on shape functions |
| Lumped Mass $[m_L]$ | Diagonal approximation of $[m]$ | Concentrated inertia at nodes |
| Load Vector $\{f\}$ | $\int_V [N]^T\{b\}\, dV + \int_S [N]^T\{t\}\, dS$ | Equivalent nodal loads |
| Geometric (Stress) Stiffness $[k_g]$ | $\int_V [G]^T [S][G]\, dV$ | Effect of initial stress |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the most-tested set of formulas for KTU Module 3. Memorize the structure, the multipliers, and the units.**

| **Element Type** | **Stiffness Matrix $[k]$** | **Consistent Mass $[m]$** |
|---|---|---|
| **1D Linear Bar** | $\dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\dfrac{\rho A L}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **1D Tapered Bar** | $\dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L A(x)E(x)\,dx \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\dfrac{\rho L}{6}\int_0^L A(x)\begin{bmatrix} 2(1-\tfrac{x}{L})^2 & \cdots \end{bmatrix}dx$ |
| **Euler-Bernoulli Beam** | $\dfrac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\dfrac{\rho A L}{420}\begin{bmatrix} 156 & 22L & 54 & -13L \\ 22L & 4L^2 & 13L & -3L^2 \\ 54 & 13L & 156 & -22L \\ -13L & -3L^2 & -22L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **2D Truss** | $\dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} c^2 & cs & -c^2 & -cs \\ cs & s^2 & -cs & -s^2 \\ -c^2 & -cs & c^2 & cs \\ -cs & -s^2 & cs & s^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\dfrac{\rho A L}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **2D CST (Plane Stress)** | $t \cdot A \cdot [B]^T[D][B]$ | $\dfrac{\rho A t}{12}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ |

**Where:**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area, $E$ = Young's modulus, $L$ = element length
- $I$ = second moment of area, $\rho$ = mass density
- $c = \cos\theta$, $s = \sin\theta$ (for inclined truss members)
- $t$ = thickness, $A$ = triangular element area (CST)
- $[D]$ = material property matrix (plane stress/plane strain)

### 2.4 Key Properties of Element Stiffness Matrices

> [!NOTE]
> **These properties are frequently asked as 3-mark questions in KTU exams.**

1. **Symmetry** — $[k] = [k]^T$ (follows from Betti's reciprocal theorem)
2. **Singularity** — Det$[k] = 0$ for a free element (rigid body modes)
3. **Singularity elimination** — Imposing boundary conditions makes $[k]$ non-singular
4. **Order** — For $n$ DOFs per element, $[k]$ is an $n \times n$ matrix
5. **Bandwidth** — Depends on nodal numbering (smaller bandwidth = efficient solve)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where are Element Matrices Used in Industry?**
> 
> - **Aerospace**: Stress analysis of aircraft wings, fuselage panels (CST elements)
> - **Civil Engineering**: Bridge analysis, high-rise buildings (beam/frame elements)
> - **Automotive**: Crash simulation, NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) — uses mass and stiffness matrices
> - **Biomedical**: Bone-implant analysis, stent design
> - **Mechanical**: Gear, shaft, and turbine blade analysis
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stiffness Matrix of a 2-Node Linear Bar Element

**Problem Statement:** Derive the element stiffness matrix $[k]$ for a 2-node bar element of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$.

**Step 1 — Define Nodal Displacements:**

The bar element has 2 nodes with axial displacements $u_1$ and $u_2$. The total displacement vector is:
$$\{d\} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Assume a Displacement Field (Shape Functions):**

For a 1D linear bar, we assume a linear variation of displacement:
$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$$

Applying boundary conditions at nodes (x = 0 and x = L):
- At $x = 0$: $u(0) = u_1$ → $a_0 = u_1$
- At $x = L$: $u(L) = u_2$ → $a_0 + a_1 L = u_2$ → $a_1 = (u_2 - u_1)/L$

Substituting back:
$$u(x) = u_1\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right) + u_2\left(\frac{x}{L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Express Using Shape Functions $[N]$:**

$$u(x) = \begin{bmatrix} N_1 & N_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

where the **linear shape functions** are:
$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Strain-Displacement Matrix $[B]$:**

Strain in the bar:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{dN_1}{dx}u_1 + \frac{dN_2}{dx}u_2 = -\frac{1}{L}u_1 + \frac{1}{L}u_2$$

Therefore:
$$\varepsilon = \begin{bmatrix} B \end{bmatrix} \{d\} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{[B] = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{L} & \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Constitutive Relation $[D]$:**

For 1D axial bar:
$$\sigma = E\varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad [D] = [E]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the General Stiffness Formula:**

$$[k] = \int_V [B]^T [D][B]\, dV = \int_0^L [B]^T E A [B]\, dx$$

Substituting $[B]$:
$$[k] = \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} -1/L \\ 1/L \end{bmatrix} E A \begin{bmatrix} -1/L & 1/L \end{bmatrix} dx$$

$$[k] = \frac{EA}{L^2} \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx = \frac{EA}{L^2} \cdot L \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{[k] = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 7 — Verification of Properties:**

- **Symmetry check:** $[k]_{12} = -AE/L = [k]_{21}$ ✓
- **Singularity check:** $\det[k] = (AE/L)^2 - (AE/L)^2 = 0$ ✓ (rigid body modes)
- **Units check:** $(m^2 \cdot N/m^2) / m = N/m$ ✓ (correct for stiffness)

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Consistent Mass Matrix of a Bar Element

**Step 1 — Apply the General Mass Formula:**

$$[m] = \int_V \rho [N]^T [N]\, dV = \int_0^L \rho A \begin{bmatrix} N_1 \\ N_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} N_1 & N_2 \end{bmatrix} dx$$

$$[m] = \rho A \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} N_1^2 & N_1 N_2 \\ N_1 N_2 & N_2^2 \end{bmatrix} dx$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Shape Functions:**

$$[m] = \rho A \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} \left(1 - \tfrac{x}{L}\right)^2 & \left(1 - \tfrac{x}{L}\right)\left(\tfrac{x}{L}\right) \\ \left(1 - \tfrac{x}{L}\right)\left(\tfrac{x}{L}\right) & \left(\tfrac{x}{L}\right)^2 \end{bmatrix} dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Integral Separately:**

Let $\xi = x/L$ so $dx = L\, d\xi$:

**Integral 1:**
$$I_{11} = \int_0^L \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)^2 dx = L\int_0^1 (1-\xi)^2 d\xi = L\left[\frac{(1-\xi)^3}{-3}\right]_0^1 = \frac{L}{3}$$

**Integral 2:**
$$I_{22} = \int_0^L \left(\frac{x}{L}\right)^2 dx = L\int_0^1 \xi^2 d\xi = L \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{L}{3}$$

**Integral 3 (off-diagonal):**
$$I_{12} = \int_0^L \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)\left(\frac{x}{L}\right) dx = L\int_0^1 (\xi - \xi^2) d\xi = L\left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\right] = \frac{L}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the Mass Matrix:**

$$[m] = \rho A \begin{bmatrix} L/3 & L/6 \\ L/6 & L/3 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{\rho A L}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{[m] = \frac{\rho A L}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 5 — Total Mass Verification:**

Sum of all entries of $[m]$:
$$\sum m_{ij} = \frac{\rho AL}{6}(2 + 1 + 1 + 2) = \rho AL$$

This correctly represents the total mass of the bar element ✓

**Step 6 — Lumped Mass Matrix (Comparison):**

The lumped mass matrix assigns half the total mass to each node:
$$[m_L] = \frac{\rho A L}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Beam Element Stiffness Matrix (Euler-Bernoulli)

**Step 1 — Define Nodal DOFs:**

A 2-node beam element has 4 DOFs: transverse displacement $v$ and rotation $\theta$ at each node:
$$\{d\} = \begin{Bmatrix} v_1 \\ \theta_1 \\ v_2 \\ \theta_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Cubic Hermite Shape Functions:**

The transverse displacement is interpolated using cubic polynomials (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory requires $C^1$ continuity):
$$v(x) = N_1 v_1 + N_2 \theta_1 + N_3 v_2 + N_4 \theta_2$$

The shape functions are:
$$N_1 = 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3, \quad N_2 = L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3)$$
$$N_3 = 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3, \quad N_4 = L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3)$$

where $\xi = x/L$.

**Step 3 — Strain-Displacement Matrix:**

For Euler-Bernoulli beam, curvature (related to bending strain) is:
$$\kappa = \frac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = [B]\{d\}$$

After differentiating and simplifying:
$$[B] = \frac{1}{L^2}\begin{bmatrix} (6 - 12\xi) & L(4 - 6\xi) & (-6 + 12\xi) & L(-2 + 6\xi) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Stiffness Matrix:**

$$[k] = \int_0^L EI [B]^T [B]\, dx$$

Performing the integration (change of variable $x = L\xi$, $dx = L\,d\xi$):

$$\boxed{[k] = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification of Singularity (rigid body modes):**

A rigid body translation $v_1 = v_2 = 1$, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 0$ should give $\{F\} = \{0\}$:
$$[k]\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{Bmatrix} 12 - 12 \\ 6L - 6L \\ -12 + 12 \\ 6L - 6L \end{Bmatrix} = \{0\} \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Bar Element Stiffness

**Problem:** A steel bar element has $L = 2$ m, $A = 0.001$ m², $E = 200 \times 10^9$ N/m². Compute $[k]$ and the nodal displacements if $F_2 = 10{,}000$ N and $u_1 = 0$ (fixed end).

**Solution:**

$$\frac{AE}{L} = \frac{0.001 \times 200 \times 10^9}{2} = 10^8 \text{ N/m}$$

$$[k] = 10^8 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

Applying $[k]\{d\} = \{F\}$ with $u_1 = 0$ and $F_2 = 10^4$ N:

$$10^8 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ 10^4 \end{Bmatrix}$$

From the second equation: $10^8 \cdot u_2 = 10^4$
$$\boxed{u_2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1 \text{ mm}}$$

This is a classic KTU 14-mark sub-problem pattern.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: 2D Constant Strain Triangle (CST) Stiffness Matrix

**Step 1 — Define Nodal Coordinates and DOFs:**

A CST has 3 nodes, each with 2 DOFs (u, v), giving 6 total DOFs:
$$\{d\} = \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 & v_1 & u_2 & v_2 & u_3 & v_3 \end{Bmatrix}^T$$

**Step 2 — Shape Functions (Linear):**

$$N_1 = \frac{1}{2A}(a_1 + b_1 x + c_1 y), \quad \text{etc.}$$

where:
- $a_1 = x_2 y_3 - x_3 y_2$, $b_1 = y_2 - y_3$, $c_1 = x_3 - x_2$
- $A$ = area of triangle = $\frac{1}{2}\vert(x_2 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)\vert$

**Step 3 — Strain-Displacement Matrix:**

$$[B] = \frac{1}{2A}\begin{bmatrix} b_1 & 0 & b_2 & 0 & b_3 & 0 \\ 0 & c_1 & 0 & c_2 & 0 & c_3 \\ c_1 & b_1 & c_2 & b_2 & c_3 & b_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Constitutive Matrix (Plane Stress):**

$$[D] = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Final Stiffness Matrix:**

$$\boxed{[k] = t \cdot A \cdot [B]^T [D] [B]}$$

where $t$ = thickness. This is a $6 \times 6$ matrix for the CST element.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Process Flow: Derivation of Element Matrices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: DISCRETIZE DOMAIN] --> B[STEP 2: IDENTIFY NODES AND DOFS]
    B --> C[STEP 3: SELECT SHAPE FUNCTIONS N]
    C --> D[STEP 4: DERIVE STRAIN DISPLACEMENT MATRIX B]
    D --> E[STEP 5: DEFINE CONSTITUTIVE MATRIX D]
    E --> F{STEP 6: CHOOSE ELEMENT TYPE}
    F --> G[STIFFNESS k: Integrate B transpose D B dV]
    F --> H[MASS m: Integrate rho N transpose N dV]
    F --> I[LOAD f: Integrate N transpose b dV plus surface tractions]
    G --> J[STEP 7: VERIFY SYMMETRY AND SINGULARITY]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[STEP 8: ASSEMBLE GLOBAL MATRIX]
    K --> L[STEP 9: APPLY BOUNDARY CONDITIONS]
    L --> M[STEP 10: SOLVE FOR UNKNOWN DOFS]
```

### 4.2 Architecture: Element Matrix Formation for 1D Bar

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT1[Nodes 1 and 2] --> SHAPE[Shape Functions N1 N2]
    INPUT2[Length L Area A Modulus E] --> SHAPE
    SHAPE --> BMATRIX[Strain Matrix B = 1 over L times -1 1]
    BMATRIX --> KSTIFF[Stiffness k = AE over L times 1 -1 and -1 1]
    INPUT3[Mass Density rho] --> MASS[Mass m = rho A L over 6 times 2 1 and 1 2]
    KSTIFF --> EQUIL[Equilibrium k d = f]
    MASS --> EQUIL
    INPUT4[External Force f] --> EQUIL
    EQUIL --> SOLVE[Solve for Displacements d]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Components Interacting in Element Matrix Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHAPE_BLOCK[SHAPE FUNCTION BLOCK]
        SF1[Linear N1 = 1 minus x over L]
        SF2[Linear N2 = x over L]
        SF3[Hermite for Beam]
    end
    subgraph B_BLOCK[STRAIN DISPLACEMENT BLOCK]
        BX1[Compute du over dx]
        BX2[Compute d2v over dx2 for beam]
        BX3[Compute partial derivatives for 2D]
    end
    subgraph D_BLOCK[MATERIAL PROPERTY BLOCK]
        DY1[Young Modulus E for 1D]
        DY2[EI for Beam]
        DY3[D matrix for 2D Plane Stress]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK[ELEMENT MATRIX OUTPUT]
        OUT1[Stiffness Matrix k]
        OUT2[Mass Matrix m]
        OUT3[Load Vector f]
    end
    SHAPE_BLOCK --> B_BLOCK
    B_BLOCK --> OUTPUT_BLOCK
    D_BLOCK --> OUTPUT_BLOCK
```

### 4.4 Comparison Diagram: Consistent vs. Lumped Mass Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Element Mass Matrix Type] --> B[Consistent Mass Matrix]
    A --> C[Lumped Mass Matrix]
    B --> B1[Derived from Shape Functions]
    B --> B2[Coupled Off Diagonal Terms]
    B --> B3[More Accurate for Higher Modes]
    C --> C1[Diagonal Matrix]
    C --> C2[Half Total Mass at Each Node]
    C --> C3[Faster Computation Inverse]
    B --> B4[Example: rho AL over 6 times 2 1 and 1 2]
    C --> C4[Example: rho AL over 2 times 1 0 and 0 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
*Define the consistent mass matrix. How is it different from the lumped mass matrix? Mention its advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Consistent Mass Matrix:** It is the mass matrix derived by using the same shape functions used for the displacement field as weighting functions for the inertial forces. For a 1D bar element, it is:
> 
> $$[m] = \int_V \rho[N]^T[N]\, dV = \frac{\rho A L}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> **Lumped Mass Matrix:** It is a diagonal approximation where the total mass is distributed equally (or by some heuristic) at the nodes:
> 
> $$[m_L] = \frac{\rho A L}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
> 
> **Differences:**
> - Consistent mass matrix has off-diagonal terms; lumped is purely diagonal.
> - Consistent mass gives more accurate natural frequencies; lumped is computationally cheaper.
> - Lumped mass has a trivial inverse; consistent requires matrix inversion.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**
*State the properties of the element stiffness matrix. Why is it singular for a free (unconstrained) element?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Properties of $[k]$:**
> 1. **Symmetric:** $[k] = [k]^T$ (from Betti's reciprocal theorem and conservation of energy)
> 2. **Singular for unconstrained element:** Det$[k] = 0$ for a free body
> 3. **Positive Semi-Definite:** $[d]^T[k][d] \geq 0$ (strain energy is non-negative)
> 4. **Order = number of DOFs per element:** $n \times n$ matrix
> 5. **Bandwidth depends on nodal numbering**
> 
> **Why Singular:** For a free (unconstrained) element, the structure can undergo rigid body motion without any deformation. Since no strain energy is stored during rigid body motion, $[k]\{d\} = \{0\}$ for non-zero $\{d\}$ (rigid body modes), implying Det$[k] = 0$. Boundary conditions must be applied to make $[k]$ non-singular and solvable.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state units in the final answer. A bar stiffness should be in **N/m**, not just a number.
> 2. **Skipping integration steps:** KTU examiners deduct 2-3 marks if the integral evaluation (e.g., $\int_0^L (1-x/L)^2 dx = L/3$) is not shown explicitly.
> 3. **Sign errors in $[B]$ matrix:** A common mistake is writing $B = [1/L \; -1/L]$ instead of $B = [-1/L \; 1/L]$. The sign convention must be consistent with the global axis.
> 4. **Confusing consistent and lumped mass:** KTU specifically asks for "consistent" mass matrix in many problems. Do not substitute the lumped version.
> 5. **Missing shape function verification:** Always verify that $\sum N_i = 1$ (partition of unity property).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Bar & Beam Stiffness Matrix Derivation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix for a 2-node linear bar element of length $L$, area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$, starting from the assumed displacement field. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For a 2-node beam element, write down the shape functions and the element stiffness matrix. State the boundary conditions required to make it non-singular. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Assume linear displacement field: $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x$ (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions at $x=0$ and $x=L$ to obtain shape functions:
$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 3:** Express displacement: $u(x) = [N]\{d\} = [N_1 \; N_2]\{u_1, u_2\}^T$ (1 mark)

**Step 4:** Compute $[B]$:
$$[B] = \frac{d[N]}{dx} = \left[-\frac{1}{L} \; \frac{1}{L}\right] \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 5:** Apply the general formula:
$$[k] = \int_0^L [B]^T E A [B]\, dx = \frac{EA}{L^2}\int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}dx \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 6:** Final answer and verification:
$$[k] = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 7:** Verify symmetry and singularity. (0.5 marks)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Nodal DOFs: $\{d\} = \{v_1, \theta_1, v_2, \theta_2\}^T$ (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Hermite shape functions (with $\xi = x/L$):
$$N_1 = 1 - 3\xi^2 + 2\xi^3, \quad N_2 = L(\xi - 2\xi^2 + \xi^3)$$
$$N_3 = 3\xi^2 - 2\xi^3, \quad N_4 = L(-\xi^2 + \xi^3) \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3:** Element stiffness matrix:
$$[k] = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 4:** Properties: symmetric, positive semi-definite, singular for free element. (1 mark)

**Step 5:** Boundary conditions to remove singularity: Fix $v_1$ and $\theta_1$ (clamped end) and/or $v_2$ and $\theta_2$ (simply supported or clamped). The minimum required is to prevent 2 rigid body translations and 1 rigid body rotation = 3 boundary conditions. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Mass Matrix & Numerical Application

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the consistent mass matrix for a 2-node linear bar element. Verify that the sum of all entries equals the total mass of the element. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A steel bar of length $L = 1$ m, area $A = 200$ mm², density $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, and Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa is fixed at one end and subjected to an axial load $F = 5$ kN at the free end. Using 1 element, compute the displacement at the free end and the reaction force. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** General formula:
$$[m] = \int_V \rho[N]^T[N]\, dV = \rho A \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} N_1^2 & N_1 N_2 \\ N_1 N_2 & N_2^2 \end{bmatrix} dx \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N_1 = 1 - x/L$ and $N_2 = x/L$:
$$[m] = \rho A \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} (1 - x/L)^2 & (1 - x/L)(x/L) \\ (1 - x/L)(x/L) & (x/L)^2 \end{bmatrix} dx \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate integrals:
- $\int_0^L (1 - x/L)^2 dx = L/3$
- $\int_0^L (x/L)^2 dx = L/3$
- $\int_0^L (1 - x/L)(x/L) dx = L/6$
(2 marks)

**Step 4:** Assemble:
$$[m] = \frac{\rho A L}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 5:** Verification:
$$\text{Sum} = \frac{\rho A L}{6}(2 + 1 + 1 + 2) = \rho A L = \text{Total mass} \checkmark \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $L = 1$ m, $A = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ N/m², $F = 5000$ N

**Step 1:** Stiffness:
$$k = \frac{AE}{L} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^9}{1} = 4 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

$$[k] = 4 \times 10^7 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Equilibrium: $[k]\{d\} = \{F\}$
$$\begin{bmatrix} 4 \times 10^7 & -4 \times 10^7 \\ -4 \times 10^7 & 4 \times 10^7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} R_1 \\ 5000 \end{Bmatrix} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3:** Apply boundary condition: $u_1 = 0$ (fixed end) (1 mark)

**Step 4:** Solve for $u_2$:
$$4 \times 10^7 \cdot u_2 = 5000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_2 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.125 \text{ mm} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 5:** Reaction force:
$$R_1 = -4 \times 10^7 \cdot u_2 = -5000 \text{ N} \quad (1.5 \text{ marks})$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction acts opposite to the applied load direction, which is physically correct.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — ELEMENT MATRICES (MODULE 3)**

#### **Core Formulas to Memorize:**

- ✅ **Bar Stiffness:** $[k] = \frac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- ✅ **Bar Consistent Mass:** $[m] = \frac{\rho A L}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
- ✅ **Bar Lumped Mass:** $[m_L] = \frac{\rho A L}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- ✅ **Beam Stiffness:** $[k] = \frac{EI}{L^3}\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 6L & -12 & 6L \\ 6L & 4L^2 & -6L & 2L^2 \\ -12 & -6L & 12 & -6L \\ 6L & 2L^2 & -6L & 4L^2 \end{bmatrix}$
- ✅ **2D Truss Stiffness:** Use $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ in a $4 \times 4$ matrix
- ✅ **CST Stiffness:** $[k] = t \cdot A \cdot [B]^T[D][B]$ (6×6 matrix)
- ✅ **General Formula:** $[k] = \int_V [B]^T[D][B]\,dV$, $[m] = \int_V \rho[N]^T[N]\,dV$

#### **Key Properties to State in Exams:**

- ✅ $[k]$ is **symmetric, singular** for unconstrained elements, and **banded**
- ✅ Sum of all entries of consistent mass matrix = total mass of element
- ✅ Shape functions must satisfy **partition of unity** ($\sum N_i = 1$) and **completeness**
- ✅ Lumped mass matrix is **diagonal**; consistent mass is generally **full**

#### **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**

- ❌ Forgetting to include **units** in final answer
- ❌ Confusing **plane stress** and **plane strain** $[D]$ matrices
- ❌ Missing the **thickness $t$** in CST stiffness formula
- ❌ Not applying **boundary conditions** before solving
- ❌ Writing $[B]$ with wrong **sign convention**

#### **Exam Strategy Tips:**

- 🎯 For 7-mark derivations, always show: **shape function → B matrix → integration → final answer → verification**
- 🎯 For numerical problems, always state: **Given → Formula → Substitution → Answer with units**
- 🎯 For 3-mark questions, focus on **definitions + 1 distinguishing property**
- 🎯 Practice the integration steps — KTU values stepwise working over final answers
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plane Bilinear Element
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plane Bilinear Element

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plane Bilinear Element (Q4 Element)**
> A *plane bilinear element* is a **two-dimensional, four-noded isoparametric quadrilateral finite element** used to model stress, strain, and displacement fields in plane elasticity problems (plane stress and plane strain). It is called "bilinear" because the shape functions are *linear in each of the two natural coordinates* ($\xi$ and $\eta$) separately, but *bilinear* in their product. The element uses **bilinear interpolation** of geometry and field variables over a master square domain of side length **2 units** in natural coordinates, defined by $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ and $\eta \in [-1, +1]$.

### 1.2 Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Plane** | Restricted to 2-D (x–y coordinate system) |
| **Bilinear** | Linear in $\xi$ when $\eta$ fixed; linear in $\eta$ when $\xi$ fixed |
| **Q4** | Quadrilateral with **4 nodes** (one at each corner) |
| **Isoparametric** | Same shape functions used to map geometry AND field variable |
| **Master element** | Standard square in natural coords ($\xi,\eta$) |
| **Parent domain** | Synonym for master element |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Sheet" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the plane bilinear element as a flexible rubber sheet stretched over four corner pegs.**
> Imagine you have a **square rubber sheet** with four pegs fixed at its corners (nodes 1, 2, 3, 4). If you push or pull one peg, the rubber deforms in a *bilinear* way — straight lines remain straight along the edges, but the interior deforms into a gently curved (actually, ruled/linear-in-cross-section) shape. The element "knows" how to stretch because it remembers **four numbers at each corner**: the two coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ defining *where* the peg sits, and the unknown displacement $(u_i, v_i)$ defining *how much* the peg has moved.
>
> The element transforms any arbitrary quadrilateral in the real (x, y) world into a perfect square in the abstract ($\xi, \eta$) world — this is the **isoparametric mapping**. Inside the master square, the math is clean and symmetric; outside, in the real world, it fits any shape your structure demands.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Reference Values**
> - Natural coordinate range: $\xi \in [-1, +1]$, $\eta \in [-1, +1]$ **(must memorize)**
> - Total nodes per element: **4**
> - Degrees of freedom (DOF) per node in 2-D: **2** ($u$ and $v$)
> - Total DOF per element: **8**
> - Gauss points for exact $2 \times 2$ integration: **4** (at $\xi, \eta = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Element — Natural Coordinate System

The plane bilinear element is defined on a **reference square** in natural (parent) coordinates:

- Node 1: $(\xi, \eta) = (-1, -1)$
- Node 2: $(\xi, \eta) = (+1, -1)$
- Node 3: $(\xi, \eta) = (+1, +1)$
- Node 4: $(\xi, \eta) = (-1, +1)$

Nodes are numbered **counter-clockwise** for positive Jacobian.

### 2.2 Shape Functions (Core of the Element)

The **four bilinear shape functions** $N_i(\xi, \eta)$ are derived as products of 1-D linear Lagrange polynomials:

**Logical construction steps:**
1. In 1-D, $L_1 = \frac{1-\xi}{2}$ and $L_2 = \frac{1+\xi}{2}$.
2. In 2-D, simply multiply the 1-D Lagrange polynomials for each direction.
3. This yields the famous **bilinear shape functions** shown below.

### 2.3 Isoparametric Mapping (Geometry & Field Variable)

Both the **geometry** (coordinates $x, y$) and the **field variable** (displacements $u, v$) are interpolated using the *same* shape functions:

$$
x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i, \qquad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, y_i
$$

$$
u(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, u_i, \qquad v(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, v_i
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must memorize the entire table below for KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Shape function $N_1$ | $\frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)$ | Corner node 1 |
| 2 | Shape function $N_2$ | $\frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)$ | Corner node 2 |
| 3 | Shape function $N_3$ | $\frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta)$ | Corner node 3 |
| 4 | Shape function $N_4$ | $\frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)$ | Corner node 4 |
| 5 | Sum check | $\sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i = 1$ | Partition of unity |
| 6 | Kronecker property | $N_i(\xi_j, \eta_j) = \delta_{ij}$ | Interpolation property |
| 7 | Jacobian matrix | $\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x/\partial\xi & \partial y/\partial\xi \\ \partial x/\partial\eta & \partial y/\partial\eta \end{bmatrix}$ | $2 \times 2$ matrix |
| 8 | Jacobian determinant | $\det(\mathbf{J}) = J$ | Must be $>0$ |
| 9 | Strain-displacement | $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial x & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial x & 0 & \cdots \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial y & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial y & \cdots \\ \partial N_1/\partial y & \partial N_1/\partial x & \partial N_2/\partial y & \partial N_2/\partial x & \cdots \end{bmatrix}$ | $3 \times 8$ matrix |
| 10 | Gradient transform | $\begin{Bmatrix} \partial N/\partial x \\ \partial N/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \partial N/\partial\xi \\ \partial N/\partial\eta \end{Bmatrix}$ | Chain rule |
| 11 | Stiffness matrix | $\mathbf{K}^e = \int_{-1}^{+1} \int_{-1}^{+1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D}\, \mathbf{B}\, \det(\mathbf{J})\, d\xi\, d\eta$ | $8 \times 8$ matrix |
| 12 | Gauss point | $\xi = \eta = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ | For $2 \times 2$ rule |
| 13 | Gauss weight | $w_i = 1$ (for $2 \times 2$ rule) | Per integration point |

### 2.5 Why This Element Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of the Plane Bilinear Element**
> - **Aerospace:** Stress analysis of aircraft wing skin panels (flat or gently curved).
> - **Civil:** 2-D analysis of dams, retaining walls, and soil masses (plane strain).
> - **Mechanical:** Stress concentration analysis in plates with holes — mesh it with many Q4 elements.
> - **Automotive:** Crash-worthiness simulation of sheet-metal panels.
> - **Biomedical:** 2-D modelling of bone slices, arterial walls, and soft tissues.
>
> The Q4 element is the **workhorse of commercial FEA codes** (ANSYS, ABAQUS, NASTRAN) for 2-D problems. While not as accurate as higher-order elements (Q8, Q9), its **simplicity, robustness, and computational efficiency** make it the default first-choice element in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Four Shape Functions

**Step 1: Start with the 1-D Lagrange polynomials along $\xi$.**

$$
L_1^{(1D)} = \frac{1-\xi}{2}, \qquad L_2^{(1D)} = \frac{1+\xi}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Similarly, define 1-D Lagrange polynomials along $\eta$.**

$$
L_1^{'(1D)} = \frac{1-\eta}{2}, \qquad L_2^{'(1D)} = \frac{1+\eta}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply the appropriate 1-D polynomials for each corner node.**

For node 1 at $(\xi,\eta)=(-1,-1)$, the shape function is the product of the polynomials that equal **1 at node 1** and **0 at all other nodes**:

$$
N_1(\xi,\eta) = \frac{1-\xi}{2} \cdot \frac{1-\eta}{2} = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)
$$

For node 2 at $(\xi,\eta)=(+1,-1)$:

$$
N_2(\xi,\eta) = \frac{1+\xi}{2} \cdot \frac{1-\eta}{2} = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)
$$

For node 3 at $(\xi,\eta)=(+1,+1)$:

$$
N_3(\xi,\eta) = \frac{1+\xi}{2} \cdot \frac{1+\eta}{2} = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta)
$$

For node 4 at $(\xi,\eta)=(-1,+1)$:

$$
N_4(\xi,\eta) = \frac{1-\xi}{2} \cdot \frac{1+\eta}{2} = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)
$$

**Step 4: Verify partition of unity.**

$$
N_1+N_2+N_3+N_4 = \frac{1}{4}\left[4\right] = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Verify the Kronecker delta property** (e.g., at node 1):

$$
N_1(-1,-1) = \frac{1}{4}(1-(-1))(1-(-1)) = \frac{1}{4}(2)(2) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
N_2(-1,-1) = \frac{1}{4}(1+(-1))(1-(-1)) = \frac{1}{4}(0)(2) = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Jacobian Matrix

**Step 1: Differentiate the geometric mapping $x(\xi,\eta)$ with respect to $\xi$.**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i
$$

The derivatives of the shape functions with respect to $\xi$ are:

$$
\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} = -\frac{1}{4}(1-\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi} = +\frac{1}{4}(1-\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi} = +\frac{1}{4}(1+\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi} = -\frac{1}{4}(1+\eta)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Jacobian.**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \frac{1}{4}\left[-(1-\eta)x_1 + (1-\eta)x_2 + (1+\eta)x_3 - (1+\eta)x_4\right]
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \frac{1}{4}\left[(x_2-x_1)(1-\eta) + (x_3-x_4)(1+\eta)\right]
$$

**Step 3: By symmetry, the other three entries** $\partial x/\partial\eta$, $\partial y/\partial\xi$, $\partial y/\partial\eta$ **follow the same pattern with $x_i$ replaced by $y_i$ and $\xi$ swapped with $\eta$.**

### 3.3 Derivation of the Strain-Displacement Matrix B

**Step 1: Strain definition in 2-D elasticity.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} \\ \varepsilon_{yy} \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial x \\ \partial v/\partial y \\ \partial u/\partial y + \partial v/\partial x \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Express displacement derivatives via the chain rule.**

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial x \\ \partial u/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial\xi \\ \partial u/\partial\eta \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Use isoparametric interpolation** $u = \sum N_i u_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} u_i, \qquad \frac{\partial u}{\partial \eta} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} u_i
$$

**Step 4: Assemble the B matrix** (a $3 \times 8$ matrix, since we have 8 DOFs total: $u_1, v_1, u_2, v_2, u_3, v_3, u_4, v_4$):

$$
\mathbf{B}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_i/\partial y \\ \partial N_i/\partial y & \partial N_i/\partial x \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B}_1 & \mathbf{B}_2 & \mathbf{B}_3 & \mathbf{B}_4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.4 Element Stiffness Matrix — Numerical Integration Form

**Step 1: General stiffness integral.**

$$
\mathbf{K}^e = \int_{-1}^{+1} \int_{-1}^{+1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D}\, \mathbf{B}\, \det(\mathbf{J})\, d\xi\, d\eta
$$

where $\mathbf{D}$ is the **constitutive matrix**:

$$
\mathbf{D}_{\text{plane stress}} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & (1-\nu)/2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Apply $2 \times 2$ Gauss quadrature** (exact for the Q4 element).

$$
\mathbf{K}^e = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} w_i w_j \, \mathbf{B}^T(\xi_i, \eta_j)\, \mathbf{D}\, \mathbf{B}(\xi_i, \eta_j)\, \det(\mathbf{J}(\xi_i, \eta_j))
$$

where the Gauss points and weights are:

$$
\xi_i = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad \eta_j = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad w_i = w_j = 1
$$

**Step 3: Compute at each Gauss point** by substituting $\xi = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$ and $\eta = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$ into $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{J}$, and $\det(\mathbf{J})$, then performing the matrix multiplication.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Jacobian for a Simple Element

> [!NOTE]
> **Illustrative problem:** Consider a square element with corner nodes:
> - Node 1: $(0, 0)$
> - Node 2: $(2, 0)$
> - Node 3: $(2, 2)$
> - Node 4: $(0, 2)$

**Step 1: Compute $\partial x/\partial \xi$ at the element centroid** $(\xi, \eta) = (0, 0)$.

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi}\bigg|_{(0,0)} = \frac{1}{4}\left[(x_2-x_1)(1-0) + (x_3-x_4)(1+0)\right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[2 + 2\right] = 1
$$

**Step 2: Similarly compute the other three entries** (since the element is a perfect square with side 2, all four partial derivatives equal **1** in absolute value).

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = 1, \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = -1, \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} = 1
$$

**Step 3: Assemble the Jacobian and compute its determinant.**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \det(\mathbf{J}) = (1)(1) - (-1)(1) = 2
$$

**Interpretation:** A $2 \times 2$ real square maps to a $1 \times 1$ master square with a Jacobian determinant of **2**, which is exactly the area scaling factor — physically correct.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Q4 Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Domain x, y] --> B[Isoparametric Mapping]
    B --> C[Master Domain xi, eta = -1 to +1]
    C --> D[Shape Functions N1 to N4]
    D --> E[Interpolate Geometry x,y]
    D --> F[Interpolate Displacement u,v]
    E --> G[Compute Jacobian J]
    G --> H[Compute B Matrix]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Integrate B transpose D B detJ]
    I --> J[Element Stiffness Matrix K 8x8]
    J --> K[Assembly into Global K]
    K --> L[Solve Ku = F for u]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Node Numbering Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N4["Node 4: xi=-1, eta=+1"] --- N3["Node 3: xi=+1, eta=+1"]
    N1["Node 1: xi=-1, eta=-1"] --- N2["Node 2: xi=+1, eta=-1"]
    N1 --- N4
    N2 --- N3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Computational Workflow for Q4 Stiffness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Input: Node Coordinates x_i, y_i, Material E, nu]) --> loop[Loop over 4 Gauss Points]
    loop --> eval_shape[Evaluate N1 to N4]
    eval_shape --> calc_jacob[Compute Jacobian Matrix J]
    calc_jacob --> inv_jacob[Invert J to get J inverse]
    inv_jacob --> calc_b[Compute Strain-Displacement B]
    calc_b --> calc_d[Form Material Matrix D]
    calc_d --> mult[Multiply B transpose D B detJ]
    mult --> accum[Accumulate into K element]
    accum --> loop
    loop --> done([Output 8x8 Element Stiffness K])
```

### 4.4 Physical Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Physical Quadrilateral] -->|Isoparametric Map| M[Master Square]
    M -->|Bilinear Interpolation| SF[4 Shape Functions]
    SF --> GEOM[Geometry Recovery]
    SF --> DISP[Displacement Field]
    DISP --> STRAIN[Strain via B Matrix]
    STRAIN --> STRESS[Stress via D Matrix]
    STRESS --> STIFF[Stiffness via Integration]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define a plane bilinear element. Write its four shape functions in natural coordinates.**

**Model Answer:**
A plane bilinear element is a 4-noded 2-D isoparametric quadrilateral element with bilinear shape functions defined over a master square domain $\xi, \eta \in [-1, +1]$.

$$
N_1 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_3 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta), \quad N_4 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)
$$

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **State the significance of the Jacobian matrix in an isoparametric formulation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}$ maps derivatives from the natural coordinate system $(\xi, \eta)$ to the global Cartesian system $(x, y)$ via the chain rule. Its determinant $\det(\mathbf{J})$ represents the area scaling factor between the master element and the real element, and must remain strictly positive to preserve element orientation and avoid mapping singularities.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the four shape functions of a 4-noded plane bilinear element. Show that they satisfy the partition-of-unity property. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the isoparametric formulation. For a plane bilinear element, write the expression for the element stiffness matrix and explain the role of the Jacobian matrix in the integration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Identify the 1-D Lagrange polynomials.

$$
L_1 = \frac{1-\xi}{2}, \quad L_2 = \frac{1+\xi}{2}, \quad L_1' = \frac{1-\eta}{2}, \quad L_2' = \frac{1+\eta}{2}
$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Form the 2-D bilinear shape functions by tensor product.

$$
N_1 = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_2 = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_3 = \frac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta), \quad N_4 = \frac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Verify partition of unity.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i = \frac{1}{4}\left[(1-\xi)(1-\eta) + (1+\xi)(1-\eta) + (1+\xi)(1+\eta) + (1-\xi)(1+\eta)\right] = \frac{1}{4}(4) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Define isoparametric formulation — same shape functions used to interpolate both geometry and field variable:

$$
x = \sum N_i x_i, \quad y = \sum N_i y_i, \quad u = \sum N_i u_i, \quad v = \sum N_i v_i
$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Write the stiffness matrix expression:

$$
\mathbf{K}^e = \int_{-1}^{+1} \int_{-1}^{+1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \mathbf{B} \det(\mathbf{J})\, d\xi\, d\eta
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Explain Jacobian's role — converts derivatives from natural to global coords and provides area scaling:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \partial N/\partial x \\ \partial N/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \partial N/\partial \xi \\ \partial N/\partial \eta \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathbf{J}) = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} - \frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta}\frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi}
$$

$\det(\mathbf{J}) > 0$ is required for proper element orientation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Define the term "isoparametric element" and list any four advantages of isoparametric formulation over the standard displacement formulation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a 4-noded plane bilinear element, derive the expression for the Jacobian matrix. Explain why its determinant must be positive throughout the element domain. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (3 Marks):** Definition — An isoparametric element is one in which **the same set of shape functions** is used to interpolate both the geometry and the unknown field variable (displacement). For the Q4 element:

$$
u(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi,\eta)\, u_i, \quad x(\xi,\eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} N_i(\xi,\eta)\, x_i
$$

**Step 2 (4 Marks):** Four advantages:

1. **Curved boundaries** can be modelled accurately without requiring extra nodes.
2. The element can handle **general quadrilateral shapes** (not just rectangles or parallelograms).
3. The formulation is **coordinate-invariant** — the same equations hold regardless of orientation.
4. **Higher-order accuracy** can be achieved by simply increasing the order of the polynomial shape functions.
5. (Bonus) Numerical integration via Gauss quadrature is straightforward in the natural coordinate system.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (4 Marks):** Derive the Jacobian matrix.

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where, using the isoparametric mapping:

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} x_i = \frac{1}{4}\left[-(1-\eta)x_1 + (1-\eta)x_2 + (1+\eta)x_3 - (1+\eta)x_4\right]
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi} y_i, \quad \frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} x_i, \quad \frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta} y_i
$$

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Why $\det(\mathbf{J}) > 0$:

- $\det(\mathbf{J})$ is the **local area scaling factor** between the master and real elements.
- If $\det(\mathbf{J}) < 0$, the element is **inverted** (mirror-imaged), which destroys the physical interpretation.
- If $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$, the element is **degenerate** (zero area) — division by zero occurs in $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$.
- A **positive, bounded** $\det(\mathbf{J})$ throughout the domain ensures a one-to-one mapping and prevents singularities during numerical integration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Node numbering confusion:** Many students mix up node 2 and node 4 in the shape functions. Always number **counter-clockwise** starting from the **bottom-left** corner.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\det(\mathbf{J})$ factor:** When writing the stiffness integral, omitting the Jacobian determinant is the single most common mistake — it costs **2 full marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $\partial N/\partial \xi$ with $\partial N/\partial x$:** Shape functions are defined in natural coordinates — derivatives must be converted via $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$.
> 4. **Wrong Gauss points:** Writing $\xi = \pm 0.5$ instead of $\pm 1/\sqrt{3}$. Memorize: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$.
> 5. **Stiffness matrix size:** The Q4 stiffness matrix is **$8 \times 8$**, not $4 \times 4$ — each node contributes 2 DOFs.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plane Bilinear Element**

- **Definition:** 4-noded, 2-D, isoparametric quadrilateral element; bilinear interpolation in natural coordinates.
- **Master domain:** Square with $\xi \in [-1, +1]$ and $\eta \in [-1, +1]$.
- **Number of nodes:** 4; **Total DOFs per element:** 8 (2 per node).
- **Shape functions:**
  - $N_1 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta)$
  - $N_2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)$
  - $N_3 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta)$
  - $N_4 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)$
- **Partition of unity:** $\sum N_i = 1$ always.
- **Kronecker property:** $N_i = 1$ at node $i$, zero at other nodes.
- **Isoparametric:** same $N_i$ used for geometry ($x, y$) and field ($u, v$).
- **Jacobian:** $2 \times 2$ matrix; $\det(\mathbf{J}) > 0$ mandatory.
- **B matrix:** $3 \times 8$ strain-displacement matrix; requires $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$.
- **Stiffness:** $\mathbf{K}^e = \int \int \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \mathbf{B} \det(\mathbf{J})\, d\xi\, d\eta$.
- **Gauss quadrature:** $2 \times 2$ rule at $\xi, \eta = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$, weights $w_i = 1$.
- **Advantages:** handles curved boundaries, general quad shapes, coordinate invariance, easy higher-order extension.
- **Disadvantages:** shear locking in nearly-incompressible / bending-dominated problems — use Q8 or Q9 instead.
- **Applications:** aerospace panels, civil dams (plane strain), sheet-metal analysis, biomedical 2-D models.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Galerkin FE Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Galerkin FE Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Galerkin FE Formulation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Galerkin Method (Weighted Residual Formulation):**  
> The **Galerkin method** is a *weighted residual technique* in which the **trial (approximate) functions** and the **weight (test) functions** are chosen to be the *same set of functions* — typically the **shape functions** $N_i(x)$ of the finite element. It converts a *governing differential equation* into an *equivalent integral (weak) form* that the approximate solution satisfies in an average sense.

Mathematically, for a general differential operator $\mathcal{L}(u) + f = 0$ with approximate solution $u^h = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j(x)\,u_j$, the Galerkin equations are:

$$\int_{\Omega} N_i \bigl[\mathcal{L}(u^h) + f\bigr]\, d\Omega = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where $N_i$ are the **weighting functions** (identical to the trial/shape functions). This is the cornerstone of virtually every commercial finite element formulation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Taut Rope on Stakes" Picture

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition:** Imagine you must hang a heavy rope between two posts, but the rope cannot follow the *exact* catenary curve (governing PDE). Instead, you fix a small number of **stakes** (the nodal points) at intermediate locations and stretch the rope straight between them.  
> – The **stakes** = nodal degrees of freedom.  
> – The **straight rope segments** = shape functions $N_i(x)$.  
> – **Pulling the rope** = minimizing the residual.  
> The Galerkin method asks: *"At each stake, in which direction and with how much force should I push so that the rope (approximate solution) is as close as possible to the true catenary (exact solution)?"* The push direction is $N_i$ — the same family used to model the rope. Hence *trial = test = $N_i$*.

---

### 1.3 Why Galerkin is the Backbone of FEM

| Aspect | Galerkin Property |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Symmetry** | Produces a *symmetric* stiffness matrix for self-adjoint problems |
| **Optimality** | Same as the Rayleigh–Ritz variational approach for self-adjoint operators |
| **Generality** | Works for *non-self-adjoint* problems (unlike Ritz) |
| **Consistency** | Satisfies the governing PDE exactly as $h \to 0$ |
| **Ease of implementation** | Test functions are already available as shape functions |

> [!TIP]  
> **Key Insight for KTU:** In a 1-D bar problem, if you used *different* test functions (e.g., Collocation — Dirac delta), you would obtain a *non-symmetric* matrix and a *less accurate* solution. Galerkin guarantees the *best* mean-square accuracy for a given mesh density.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **$EA$ (Axial Rigidity):** $E$ = Young's modulus, $A$ = cross-section area — measured in **N**.
- **$k$ (Thermal Conductivity):** Measured in **W/(m·K)**.
- **$h$ (Element Length / Characteristic Size):** Convergence rate depends on $h^p$, where $p$ = polynomial order of $N_i$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Picture — Where Does Galerkin Sit in FEM?

The complete FEM pipeline has **5 stages**:

1. **Strong form** — the governing PDE + boundary conditions.
2. **Weak form** — obtained by multiplying by weight function and integrating (this is where Galerkin acts).
3. **Discrete form** — element-level matrices ($\mathbf{K}^{(e)}, \mathbf{F}^{(e)}$).
4. **Assembly** — global $\mathbf{Ku = F}$.
5. **Solution & post-processing.**

Galerkin operates in **Step 2**, transforming a *differential* statement into an *integral* statement that demands **one order less** of differentiability — making it ideal for the polynomial shape functions used in FEM.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Galerkin FE Formulation

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The 7 Sacred Steps of Galerkin FE Formulation (Memorize for KTU)**

- **Step 1 — Identify the Strong Form:** Write the governing PDE and BCs.
  Example: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\dfrac{du}{dx}\right) + f(x) = 0$ in $\Omega = (0, L)$.
- **Step 2 — Define the Approximation:** $u^h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j(x)\,u_j$.
- **Step 3 — Choose Weight Functions:** $W_i(x) = N_i(x)$ (Galerkin choice).
- **Step 4 — Form the Weighted Residual:**
  $\int_{\Omega} N_i \left[\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\dfrac{du^h}{dx}\right) + f(x)\right] dx = 0$.
- **Step 5 — Integrate by Parts (Green's Theorem):** Moves one derivative from $u^h$ to $N_i$, producing the boundary term.
- **Step 6 — Substitute Shape Functions:** Identify element matrices:
  $k_{ij}^{(e)} = \int_{\Omega^e} \dfrac{dN_i}{dx}\,EA\,\dfrac{dN_j}{dx}\, dx$, $\quad f_i^{(e)} = \int_{\Omega^e} N_i f\, dx$.
- **Step 7 — Apply BCs & Solve:** Assemble global system $\mathbf{Ku = F}$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]  
> **Print or Memorize — This Table Covers ~80% of Numerical Questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Approximate solution | $u^h(x) = \sum_{j} N_j(x)\,u_j$ | Sum over element nodes |
| 2 | Galerkin equation | $\int_{\Omega} N_i\,R\, d\Omega = 0$ | $R = \mathcal{L}(u^h) + f$ |
| 3 | Element stiffness entry | $k_{ij}^{(e)} = \int_{\Omega^e} B_i^T\, D\, B_j\, d\Omega$ | $B = dN/dx$ for 1-D |
| 4 | Element load entry | $f_i^{(e)} = \int_{\Omega^e} N_i\, f(x)\, dx$ | $f$ = body force / source |
| 5 | Boundary term (natural BC) | $F_i^{BC} = \left[N_i\, q\right]_{\Gamma_q}$ | $q$ = flux / traction |
| 6 | Linear 1-D shape functions | $N_1 = 1 - \xi,\ \ N_2 = \xi$ | $\xi = x/L_e$ |
| 7 | Derivatives | $\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L_e},\ \ \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L_e}$ | Constant on the element |
| 8 | 1-D bar element $\mathbf{K}^{(e)}$ | $\dfrac{EA}{L_e}\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Symmetric, singular w/o BC |
| 9 | Consistent load (constant $f$) | $\mathbf{F}^{(e)} = \dfrac{fL_e}{2}\!\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$ | Equals "lumped" for linear shape fns |
| 10 | Convergence rate | Error $= \mathcal{O}(h^{p+1})$ for displacement, $\mathcal{O}(h^{p})$ for stress | $p$ = polynomial order |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert x \vert` inside the table** — never the pipe symbol, as it breaks Markdown rendering. In handwritten exams, plain $\mid x \mid$ is fine.

---

## 2.4 Why the Weak Form? — The Engineering Justification

> [!TIP]  
> The *strong form* requires $u$ to be **twice differentiable** (for second-order PDEs). Piecewise-polynomial shape functions $N_i$ are only $C^0$-continuous, so they **cannot** satisfy the strong form exactly at element interfaces. The *weak form* relaxes this requirement to **$C^0$ continuity**, making piecewise-linear / quadratic / cubic approximations valid.

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **Civil:** Trusses, frames, beams (Galerkin $\Rightarrow$ symmetric $\mathbf{K}$).
- **Mechanical:** Heat conduction, torsion, elasticity.
- **Aerospace:** Plate/shell buckling where Ritz is impractical.
- **Biomedical:** Diffusion in tissues (non-symmetric) — only Galerkin works.

---

## 2.5 The Variational Equivalence Theorem (Crucial for 14-Mark Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Theorem:** For a **self-adjoint, positive-definite** differential operator $\mathcal{L}$, the Galerkin method yields **exactly** the same linear algebraic system as the **Rayleigh–Ritz variational method** based on the *energy functional* $\Pi = \frac{1}{2}\int (\cdots) - \int f\,u$.  
> **Proof sketch:** Galerkin equations = $\delta\Pi = 0$.

This is *why* Galerkin is treated as the "universal FEM method" — it is **the most general** of the weighted-residual techniques and coincides with the variational method for the most common engineering problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Derivation — Galerkin FE for a 1-D Bar Element

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (Strong Form)

Consider a uniform prismatic bar of length $L$, cross-section $A$, Young's modulus $E$, subjected to an axial distributed load $f(x)$ (N/m) and a tip force $P$ at $x = L$.

$$EA\,\frac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}} + f(x) = 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega = (0, L)$$

Boundary conditions:
- **Essential (Dirichlet):** $u(0) = 0$ (fixed at left end).
- **Natural (Neumann):** $EA\,\dfrac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{x = L} = P$ (prescribed end force).

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Define the Approximate Solution

Divide the bar into $E$ linear elements. For element $e$ connecting nodes $i$ and $j$ of length $L_e$:

$$u^h(x) = N_1(x)\,u_1 + N_2(x)\,u_2$$

where the **linear shape functions** in physical coordinates are:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L_e}, \qquad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L_e}, \qquad 0 \le x \le L_e$$

Their derivatives are constants:

$$\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L_e}, \qquad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = +\frac{1}{L_e}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Form the Galerkin Weighted Residual

For node $i = 1, 2$ of the element, require:

$$\int_{0}^{L_e} N_i(x)\!\left[EA\,\frac{d^{2}u^{h}}{dx^{2}} + f(x)\right] dx = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Integration by Parts (the Key Move)

Apply integration by parts to the *first* term. Recall the identity:

$$\int_{a}^{b} v\, \frac{du}{dx}\, dx = \bigl[v\,u\bigr]_{a}^{b} - \int_{a}^{b} \frac{dv}{dx}\,u\, dx$$

Setting $v = N_i$ and $u = \dfrac{du^{h}}{dx}$:

$$\int_{0}^{L_e} N_i\,EA\,\frac{d^{2}u^{h}}{dx^{2}}\, dx = EA\!\left[N_i\,\frac{du^{h}}{dx}\right]_{0}^{L_e} - \int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\frac{dN_i}{dx}\,\frac{du^{h}}{dx}\, dx$$

Substituting back, the Galerkin equation becomes:

$$EA\!\left[N_i\,\frac{du^{h}}{dx}\right]_{0}^{L_e} - \int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\frac{dN_i}{dx}\,\frac{du^{h}}{dx}\, dx + \int_{0}^{L_e} N_i\, f(x)\, dx = 0$$

Rearranging (moving the *negative* stiffness term to the left):

$$\int_{0}^{L_e} EA\,\frac{dN_i}{dx}\,\frac{du^{h}}{dx}\, dx = \int_{0}^{L_e} N_i\, f(x)\, dx + \left[EA\,N_i\,\frac{du^{h}}{dx}\right]_{0}^{L_e}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Substitute the Approximation

Because $u^h = N_1 u_1 + N_2 u_2$, the derivative is:

$$\frac{du^h}{dx} = -\frac{u_1}{L_e} + \frac{u_2}{L_e} = \frac{1}{L_e}\bigl(-u_1 + u_2\bigr)$$

The left-hand side of the Galerkin equation for $i = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{L_e} EA\!\left(-\frac{1}{L_e}\right)\!\left(-\frac{u_1}{L_e} + \frac{u_2}{L_e}\right) dx = \frac{EA}{L_e^{2}}\int_{0}^{L_e}\bigl(u_1 - u_2\bigr) dx = \frac{EA}{L_e}\bigl(u_1 - u_2\bigr)$$

For $i = 2$:

$$\int_{0}^{L_e} EA\!\left(+\frac{1}{L_e}\right)\!\left(-\frac{u_1}{L_e} + \frac{u_2}{L_e}\right) dx = \frac{EA}{L_e^{2}}\int_{0}^{L_e}\bigl(-u_1 + u_2\bigr) dx = \frac{EA}{L_e}\bigl(-u_1 + u_2\bigr)$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Assemble the Element Stiffness Matrix

Writing both equations $i = 1, 2$ in matrix form:

$$\frac{EA}{L_e}\begin{bmatrix} +1 & -1 \\ -1 & +1 \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \mathbf{F}^{(e)}$$

Therefore, the **element stiffness matrix** is:

$$\mathbf{K}^{(e)} = \frac{EA}{L_e}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Notice:** This matrix is **symmetric**, **positive semi-definite** (singular — no rigid-body constraint removed yet), and its **rows sum to zero** (rigid-body translation $u_1 = u_2 = c$ gives zero force — fundamental consistency check).

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Element Consistent Load Vector

For a constant body force $f(x) = f_0$:

$$F_i^{(e)} = \int_{0}^{L_e} N_i(x)\,f_0\, dx$$

For $i = 1$:

$$F_1^{(e)} = f_0\int_{0}^{L_e}\!\left(1 - \frac{x}{L_e}\right) dx = f_0\!\left[x - \frac{x^{2}}{2L_e}\right]_{0}^{L_e} = f_0\!\left(L_e - \frac{L_e}{2}\right) = \frac{f_0 L_e}{2}$$

For $i = 2$:

$$F_2^{(e)} = f_0\int_{0}^{L_e}\frac{x}{L_e}\, dx = \frac{f_0}{L_e}\!\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{L_e} = \frac{f_0 L_e}{2}$$

Hence the consistent load vector is:

$$\mathbf{F}^{(e)} = \frac{f_0 L_e}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]  
> For linear shape functions, the *consistent* (Galerkin) and *lumped* (point-load at midpoint) load vectors are **identical**. For quadratic elements, they differ.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Apply the Tip Force as a Natural Boundary Term

At $x = L_e$, the natural BC contributes $N_i(L_e)\,P$. For node 2 (right end of the element):

$$F_2^{(BC)} = N_2(L_e)\,P = (1)\,P = P, \qquad F_1^{(BC)} = N_1(L_e)\,P = (0)\,P = 0$$

The complete right-hand side for the element is therefore:

$$\mathbf{F}^{(e)} = \frac{f_0 L_e}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 1-Element Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A bar with $EA = 1000$ N, $L = 1$ m, fixed at $x = 0$, with point load $P = 50$ N at $x = 1$ m, no body force. Determine nodal displacements using a single linear Galerkin element.

**Solution:**

Stiffness matrix:

$$\mathbf{K} = \frac{1000}{1}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}$$

Load vector:

$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 50 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Apply essential BC: $u_1 = 0$ (eliminate row 1 and column 1):

$$1000\,u_2 = 50 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad u_2 = 0.05\ \text{m} = 50\ \text{mm}$$

Reaction at node 1:

$$R_1 = 1000\,(0) - 1000\,(0.05) = -50\ \text{N}\ \text{(left-pointing, as expected)}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Sanity Check:** Total external load = $+50$ N, total reaction = $-50$ N. Equilibrium satisfied. ✔

---

## 3.3 The Boundary Term — Why Natural BCs are "Free"

A key feature of the Galerkin weak form is that **natural boundary conditions appear automatically** as the boundary integral term $\left[N_i\,q\right]_{\Gamma_q}$. This is the reason that:

- A prescribed traction (force) requires **no modification** of the stiffness matrix.
- Only **essential (Dirichlet) BCs** need explicit enforcement (via row/column elimination or penalty methods).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Galerkin FE Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Strong Form PDE + BCs]) --> A1[Identify Domain and Boundary]
    A1 --> A2[Discretize Domain into Ne Elements]
    A2 --> A3[Choose Shape Functions N sub i x]
    A3 --> A4[Define Approximate Solution u super h = sum N sub i u sub i]
    A4 --> B1[Set Weight Functions W sub i = N sub i]
    B1 --> B2[Form Weighted Residual Integral]
    B2 --> B3[Integrate by Parts to Get Weak Form]
    B3 --> B4[Generate Element Stiffness K super e and Load F super e]
    B4 --> B5[Assemble Global System K u = F]
    B5 --> B6[Apply Essential BCs Dirichlet]
    B6 --> B7[Solve Linear System for Nodal u]
    B7 --> B8[Post Process: Stresses, Strains, Reactions]
    B8 --> Z0([End: Approximate Solution])

    A0:::start
    Z0:::end
    B3:::critical
    B4:::critical

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Galerkin FE Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Governing PDE Strong Form] --> P2[Weighting Function W sub i = N sub i]
    P1 --> P3[Trial Function u super h]
    P2 --> P4[Residual R = L u super h + f]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Integral over Domain int W sub i R d Omega = 0]
    P5 --> P6[Integration by Parts Weak Form]
    P6 --> P7[Element K super e and F super e]
    P7 --> P8[Assembly Global K u = F]
    P8 --> P9[Apply BCs and Solve]
    P9 --> P10[Field Output u, sigma, epsilon]

    P1:::inp
    P10:::out
    P6:::wk
    P7:::key

    classDef inp fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wk fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef key fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematic — Single Linear Bar Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1((Node 1: u1)) --- ELEM[Linear Element of Length L sub e]
    ELEM --- N2((Node 2: u2))
    ELEM --> LOAD1[Body Force f x N per m]
    N2 --> LOAD2[Tip Force P at x = L sub e]
    N1 --> BC1[Essential BC: u1 = 0]

    N1:::node
    N2:::node
    ELEM:::el
    BC1:::bc
    LOAD1:::load
    LOAD2:::load

    classDef node fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef el fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bc fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the diagram:** The left node is *constrained* (Dirichlet), the right node is *loaded* (Neumann — appears naturally in the weak form). The body force $f(x)$ is *distributed* and is integrated against $N_i$ to form the consistent load vector.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define the Galerkin method. Why is it preferred over the collocation method in FEM?**  
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]  
> The **Galerkin method** is a weighted residual technique in which the **weight functions** $W_i(x)$ are chosen to be **identical to the shape (trial) functions** $N_i(x)$ used in the approximate solution $u^h = \sum N_i u_i$.  
> It is preferred over the collocation method because:
> 1. It produces a **symmetric** stiffness matrix for self-adjoint problems, enabling efficient solvers (Cholesky, CG).
> 2. It yields **better accuracy** — the residual is forced to zero *on average* over the entire domain rather than at a few discrete points.
> 3. It coincides with the **Rayleigh–Ritz variational formulation**, providing a clear physical (energy) interpretation.
> 4. The test functions are already available as shape functions, requiring **no extra computational setup**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between the Galerkin method and the Rayleigh–Ritz method.**  
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> | **Aspect** | **Galerkin** | **Rayleigh–Ritz** |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Origin** | Weighted residual approach | Variational (energy minimization) |
> | **Starting point** | Governing PDE (strong form) | Total potential energy functional $\Pi$ |
> | **Generality** | Works for *any* differential equation (self- or non-self-adjoint) | Restricted to problems admitting a *variational principle* |
> | **Equivalence** | Equals Ritz for self-adjoint operators | A *special case* of Galerkin for self-adjoint operators |
> | **Test function** | $W_i = N_i$ | Variation $\delta u = \sum \delta u_i\,N_i$ |

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

---

### 🔹 **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the weak form of the governing equation $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(EA\,\dfrac{du}{dx}\right) + f(x) = 0$ using the Galerkin method. Clearly state the steps and the role of boundary conditions.**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Strong Form:** The governing ODE is

$$EA\,\frac{d^{2}u}{dx^{2}} + f(x) = 0, \qquad 0 \le x \le L$$

with BCs $u(0) = \bar{u}_0$ and $EA\,\dfrac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = \bar{P}_L$.

**Step 2 — Approximation:** $u^h(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j(x)\,u_j$, with $N_j$ = shape functions.

**Step 3 — Galerkin Weights:** $W_i = N_i(x)$, $i = 1, \dots, n$.

**Step 4 — Weighted Residual:**
