> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gauss Quadrature # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Gauss Quadrature** is a numerical integration technique that achieves *maximum algebraic accuracy* for a given number of function evaluations by *optimally positioning* both the sampling points (abscissas) and their associated weighting coefficients. Unlike Newton-Cotes rules which fix the sampling points at equally spaced intervals (including the endpoints), Gauss quadrature allows both the points and weights to vary freely to minimize integration error. For a one-dimensional definite integral over the canonical interval $[-1, +1]$, the Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule with **n** sampling points is stated as: $$\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, f(\xi_i)$$ where: - $\xi_i$ = **Gauss points** (abscissas) — the *optimally chosen* sampling locations - $w_i$ = **Gauss weights** — the corresponding weight coefficients - $n$ = the *order* of the quadrature rule The "Legendre" in Gauss-Legendre refers to the fact that the optimal Gauss points are precisely the **roots of the Legendre polynomials** $P_n(\xi)$ over $[-1, +1]$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Theorem — Maximum Accuracy**: An *n*-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule integrates any polynomial of degree $\le (2n - 1)$ **exactly**. This is why a 2-point rule can integrate cubics exactly — a feat impossible with the trapezoidal rule (which needs 4 points for cubics). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Sampling" Principle Imagine you are a quality-control engineer who must estimate the *average sweetness* of a mango orchard by tasting only a handful of mangoes. The naive approach (Newton-Cotes) would be to sample mangoes at the **edges** and the **center** of the orchard. But Gauss's brilliant insight says: *"Don't sample at the boundaries where you've already constrained the curve to pass through!"* Instead, Gauss says to push the sample points **slightly inward** (e.g., at $\xi = \pm 1/\sqrt{3} \approx \pm 0.577$ for a 2-point rule), so that the rule has the maximum *degrees of freedom* to match as many polynomial moments as possible. The weights then self-adjust to compensate. **Real-world engineering analogy**: In **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)**, Gauss quadrature is used to compute the **element stiffness matrices** and **load vectors**: $$[K]^e = \int_{-1}^{+1} \int_{-1}^{+1} [B]^T [D] [B] \, \vert J \vert \, d\xi \, d\eta$$ These integrals usually have *no closed-form antiderivative*, so we evaluate them **numerically** at a few clever Gauss points inside each element — gaining very high accuracy with minimal computational cost. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Quadrature"?** The word comes from Latin *quadratura* (making square). Historically, integration was viewed as finding the area (square) under a curve. In modern numerical analysis, "quadrature" simply means numerical integration. ## 1.3 Standard Gauss-Legendre Rules (Canonical Interval $[-1, +1]$) | Order $n$ | Gauss Points $\xi_i$ | Weights $w_i$ | Exact for Polynomials up to Degree | |:---------:|:---------------------|:-------------:|:-----------------------------------:| | **1** | $0$ | $2$ | 1 | | **2** | $\pm 1/\sqrt{3} \approx \pm 0.5774$ | $1, \; 1$ | 3 | | **3** | $0, \; \pm\sqrt{3/5} \approx \pm 0.7746$ | $8/9, \; 5/9, \; 5/9$ | 5 | | **4** | $\pm 0.8611, \; \pm 0.3400$ | $0.3479, \; 0.6521, \; 0.6521, \; 0.3479$ | 7 | These are the **standard metrics** you must memorize for KTU board examinations. Notice the elegant **symmetry**: weights and points always come in mirror-image pairs about $\xi = 0$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Gauss Quadrature? — The Underlying Logic The idea of Gauss quadrature can be motivated step-by-step: 1. **Starting Point**: Consider the general quadrature form over $[-1, +1]$: $$I = \int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, f(\xi_i)$$ 2. **Count the Unknowns**: This rule has **$2n$ free parameters** — the $n$ weights $w_i$ and the $n$ point locations $\xi_i$. 3. **Count the Constraints**: To force the rule to be *exact* for the lowest-degree polynomials, we require it to integrate the **monomial basis** $\{1, \xi, \xi^2, \dots, \xi^{2n-1}\}$ exactly. That gives us **$2n$ moment equations**. 4. **Solve the System**: By matching $2n$ unknowns against $2n$ constraints, we determine both points and weights uniquely. 5. **Bonus Result**: Because we matched up to degree $(2n-1)$, the rule is **automatically exact for *all* polynomials up to that degree** — a powerful bonus from polynomial linearity. ## 2.2 The Moment-Matching Equations For the rule to be exact for $f(\xi) = 1, \xi, \xi^2, \dots, \xi^{2n-1}$, we enforce: $$\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^k \, d\xi \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, \xi_i^k \quad \text{for} \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 2n-1$$ Since $\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^k \, d\xi = \dfrac{1-(-1)^{k+1}}{k+1}$ (i.e., **0** for odd $k$, **$2/(k+1)$** for even $k$), the odd-$k$ equations tell us that the Gauss points must be **symmetric about the origin**, while the even-$k$ equations fix the weights. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table and memorize it — these are the most-tested formulas in KTU board exams for Module 4.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks / Use in FEA | |:-:|:------------------|:----------:|:---------------------| | 1 | **Canonical Gauss-Legendre Rule** | $\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i f(\xi_i)$ | Standard form on $[-1, +1]$ | | 2 | **General Interval Transformation** | $\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx = \frac{b-a}{2} \int_{-1}^{+1} f\bigl(x(\xi)\bigr) \, d\xi$ | Used to map any element to canonical domain | | 3 | **Linear Map from $[-1,+1]$ to $[a,b]$** | $\displaystyle x(\xi) = \frac{b-a}{2}\xi + \frac{b+a}{2}$ | Isoparametric mapping in 1D | | 4 | **Jacobian of Linear Map** | $\displaystyle J = \frac{dx}{d\xi} = \frac{b-a}{2}$ | Constant for linear map | | 5 | **Transformed Integral** | $\displaystyle \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx \approx \frac{b-a}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i f\bigl(x(\xi_i)\bigr)$ | Working form for FEM element integration | | 6 | **$n$-point Accuracy Order** | Exact for polynomials of degree $\le 2n-1$ | Gauss beats Newton-Cotes | | 7 | **2-Point Rule (most-used)** | $\xi = \pm \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \; w_1 = w_2 = 1$ | Default for linear 1D elements | | 8 | **3-Point Rule** | $\xi_1 = 0,\; \xi_{2,3} = \pm\tfrac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{3}},\; w_1 = \tfrac{8}{9},\; w_{2,3} = \tfrac{5}{9}$ | For higher-order elements | | 9 | **2D Tensor-Product Rule** | $\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{+1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi,\eta) \, d\xi \, d\eta \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_i w_j f(\xi_i, \eta_j)$ | Standard 2D FEM integration | | 10 | **Number of Evaluations (2D)** | $n \times m$ function evaluations | For $2 \times 2$ rule: 4 evaluations | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Gauss quadrature is the **numerical backbone** of nearly every commercial FEA package (ANSYS, ABAQUS, NASTRAN, COMSOL). Its applications include: - **Element Stiffness Matrices**: $[K]^e = \int_{\Omega^e} [B]^T [D][B] \, t \, dA$ — evaluated numerically at Gauss points. - **Consistent Load Vectors**: $\{F\}^e = \int_{\Omega^e} [N]^T \, \vec{b} \, dA$. - **Stress Recovery**: Post-processing stresses are typically evaluated at Gauss points (or extrapolated to nodes). - **Reduced Integration**: Deliberately using *fewer* Gauss points than theoretically required to relieve "locking" phenomena in nearly-incompressible materials or thin beams/shells. - **Selective/Sub-integration**: Using lower-order rules on the shear terms and higher-order rules on the volumetric terms (e.g., B-bar or selective reduced integration methods). > [!NOTE] > **Why Gauss and not Simpson's Rule?** Simpson's rule is a *Newton-Cotes* formula that fixes points at endpoints. While easy to implement, it is **less efficient** — a 4-point Simpson rule has accuracy similar to a 3-point Gauss rule but with one more function evaluation. In 3D FEA, this difference becomes massive (e.g., 27 vs 8 Gauss evaluations per hex element). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the 2-Point Gauss-Legendre Rule We seek $w_1, w_2, \xi_1, \xi_2$ such that: $$\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \;=\; w_1 f(\xi_1) + w_2 f(\xi_2) \quad \text{(exact for } f = 1, \xi, \xi^2, \xi^3 \text{)}$$ ### Step 1: Set up the four moment equations Testing against monomials $1, \xi, \xi^2, \xi^3$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(i)} \quad f(\xi) = 1: \quad &\int_{-1}^{+1} 1 \, d\xi = 2 = w_1 + w_2 \\[4pt] \text{(ii)} \quad f(\xi) = \xi: \quad &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi \, d\xi = 0 = w_1 \xi_1 + w_2 \xi_2 \\[4pt] \text{(iii)} \quad f(\xi) = \xi^2: \quad &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^2 \, d\xi = \tfrac{2}{3} = w_1 \xi_1^2 + w_2 \xi_2^2 \\[4pt] \text{(iv)} \quad f(\xi) = \xi^3: \quad &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^3 \, d\xi = 0 = w_1 \xi_1^3 + w_2 \xi_2^3 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Exploit symmetry to simplify Equations (ii) and (iv) both equal **zero**. The simplest way to satisfy them simultaneously is to demand: - **Symmetry of weights**: $w_1 = w_2$ - **Antisymmetry of points**: $\xi_1 = -\xi_2$ This is because if $\xi_2 = -\xi_1$, then $w_1 \xi_1 + w_2 \xi_2 = (w_1 - w_2)\xi_1$, which forces $w_1 = w_2$ from equation (ii). Equation (iv) is then automatically satisfied. ### Step 3: Apply $w_1 = w_2$ to equation (i) $$w_1 + w_2 = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2w_1 = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{w_1 = w_2 = 1}$$ ### Step 4: Apply $\xi_2 = -\xi_1$ to equation (iii) $$w_1 \xi_1^2 + w_2 \xi_2^2 = 1 \cdot \xi_1^2 + 1 \cdot (-\xi_1)^2 = 2\xi_1^2 = \tfrac{2}{3}$$ $$\boxed{\xi_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \xi_1 = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad \xi_2 = +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}$$ ### Step 5: Verification (sanity check) — integrate $f(\xi) = \xi^4$: The 2-point rule should give an *approximation* (since degree $4 > 2n-1 = 3$). True value: $\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^4 \, d\xi = \tfrac{2}{5} = 0.4000$. $$1 \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^4 + 1 \cdot \left(+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^4 = \tfrac{1}{9} + \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$$ The error $\vert 0.2222 - 0.4000 \vert = 0.1778$ is non-zero, confirming the rule is *not* exact for degree 4 — as expected. The rule is exact up to and including degree 3. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the 3-Point Gauss-Legendre Rule We need $w_1, w_2, w_3, \xi_1, \xi_2, \xi_3$ — six parameters. We enforce exactness for $f = 1, \xi, \xi^2, \xi^3, \xi^4, \xi^5$ — six equations. ### Step 1: Set up the moment equations $$ \begin{aligned} &\int_{-1}^{+1} 1 \, d\xi = 2 = w_1 + w_2 + w_3 \\ &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi \, d\xi = 0 = w_1\xi_1 + w_2\xi_2 + w_3\xi_3 \\ &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^2 \, d\xi = \tfrac{2}{3} = w_1\xi_1^2 + w_2\xi_2^2 + w_3\xi_3^2 \\ &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^3 \, d\xi = 0 = w_1\xi_1^3 + w_2\xi_2^3 + w_3\xi_3^3 \\ &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^4 \, d\xi = \tfrac{2}{5} = w_1\xi_1^4 + w_2\xi_2^4 + w_3\xi_3^4 \\ &\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^5 \, d\xi = 0 = w_1\xi_1^5 + w_2\xi_2^5 + w_3\xi_3^5 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Exploit symmetry By the same logic as before, we expect symmetric placement. One point must lie on the origin (the axis of symmetry), and the other two are symmetric pairs: $$\boxed{\xi_1 = -\xi_3 = \xi, \quad \xi_2 = 0, \quad w_1 = w_3 = w, \quad w_2 = w_0}$$ ### Step 3: Solve using the simplified form Equations (ii), (iv), (vi) are now automatically zero (odd moments vanish by symmetry). The remaining equations become: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(i)} \quad & 2w + w_0 = 2 \\ \text{(iii)} \quad & 2w\xi^2 = \tfrac{2}{3} \;\Longrightarrow\; w\xi^2 = \tfrac{1}{3} \\ \text{(v)} \quad & 2w\xi^4 = \tfrac{2}{5} \;\Longrightarrow\; w\xi^4 = \tfrac{1}{5} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Divide equation (v) by equation (iii) to eliminate $w$ $$\frac{w\xi^4}{w\xi^2} = \frac{1/5}{1/3} \;\Longrightarrow\; \xi^2 = \frac{3}{5} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\xi = \sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}} \approx 0.7746}$$ ### Step 5: Back-substitute to find weights $$w = \frac{1}{3\xi^2} = \frac{1}{3 \cdot 3/5} = \frac{5}{9}$$ $$w_0 = 2 - 2w = 2 - \tfrac{10}{9} = \tfrac{8}{9}$$ $$\boxed{w_1 = w_3 = \tfrac{5}{9}, \quad w_2 = \tfrac{8}{9}, \quad \xi_1 = -\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}}, \quad \xi_2 = 0, \quad \xi_3 = +\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}}}$$ ### Step 6: Verification Test $f(\xi) = \xi^6$. True value: $\int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^6 \, d\xi = \tfrac{2}{7} \approx 0.2857$. Rule's prediction: $$\tfrac{5}{9}\left(-\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}}\right)^6 + \tfrac{8}{9}(0)^6 + \tfrac{5}{9}\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}}\right)^6 = \tfrac{10}{9}\left(\tfrac{3}{5}\right)^3 = \tfrac{10}{9} \cdot \tfrac{27}{125} = \tfrac{270}{1125} = \tfrac{6}{25} = 0.24$$ Error = $\vert 0.24 - 0.2857 \vert \approx 0.0457$. Non-zero as expected (degree 6 > $2 \cdot 3 - 1 = 5$). --- ## 3.3 Mapping to an Arbitrary Interval $[a, b]$ — Worked Numerical Example **Problem**: Evaluate $I = \int_{0}^{2} (x^3 + 2x) \, dx$ using 2-point Gauss quadrature. ### Step 1: Compute the exact value (for verification) $$\int_{0}^{2} (x^3 + 2x) \, dx = \left[\tfrac{x^4}{4} + x^2\right]_0^2 = \tfrac{16}{4} + 4 = 4 + 4 = 8$$ ### Step 2: Apply the coordinate transformation With $a = 0, b = 2$: $$x(\xi) = \tfrac{b-a}{2}\xi + \tfrac{b+a}{2} = \tfrac{2-0}{2}\xi + \tfrac{2+0}{2} = \xi + 1$$ $$\frac{dx}{d\xi} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad J = \tfrac{b-a}{2} = 1$$ So: $$I = \int_{-1}^{+1} \bigl[(\xi+1)^3 + 2(\xi+1)\bigr] \cdot 1 \, d\xi$$ ### Step 3: Apply 2-point Gauss rule with $\xi_1 = -1/\sqrt{3}, \xi_2 = +1/\sqrt{3}, w_1 = w_2 = 1$ $$I \approx 1 \cdot f(\xi_1) + 1 \cdot f(\xi_2) = f\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + f\!\left(+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$$ Let $\alpha = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$, so $\xi+1 = 1 \pm \alpha$. **First evaluation** ($\xi_1 = -\alpha$, $x_1 = 1-\alpha \approx 0.4226$): $$f(\xi_1) = (0.4226)^3 + 2(0.4226) = 0.0754 + 0.8452 = 0.9206$$ **Second evaluation** ($\xi_2 = +\alpha$, $x_2 = 1+\alpha \approx 1.5774$): $$f(\xi_2) = (1.5774)^3 + 2(1.5774) = 3.9246 + 3.1548 = 7.0794$$ **Sum**: $I \approx 0.9206 + 7.0794 = 8.0000$ ### Step 4: Compare with exact value The 2-point Gauss rule gives $I = 8.0000$, **matching the exact value to 4 decimal places** — confirming the theoretical guarantee, since $f(x) = x^3 + 2x$ is a cubic (degree $\le 3 = 2n-1$). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Derived Rules | Rule $n$ | Points $\xi_i$ | Weights $w_i$ | Legendre Polynomial $P_n(\xi)$ | Proof Strategy | |:--------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:------------------------------:|:--------------| | 1 | $0$ | $2$ | $P_1 = \xi$ | One equation: $w_1 = 2$ | | 2 | $\pm 1/\sqrt{3}$ | $1, 1$ | $P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(3\xi^2 - 1)$ | Symmetry + 2 unknowns | | 3 | $0, \pm\sqrt{3/5}$ | $8/9, 5/9, 5/9$ | $P_3 = \tfrac{1}{2}(5\xi^3 - 3\xi)$ | Symmetry + 3 unknowns | | 4 | $\pm 0.8611, \pm 0.3400$ | $0.3479, 0.6521, 0.6521, 0.3479$ | $P_4 = \tfrac{1}{8}(35\xi^4 - 30\xi^2 + 3)$ | Roots of quartic Legendre polynomial | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip**: For higher-order rules (n ≥ 4), KTU examiners rarely ask you to derive weights from scratch. Instead, they expect you to *quote* the standard table and *apply* it to a specific integral. Focus your study energy on the 2-point and 3-point derivations shown above. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Gauss Quadrature in the FEA Pipeline The diagram below shows where Gauss Quadrature fits inside the broader Finite Element Analysis workflow: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Problem Definition: PDE / Physical Domain"] --> B["Domain Discretization into Elements"] B --> C["Element-Level Formulation"] C --> D["Element Stiffness Matrix K^e"] D --> D1["Identifies Integral Forms:
∫[B]^T[D][B] t dA"] D1 --> E["Coordinate Mapping
x → ξ in [-1,+1]"] E --> F{"Apply Gauss
Quadrature"} F --> F1["Select Gauss Points ξ_i
and Weights w_i"] F1 --> F2["Loop i = 1 to n:
Compute Jacobian J_i"] F2 --> F3["Evaluate [B], [D]
at each ξ_i"] F3 --> F4["Accumulate:
K^e += w_i * |J| * [B]^T[D][B]"] F4 --> G["Assemble Global K
and Solve [K]{u} = {F}"] G --> H["Post-Process:
Stresses, Strains"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Gauss Point Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"] A1["Element Type
(1D/2D/3D)"] --> A2["Polynomial Order p
of Shape Functions"] end subgraph DECISION["DECISION STAGE"] B1{"Required
Accuracy?"} B2["Choose n via:
2n - 1 ≥ p"] B3["Look up Standard
Gauss Table"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE"] C1["Gauss Points ξ_i"] C2["Weights w_i"] C3["Sampling Locations
in Physical Element"] end INPUT --> DECISION B1 --> B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B3 --> C3 ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Schematic — Gauss vs. Newton-Cotes Sampling ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NC["NEWTON-COTES (e.g., Simpson)"] N1["ξ = -1.00"]:::ncnode N2["ξ = -0.50"]:::ncnode N3["ξ = 0.00"]:::ncnode N4["ξ = +0.50"]:::ncnode N5["ξ = +1.00"]:::ncnode N1 --- N2 --- N3 --- N4 --- N5 end subgraph GQ["GAUSS-LEGENDRE (2-point)"] G1["ξ = -0.577"]:::gqnode G2["ξ = +0.577"]:::gqnode G1 === G2 end classDef ncnode fill:#fde0dd,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef gqnode fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000 ``` **Reading the schematic**: Note how Gauss points are *pulled inward* from the endpoints ($-1, +1$), whereas Newton-Cotes *anchors* points at the endpoints. This inward pull is what gives Gauss quadrature its superior accuracy-per-evaluation. ## 4.4 Visual Map of Gauss Points on a Canonical Square Element ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SQ["Canonical 2D Element [-1,+1] x [-1,+1] — 2x2 Gauss Grid"] P1["(-0.577, +0.577) w=1"] P2["(+0.577, +0.577) w=1"] P3["(-0.577, -0.577) w=1"] P4["(+0.577, -0.577) w=1"] P1 --- P2 P3 --- P4 P1 --- P3 P2 --- P4 end ``` **Interpretation**: For a 2D bilinear (Q4) element, the standard $2 \times 2$ Gauss rule places four sample points inside the square (NOT at corners, edges, or center). The product $w \cdot w = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$ is the combined weight at each point. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"State the 2-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule. What is its order of accuracy, and why is it preferred over the trapezoidal rule in FEM applications?"** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > The 2-point Gauss-Legendre rule over $[-1, +1]$ is: > $$\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx f\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + f\!\left(+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$$ > with Gauss points $\xi_1 = -1/\sqrt{3}, \; \xi_2 = +1/\sqrt{3}$ and equal weights $w_1 = w_2 = 1$. > > **Order of accuracy**: The rule integrates polynomials up to degree $2n-1 = 3$ *exactly*. Hence it has *cubic* (third-order) accuracy. > > **Why preferred over trapezoidal rule in FEM**: > (i) It uses the same number of function evaluations (2) but achieves *cubic* accuracy versus only *linear* accuracy for the trapezoidal rule. > (ii) It does not require evaluating $f$ at the endpoints, which is advantageous when the integrand (e.g., $[B]^T[D][B] \cdot \vert J\vert$) is singular or poorly behaved near element boundaries. > (iii) It allows *reduced integration* schemes that suppress volumetric locking in plasticity problems. --- ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"Explain why the Gauss points of an $n$-point quadrature rule are chosen as the roots of the Legendre polynomial $P_n(\xi)$."** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: > **Conceptual reason**: The Legendre polynomial $P_n(\xi)$ is orthogonal to all polynomials of degree $< n$ on $[-1, +1]$ with respect to the uniform weight function $w(\xi) = 1$. Choosing its roots as Gauss points guarantees that the quadrature rule will satisfy the moment-matching equations with maximum efficiency. > > **Mathematical reason**: A polynomial $q(\xi)$ of degree $\le n-1$ can be expressed via polynomial division with respect to $P_n(\xi)$: > $$q(\xi) = P_n(\xi) \cdot s(\xi) + r(\xi)$$ > where $\deg(r) < n$. If $\xi_i$ are the $n$ roots of $P_n$, then $\sum_i w_i q(\xi_i) = \sum_i w_i r(\xi_i)$. Since the weights are chosen so that the rule is exact for all $r(\xi)$ of degree $< n$ (giving $n$ equations), and the points/weights also satisfy orthogonality (the remaining $n$ equations), the rule becomes exact for all polynomials up to degree $2n-1$. > > **Conclusion**: The roots of $P_n$ are the *unique* choice that satisfies the $2n$ moment conditions while keeping all $\xi_i \in (-1, +1)$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note**: As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, *Module 4 internal choice* allows the candidate to attempt **either Question B.1 OR Question B.2**. --- ### **Question B.1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Derive the 2-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature formula. Show all four moment equations and solve them explicitly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the 2-point Gauss quadrature rule, evaluate the integral $\displaystyle I = \int_{1}^{3} (x^2 + 1) \, dx$. Compare with the exact value and comment on the error. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for B.1(a)** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1 (1 mark)** — State the general form: $$I = \int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx w_1 f(\xi_1) + w_2 f(\xi_2)$$ **Step 2 (3 marks)** — Write the four moment-matching equations by demanding exactness for $f(\xi) = 1, \xi, \xi^2, \xi^3$: $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-1}^{+1} 1 \, d\xi &= 2 = w_1 + w_2 \\ \int_{-1}^{+1} \xi \, d\xi &= 0 = w_1\xi_1 + w_2\xi_2 \\ \int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^2 \, d\xi &= \tfrac{2}{3} = w_1\xi_1^2 + w_2\xi_2^2 \\ \int_{-1}^{+1} \xi^3 \, d\xi &= 0 = w_1\xi_1^3 + w_2\xi_2^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks)** — Apply the symmetry argument: for odd-moment equations to vanish, we require $w_1 = w_2$ and $\xi_2 = -\xi_1$. Then equation (i) gives $2w_1 = 2 \Rightarrow w_1 = 1$. **Step 4 (1 mark)** — Equation (iii) becomes $2\xi_1^2 = \tfrac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \xi_1 = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \xi_2 = +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$. **Final Result (must be boxed)**: $$\boxed{\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx f\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + f\!\left(+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)}$$ This rule is exact for polynomials up to degree $2(2)-1 = 3$. #### **Model Solution for B.1(b)** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1 (1 mark)** — Compute exact value: $$I_{\text{exact}} = \int_{1}^{3} (x^2 + 1) \, dx = \left[\tfrac{x^3}{3} + x\right]_1^3 = \left(9 + 3\right) - \left(\tfrac{1}{3} + 1\right) = 12 - \tfrac{4}{3} = \tfrac{32}{3} \approx 10.6667$$ **Step 2 (1 mark)** — Apply the coordinate transformation with $a = 1, b = 3$: $$x(\xi) = \tfrac{b-a}{2}\xi + \tfrac{b+a}{2} = 1 \cdot \xi + 2 = \xi + 2, \qquad \frac{dx}{d\xi} = 1$$ **Step 3 (2 marks)** — Compute Gauss points in physical coordinates: $$x_1 = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} + 2 \approx 1.4226, \qquad x_2 = +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} + 2 \approx 2.5774$$ **Step 4 (2 marks)** — Evaluate the integrand: $$f(x_1) = (1.4226)^2 + 1 = 2.0238 + 1 = 3.0238$$ $$f(x_2) = (2.5774)^2 + 1 = 6.6430 + 1 = 7.6430$$ **Step 5 (1 mark)** — Apply the quadrature formula with $\tfrac{b-a}{2} = 1$: $$I_{\text{approx}} = 1 \cdot (3.0238 + 7.6430) = 10.6668 \approx \tfrac{32}{3}$$ **Conclusion**: Error = $\vert 10.6667 - 10.6668 \vert \approx 10^{-4}$, which is purely due to rounding. Theoretically, the error is **zero** because $f(x) = x^2 + 1$ is a polynomial of degree 2, well within the rule's capability of degree 3. --- ### **Question B.2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Derive the 3-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule. Clearly state the six moment equations and show step-by-step how the weights and points are obtained using symmetry arguments. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute $\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{+1} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi \xi}{2}\right) d\xi$ using both the 2-point and 3-point Gauss quadrature rules. Compare with the exact value $\tfrac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.2732$ and discuss the convergence behavior. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for B.2(a)** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1 (1 mark)** — State the 3-point rule with 6 unknowns $(w_0, w_1, w_2, \xi_0, \xi_1, \xi_2)$: $$I = \int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx w_1 f(\xi_1) + w_2 f(\xi_2) + w_3 f(\xi_3)$$ **Step 2 (2 marks)** — Write six moment equations for monomials $1, \xi, \xi^2, \xi^3, \xi^4, \xi^5$. Show that odd-moment integrals vanish by symmetry. **Step 3 (1 mark)** — Apply symmetry: $\xi_2 = 0, \; \xi_1 = -\xi_3, \; w_1 = w_3$. This reduces to 3 unknowns $(w_0, w_1, \xi)$ with 3 equations: $$2w_1 + w_0 = 2, \qquad 2w_1\xi^2 = \tfrac{2}{3}, \qquad 2w_1\xi^4 = \tfrac{2}{5}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks)** — Divide equation (v) by equation (iii) to eliminate $w_1$: $$\xi^2 = \tfrac{3}{5} \;\Longrightarrow\; \xi = \sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}} \approx 0.7746$$ **Step 5 (1 mark)** — Back-substitute: $w_1 = \tfrac{5}{9}$, $w_0 = 2 - \tfrac{10}{9} = \tfrac{8}{9}$. **Final Result (must be boxed)**: $$\boxed{\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx \tfrac{5}{9} f\!\left(-\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}}\right) + \tfrac{8}{9} f(0) + \tfrac{5}{9} f\!\left(+\sqrt{\tfrac{3}{5}}\right)}$$ Exact for polynomials up to degree $2(3) - 1 = 5$. #### **Model Solution for B.2(b)** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1 (1 mark)** — Verify the exact value: $$\int_{-1}^{+1} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi\xi}{2}\right) d\xi = \left[\tfrac{2}{\pi}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi\xi}{2}\right)\right]_{-1}^{+1} = \tfrac{2}{\pi}\left[\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) - \sin\!\left(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\right] = \tfrac{2}{\pi}(1 - (-1)) = \tfrac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.2732$$ **Step 2 (2 marks)** — **2-Point Gauss Rule** with $\xi = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$: $$f(\xi_1) = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\right) = \cos(-0.9069) = 0.6168$$ $$f(\xi_2) = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \left(+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\right) = \cos(0.9069) = 0.6168$$ $$I_{2pt} = 0.6168 + 0.6168 = 1.2336$$ Error $= \vert 1.2732 - 1.2336 \vert = 0.0396$ (about 3.1% relative error). **Step 3 (3 marks)** — **3-Point Gauss Rule** with $\xi = 0, \pm\sqrt{3/5}$: For $\xi_1 = -\sqrt{3/5} = -0.7746$: $$f(\xi_1) = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}(-0.7746)\right) = \cos(-1.2168) = 0.3473$$ For $\xi_2 = 0$: $$f(\xi_2) = \cos(0) = 1.0000$$ For $\xi_3 = +\sqrt{3/5} = +0.7746$: $$f(\xi_3) = \cos(1.2168) = 0.3473$$ $$I_{3pt} = \tfrac{5}{9}(0.3473) + \tfrac{8}{9}(1.0000) + \tfrac{5}{9}(0.3473) = 0.1929 + 0.8889 + 0.1929 = 1.2747$$ Error $= \vert 1.2732 - 1.2747 \vert = 0.0015$ (about 0.12% relative error). **Step 4 (1 mark)** — **Discussion of convergence**: The error dropped from $\sim 3.1\%$ (2-point) to $\sim 0.12\%$ (3-point) — a **26× improvement** with just one extra function evaluation. This demonstrates the *exponential-like* convergence of Gauss quadrature for smooth integrands. The 3-point rule achieves this because the integrand's Taylor expansion contains polynomials of all degrees, and the rule is exact up to degree 5. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**: > 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian factor** $\tfrac{b-a}{2}$ when transforming from $[-1,+1]$ to $[a,b]$. This single omission causes the entire answer to be wrong by a constant multiplier — typical 4-mark loss. > 2. **Confusing Gauss points with Newton-Cotes points**. Students often write $\xi = -1, 0, +1$ (Simpson's rule points) instead of $\xi = 0, \pm\sqrt{3/5}$ (Gauss points). Marks deducted: 3-4. > 3. **Using Gauss weights but evaluating at wrong coordinates**. Always remember: weights $w_i$ multiply $f$ evaluated at the *physical* Gauss point $x_i = \tfrac{b-a}{2}\xi_i + \tfrac{b+a}{2}$, NOT at the canonical $\xi_i$. > 4. **Omitting units / units of measure** in tabular answers. KTU examiners often allocate 0.5 marks for clean notation. > 5. **Forgetting to write the integral bounds**. State clearly that the canonical rule is on $[-1, +1]$ before transformation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall. - ✅ **Canonical Rule**: Gauss-Legendre operates on $[-1, +1]$; $\int_{-1}^{+1} f(\xi) \, d\xi \approx \sum w_i f(\xi_i)$. - ✅ **Accuracy Theorem**: $n$-point rule is exact for polynomials of degree $\le 2n - 1$. - ✅ **2-Point Rule (memorize verbatim)**: $\xi = \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$, $w = 1, 1$. Cubic accuracy. - ✅ **3-Point Rule (memorize verbatim)**: $\xi = 0, \pm\sqrt{3/5}$, $w = 8/9, 5/9, 5/9$. Quintic accuracy. - ✅ **Transformation Formula**: For arbitrary $[a, b]$, use $x = \tfrac{b-a}{2}\xi + \tfrac{b+a}{2}$, $dx = \tfrac{b-a}{2} d\xi$. - ✅ **Working Formula**: $$\int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx \;\approx\; \tfrac{b-a}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, f\!\left(\tfrac{b-a}{2}\xi_i + \tfrac{b+a}{2}\right)$$ - ✅ **Why NOT Newton-Cotes?**: Gauss moves points off the endpoints, giving higher accuracy per function evaluation. - ✅ **Why Gauss points are Legendre roots**: Orthogonality condition guarantees maximum algebraic precision. - ✅ **Symmetry**: Gauss points and weights are always symmetric about $\xi = 0$. - ✅ **Application in FEM**: Used for computing $[K]^e$, $\{F\}^e$, and any element-level integrals over arbitrary-shaped domains via isoparametric mapping. - ✅ **2D Extension**: Tensor-product rule $\sum_i \sum_j w_i w_j f(\xi_i, \eta_j)$; $n \times n$ evaluations. - ✅ **Pitfall — Jacobian**: Always multiply each term by $\tfrac{b-a}{2}$ (1D) or $\vert J \vert$ (2D/3D). - ✅ **Pitfall — Physical vs Canonical**: Weights always multiply $f$ at physical coordinates $x_i$, not at $\xi_i$. - ✅ **Reduced Integration**: Using fewer Gauss points than theoretically required is a *legitimate* technique to relieve locking — do not confuse with "approximation error." - ✅ **KTU 2024 Weightage Expectation**: Module 4 typically carries **15–20%** of total marks; Gauss Quadrature alone can appear as a full 14-mark question.

topics.title: Requirements for Isoparametric Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Requirements for Isoparametric Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **isoparametric element** is a finite element in which the *same set of shape functions* (interpolation functions) is used to define both the **element geometry** (coordinates of any interior point as a function of nodal coordinates) and the **field variable** (displacement, temperature, etc.) within the element. The word *iso* (Greek: *equal*) and *parametric* (with respect to parameters/nodes) literally mean *"equal parameters for both geometry and field"*.

For a 2D isoparametric element with nodes $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$, the dual representation is:

$$x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i, \quad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, y_i$$

$$u(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, u_i, \quad v(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, v_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight — Isoparametric Family Classification**
> - **Isoparametric**: Same shape functions for geometry and field (e.g., 4-noded quad, 8-noded quad, 9-noded quad).
> - **Subparametric**: Geometry is represented by lower-order shape functions than the field.
> - **Superparametric**: Geometry is represented by higher-order shape functions than the field.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Stamp & Curved Pad" Model

Imagine you are a sign-board maker. You have a **perfect square rubber stamp** (your *parent/master element* in the natural coordinate system $(\xi,\eta)$ — the unit square $[-1,1]\times[-1,1]$). You also have a piece of soft, stretchable rubber pad in the shape of a **trapezoid** (your *distorted global element* in real $(x,y)$ space).

You press the rubber stamp onto the curved pad. The ink pattern (the *shape functions* $N_i$) on the stamp does not change — but it now sits on a curved surface. The mathematical rule that maps the straight grid on the stamp to the curved grid on the pad is the **isoparametric mapping**. The "rules" that ensure the inked image (your field variable) behaves correctly — does not tear, does not leave gaps, and prints solid color evenly — are the **requirements for isoparametric elements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of Isoparametric Element Validity (KTU 2024 Expected)**
> 1. **Completeness** — Must reproduce rigid body motion and constant strain states.
> 2. **Compatibility** — Field variable must be continuous across element boundaries ($C_0$ continuity for displacements).
> 3. **Coordinate Mapping Validity** — The Jacobian matrix must be non-singular with $\det(J) > 0$ everywhere inside the element.
> 4. **Patch Test Passability** — Assemblage of elements must pass the patch test for guaranteed convergence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Mandatory Requirements for a Valid Isoparametric Element

A finite element — whether isoparametric or not — must satisfy a hierarchy of requirements for the FEA solution to converge to the exact answer as the mesh is refined. For **isoparametric elements** (KTU Module 4), these are:

#### Requirement 1 — Completeness (Constant-Strain Patch Condition)

The shape functions must be able to represent:
- A **constant translation** (rigid body motion) of the element.
- A **constant strain state** (linear displacement field) within the element.

Formally, for a polynomial basis of order $p$ in 2D:

$$1,\ \xi,\ \eta \in \text{span}\{N_i(\xi,\eta)\} \quad \text{(rigid body + constant strain basis included)}$$

Equivalently:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi,\eta) = 1 \quad \text{(partition of unity)}$$

#### Requirement 2 — Compatibility (Conformity / $C_0$ Continuity)

Across an element boundary, the field variable (e.g., displacement) must be **continuous** between adjacent elements. For isoparametric elements, this is automatically satisfied when:
- Shape functions are defined such that $N_i = 0$ on all edges *not* containing node $i$.
- Along any shared edge, the displacement is interpolated *only* by the shape functions of the nodes on that edge.

This is the **inter-element continuity condition**.

#### Requirement 3 — Isoparametric Mapping Validity (Jacobian Condition)

The mapping from the parent (natural) domain $(\xi,\eta)$ to the global (physical) domain $(x,y)$ must be **one-to-one** and **non-degenerate**. The Jacobian matrix must satisfy:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{with} \quad \det(J) > 0 \ \ \forall (\xi,\eta) \in \text{element}$$

If $\det(J) \leq 0$ at any point, the element is **inverted** (inside-out) and the stiffness matrix becomes singular or negative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Examiner Favorite)**
> For a quadrilateral isoparametric element, $\det(J)$ should ideally **not change sign** within the element, and the element should not have interior angles exceeding $180^\circ$ (no concave corners).

#### Requirement 4 — Proper Numerical Integration (Gauss Quadrature Order)

The element stiffness integral in global coordinates,

$$K = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} B^T(\xi,\eta)\, D\, B(\xi,\eta)\, \det(J(\xi,\eta))\, t\, d\xi\, d\eta$$

cannot be evaluated analytically in general. **Gauss–Legendre quadrature** is used:

$$\int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} f(\xi,\eta)\, d\xi\, d\eta \;\approx\; \sum_{i=1}^{n_g}\sum_{j=1}^{n_g} w_i w_j\, f(\xi_i,\eta_j)$$

**Required integration order for exactness**:

| Element Type | Polynomial Degree in $B$ | Minimum Gauss Points (Full Integration) |
|---|---|---|
| 4-noded bilinear (Q4) | 1 | $2 \times 2 = 4$ |
| 8-noded serendipity (Q8) | 2 | $3 \times 3 = 9$ |
| 9-noded Lagrangian (Q9) | 2 | $3 \times 3 = 9$ |
| 3-noded triangle (T3) | 1 | 1 (centroid) |
| 6-noded triangle (T6) | 2 | 3 |

#### Requirement 5 — Patch Test Passability (Convergence Safeguard)

The **patch test** (Irons, 1963) is the most general convergence test. A patch of elements under:
- **Constant strain** mode (prescribed displacements of all boundary nodes consistent with a single constant strain) — must reproduce that constant strain exactly.
- **Rigid body mode** — must yield zero strain energy.

Passing the patch test is *necessary and sufficient* for convergence in most cases.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Memorization for KTU University Exam — Isoparametric Element Formulation**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Geometry mapping | $x = \sum N_i x_i,\ y = \sum N_i y_i$ | $N_i$ = shape functions in $(\xi,\eta)$ |
| 2 | Field mapping | $u = \sum N_i u_i,\ v = \sum N_i v_i$ | Isoparametric = same $N_i$ |
| 3 | Jacobian matrix $J$ | $J_{11}=\sum (\partial N_i/\partial \xi)\,x_i$ | Chain rule for derivatives |
| 4 | Jacobian determinant | $\det J = J_{11}J_{22} - J_{12}J_{21}$ | Must be $>0$ |
| 5 | Strain–displacement $B$ in iso-coords | $B = J^{-1} \cdot \nabla_{\!\xi\eta} N$ | Where $\nabla_{\!\xi\eta}N = [\partial N/\partial \xi\ \ \partial N/\partial \eta]^T$ |
| 6 | Differential area | $dA = \det(J)\, d\xi\, d\eta$ | Key for stiffness integration |
| 7 | Element stiffness | $K^e = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} B^T D B \det J\, t\, d\xi\, d\eta$ | $t$ = thickness |
| 8 | Gauss quadrature | $\int_{-1}^{1} f d\xi \approx \sum_{i} w_i f(\xi_i)$ | 2-pt: $\pm 1/\sqrt{3},\ w=1$; 3-pt: $0,\pm\sqrt{3/5},\ w=5/9,8/9,5/9$ |
| 9 | Partition of unity | $\sum N_i(\xi,\eta) = 1$ | Completeness test |
| 10 | Boundary condition | $N_i = 0$ on edges not containing node $i$ | Conformity test |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Isoparametric elements are the **workhorse of modern CAE**. They appear in:
- **Automotive crash simulation** (LS-DYNA, Abaqus/Explicit) — distorted 8-noded hexahedra in crumple zones.
- **Aerospace CFD/FEA** (Nastran, Ansys Mechanical) — curved-shell isoparametric shells (S8R, S9R5) for wing skins.
- **Geotechnical analysis** (Plaxis, Abaqus) — distorted 6-noded triangles / 15-noded wedges for soil domains.
- **Biomedical FEA** — patient-specific meshes of bone from CT scans using isoparametric tetrahedra.

The ability to map a *curved, irregular* engineering geometry to a *regular* computational parent domain is what makes commercial FEA possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Jacobian Matrix for an Isoparametric Element

We start from the geometry mapping in the natural coordinate system $(\xi, \eta)$:

$$x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, x_i, \qquad y(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta)\, y_i$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $x$ with respect to $\xi$**:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \xi}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi,\eta)\, x_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\, x_i$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $x$ with respect to $\eta$**:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}\, x_i$$

**Step 3 — Apply the same to $y$**:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\, y_i, \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}\, y_i$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the Jacobian matrix**:

$$J(\xi,\eta) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\,x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\,y_i \\[8pt] \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}\,x_i & \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}\,y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Determinant (the key invariant for area/volume change)**:

$$\det J = \left(\sum_{i}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\,x_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}\,y_i\right) - \left(\sum_{i}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\,y_i\right)\!\left(\sum_{i}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}\,x_i\right)$$

$$\det J = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} - \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta}$$

**Step 6 — Geometric meaning**: $\det J$ represents the **local area ratio** between the differential patch $d\xi\, d\eta$ in parent space and the differential patch $dx\, dy$ in global space:

$$dx\, dy = \det J \, d\xi\, d\eta$$

For a *valid* mapping, $\det J > 0$ for all $(\xi,\eta)$ inside the element.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Jacobian for a 4-Noded Bilinear Quad (Q4)

**Given:** A 4-noded isoparametric element with nodal coordinates
$(x_1, y_1)=(0,0)$, $(x_2, y_2)=(4,0)$, $(x_3, y_3)=(4,3)$, $(x_4, y_4)=(0,3)$.

The Q4 shape functions are:

$$N_1 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)$$
$$N_3 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta), \quad N_4 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivatives of $N_i$**:

$$\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(1-\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \eta} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)$$

$$\frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi} = +\tfrac{1}{4}(1-\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \eta} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)$$

$$\frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi} = +\tfrac{1}{4}(1+\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \eta} = +\tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)$$

$$\frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(1+\eta), \quad \frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \eta} = +\tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the element centroid $(\xi,\eta) = (0,0)$**:

$$\left.\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}, \quad \left.\frac{\partial N_1}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\tfrac{1}{4}, \quad \left.\frac{\partial N_2}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\tfrac{1}{4}, \quad \left.\frac{\partial N_3}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\tfrac{1}{4}$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \xi}\right|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}, \quad \left.\frac{\partial N_4}{\partial \eta}\right|_{(0,0)} = +\tfrac{1}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Build the Jacobian entries**:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi}\bigg|_{(0,0)} = \sum \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\,x_i = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(4) + \tfrac{1}{4}(4) - \tfrac{1}{4}(0) = 2$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta}\bigg|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) - \tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(3) + \tfrac{1}{4}(3) = 1.5$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi}\bigg|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(3) - \tfrac{1}{4}(3) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta}\bigg|_{(0,0)} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) - \tfrac{1}{4}(4) + \tfrac{1}{4}(4) + \tfrac{1}{4}(0) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Jacobian at center**:

$$J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \det J = (2)(1.5) - (0)(0) = 3.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Verification**
> The element is a $4 \times 3 = 12\ \text{unit}^2$ rectangle. The parent domain has area $(2)(2)=4$. Ratio $= 12/4 = 3$. This equals $\det J$ — confirming the formula. $\det J > 0$ implies a **valid, non-inverted** mapping. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation of the Isoparametric Stiffness Integral

The strain field is $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = B\mathbf{d}^e$, where $B$ is the strain–displacement matrix. For a 2D problem (plane stress/strain):

$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial u/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial v/\partial y \\ \partial u/\partial y & \partial v/\partial x \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Chain rule for derivatives**:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial \xi \\ \partial u/\partial \eta \end{Bmatrix} = J \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial x \\ \partial u/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial x \\ \partial u/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = J^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \partial u/\partial \xi \\ \partial u/\partial \eta \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Express $B$ in natural coordinates**:

$$B = \tilde{B}\, J^{-1}, \quad \text{where} \quad \tilde{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_1/\partial \xi & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial \xi & 0 & \cdots \\ 0 & \partial N_1/\partial \eta & 0 & \partial N_2/\partial \eta & \cdots \\ \partial N_1/\partial \eta & \partial N_1/\partial \xi & \partial N_2/\partial \eta & \partial N_2/\partial \xi & \cdots \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Strain energy and stiffness**:

$$U^e = \tfrac{1}{2} \int_{V^e} \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T D\, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\, dV = \tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{d}^e)^T \left[ \int_{V^e} B^T D B\, dV \right] \mathbf{d}^e$$

**Step 4 — Change of variables** ($dV = t\, dx\, dy = t\, \det J\, d\xi\, d\eta$):

$$K^e = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} B^T(\xi,\eta)\, D\, B(\xi,\eta)\, \det J(\xi,\eta)\, t\, d\xi\, d\eta$$

This is the canonical isoparametric stiffness form used in *every* commercial FEA code.

### 3.4 Distortion & Validity Limits (Analytical Matrix)

The following matrix summarizes the **diagnostic checks** a KTU examiner expects you to perform for any isoparametric element before analysis:

| Diagnostic Check | Mathematical Test | Pass Criterion | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partition of unity | $\sum N_i(\xi,\eta) - 1 = 0$ | $= 0$ identically | Rigid body motion error |
| Constant strain | $B \cdot \mathbf{d}_{\text{const}} = \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{\text{const}}$ | Reproduces exactly | Constant strain error (spurious) |
| Jacobian sign | $\det J(\xi,\eta)$ | $> 0$ everywhere | Inverted / singular element |
| Edge orientation | Traverse edges with $\xi = \pm 1$ and $\eta = \pm 1$ | Consistent CCW orientation | Sign errors in $B$ |
| Integration order | $n_g \geq p + 1$ per direction | Met | Inaccurate $K^e$ (rank deficient) |
| Patch test | Apply constant strain BCs | Strain constant across patch | Convergence failure |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Isoparametric Element Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Engineering Geometry\nIrregular, Curved Domain in x,y] --> B[Mesh Generator\nDivide into Elements]
    B --> C[Element Type Selection\nQ4, Q8, Q9, T3, T6]
    C --> D[Parent Element Definition\nUnit Square -1,1 in xi,eta]
    D --> E[Shape Function Library\nN_i xi,eta same for geometry and field]
    E --> F[Isoparametric Mapping\nx = Sum N_i x_i, u = Sum N_i u_i]
    F --> G{Jacobian\nValidity Test}
    G -->|det J > 0| H[Compute B Matrix\nB = J inverse times Grad N]
    G -->|det J <= 0| X1[REJECT ELEMENT\nInverted or Degenerate]
    H --> I[Gauss Quadrature\nSelect n_g points]
    I --> J[Numerical Integration\nK^e = int B^T D B det J t dxi deta]
    J --> K[Assemble Global K and F]
    K --> L[Apply BCs and Solve Ku = F]
    L --> M[Post-Process\nStress, Strain, Displacement]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Isoparametric Element Requirements

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[Isoparametric Element Validity]
    R0 --> R1[Requirement 1\nCompleteness]
    R0 --> R2[Requirement 2\nCompatibility C0]
    R0 --> R3[Requirement 3\nJacobian Positivity]
    R0 --> R4[Requirement 4\nAdequate Integration]
    R0 --> R5[Requirement 5\nPatch Test Pass]

    R1 --> R1a[Partition of Unity\nSum N_i equals 1]
    R1 --> R1b[Linear Field Recovery\nconstant strain mode]

    R2 --> R2a[Edge shape functions vanish off edge]
    R2 --> R2b[Shared edges interpolate same field]

    R3 --> R3a[det J greater than 0 everywhere]
    R3 --> R3b[No interior angle above 180 deg]

    R4 --> R4a[Full integration for accuracy]
    R4 --> R4b[Reduced integration for efficiency]

    R5 --> R5a[Constant strain patch test]
    R5 --> R5b[Rigid body mode zero energy]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Isoparametric Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        A1[Nodal Coordinates x_i, y_i] --> CORE
        A2[Nodal DOFs u_i, v_i] --> CORE
        A3[Material Matrix D] --> CORE
    end

    subgraph CORE[CORE FORMULATION BLOCK]
        B1[Shape Function Library N_i xi,eta]
        B2[Jacobian Builder J = dx/dxi]
        B3[B Matrix Assembler B = Jinv Grad N]
        B4[Stiffness Integrator K = int BtDBdetJ dxi deta]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        C1[Element Stiffness K^e] --> SOLVER
        C2[Element Force Vector F^e] --> SOLVER
    end

    SOLVER[Global Solver\nKu = F] --> RESULT[Solution u, sigma, epsilon]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define an isoparametric element. How does it differ from subparametric and superparametric formulations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An isoparametric element is one in which the **same shape functions** $N_i(\xi,\eta)$ are used to interpolate both the **element geometry** ($x = \sum N_i x_i$, $y = \sum N_i y_i$) and the **field variable** ($u = \sum N_i u_i$, $v = \sum N_i v_i$).

- **Isoparametric**: same order of $N_i$ for both geometry and field.
- **Subparametric**: lower-order $N_i$ for geometry than for field.
- **Superparametric**: higher-order $N_i$ for geometry than for field.

*Mark distribution: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for differences.*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the conditions for $\det J > 0$ and explain its significance in isoparametric element formulation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Jacobian matrix is $J = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x/\partial \xi & \partial y/\partial \xi \\ \partial x/\partial \eta & \partial y/\partial \eta \end{bmatrix}$.

**Significance:**
1. $\det J > 0$ ensures the mapping from $(\xi,\eta)$ to $(x,y)$ is **one-to-one and orientation-preserving** (no element inversion).
2. $\det J$ gives the **local area/volume scaling factor**: $dx\,dy = \det J\, d\xi\,d\eta$.
3. If $\det J \leq 0$, the element stiffness matrix $K^e$ becomes **singular or indefinite**, leading to non-physical results.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module-Internal Choice Format**

#### **Question A — (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** State and explain the **five essential requirements** for a valid isoparametric element. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** For a 4-noded bilinear isoparametric element with nodes at $(0,0)$, $(5,0)$, $(5,4)$, and $(0,4)$, determine the **Jacobian matrix** at the centre $(\xi, \eta) = (0, 0)$ and verify the $\det J$ condition. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Completeness (1.5 marks)**: The shape functions must include the polynomial basis for rigid body motion (constants) and constant strain (linear terms). Mathematically, $\sum N_i = 1$ and $\sum N_i \xi_i = \xi$ at every point.

2. **Compatibility / Conformity (1.5 marks)**: Displacements must be continuous across element edges ($C^0$ continuity). This requires that on any edge, the shape functions of nodes not on that edge vanish identically.

3. **Coordinate Mapping Validity (1 mark)**: The Jacobian $J = \partial(x,y)/\partial(\xi,\eta)$ must be non-singular, i.e., $\det J > 0$ throughout the element. This ensures the element is not folded or inverted.

4. **Adequate Numerical Integration (1.5 marks)**: Gauss quadrature must integrate $B^T D B \det J$ exactly. For an element of order $p$, the order of Gauss integration $n_g$ must be at least $p+1$ per direction (full integration).

5. **Patch Test Passability (1.5 marks)**: The element must reproduce constant strain states exactly and produce zero strain energy under rigid body motion when assembled in a patch — the most general convergence test by Irons (1963).

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $P_1(0,0)$, $P_2(5,0)$, $P_3(5,4)$, $P_4(0,4)$.

Q4 shape function derivatives at $(\xi,\eta) = (0,0)$ (derived in Section 3.2):
- $\partial N_1/\partial \xi = -1/4$, $\partial N_1/\partial \eta = -1/4$
- $\partial N_2/\partial \xi = +1/4$, $\partial N_2/\partial \eta = -1/4$
- $\partial N_3/\partial \xi = +1/4$, $\partial N_3/\partial \eta = +1/4$
- $\partial N_4/\partial \xi = -1/4$, $\partial N_4/\partial \eta = +1/4$

Compute Jacobian entries:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(5) + \tfrac{1}{4}(5) - \tfrac{1}{4}(0) = 2.5$$

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) - \tfrac{1}{4}(5) + \tfrac{1}{4}(5) + \tfrac{1}{4}(0) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(4) - \tfrac{1}{4}(4) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(0) - \tfrac{1}{4}(0) + \tfrac{1}{4}(4) + \tfrac{1}{4}(4) = 2.0$$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 2.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det J = (2.5)(2.0) - (0)(0) = 5.0$$

**Verification**: Element area $= 5 \times 4 = 20$. Parent area $= 4$. Ratio $= 20/4 = 5 = \det J$. ✓ Since $\det J = 5 > 0$, the mapping is **valid**. The element is a rectangle, hence an undistorted isoparametric element.

---

#### **Question B — (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Derive the **isoparametric stiffness matrix formulation** for a 2D plane problem. State clearly the role of the Jacobian in transforming the integral from $(x,y)$ to $(\xi,\eta)$ coordinates. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A 4-noded isoparametric element has a Jacobian matrix at a Gauss point given by $J = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 & 0.5 \\ 0.0 & 3.0 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute (i) $\det J$, (ii) $J^{-1}$, and (iii) comment on the validity of the mapping. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks:**

Starting with the strain–displacement relation in global coordinates:

$$\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = B_{\text{global}} \mathbf{d}^e, \quad B_{\text{global}} = \begin{bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial x & 0 \\ 0 & \partial N_i/\partial y \\ \partial N_i/\partial y & \partial N_i/\partial x \end{bmatrix}$$

Using the chain rule:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial x \\ \partial N_i/\partial y \end{Bmatrix} = J^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \partial N_i/\partial \xi \\ \partial N_i/\partial \eta \end{Bmatrix}$$

Thus the global $B$-matrix is constructed as $B_{\text{global}} = \tilde{B}\, J^{-1}$.

The stiffness integral in global coordinates:

$$K^e = \int_{A^e} B_{\text{global}}^T\, D\, B_{\text{global}}\, t\, dx\, dy$$

Substituting the mapping $dx\, dy = \det J\, d\xi\, d\eta$:

$$\boxed{K^e = \int_{-1}^{1}\!\!\int_{-1}^{1} \bigl[\tilde{B}\,J^{-1}\bigr]^T\, D\, \bigl[\tilde{B}\,J^{-1}\bigr]\, \det J\, t\, d\xi\, d\eta}$$

**Role of the Jacobian**:
- Encodes the geometry distortion at every integration point.
- $\det J$ scales the differential area $d\xi\,d\eta$ to $dx\,dy$.
- $J^{-1}$ transforms derivatives from natural to global coordinates.
- Validity requires $\det J > 0$ at every Gauss point.

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $J = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 & 0.5 \\ 0.0 & 3.0 \end{bmatrix}$

**(i) Determinant** (2 marks):

$$\det J = (2.0)(3.0) - (0.5)(0.0) = 6.0$$

**(ii) Inverse** (3 marks):

For a $2\times 2$ matrix, $J^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det J}\begin{bmatrix} J_{22} & -J_{12} \\ -J_{21} & J_{11} \end{bmatrix}$:

$$J^{-1} = \frac{1}{6.0}\begin{bmatrix} 3.0 & -0.5 \\ 0.0 & 2.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.0833 \\ 0.0 & 0.3333 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(iii) Validity comment** (2 marks):

Since $\det J = 6.0 > 0$ and finite, the mapping is **one-to-one and orientation-preserving**. The element is *not* inverted or degenerate. The non-zero off-diagonal term $J_{12} = 0.5$ indicates a **skewed (sheared) element** in the global frame, which is acceptable as long as the interior angles remain less than $180^\circ$ and $\det J$ does not change sign within the element.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1**: Students frequently forget the $t\, \det J$ factor in the stiffness integral — leading to a 2-mark deduction per occurrence.
> - **Pitfall 2**: Confusing the Jacobian *matrix* $J$ with its *determinant* $\det J$ — they are different mathematical objects. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> - **Pitfall 3**: When computing $B$ in isoparametric formulation, students often forget to multiply by $J^{-1}$ — yielding the *natural-coordinate* $B$ instead of the *global-coordinate* $B$. This is a 3-mark loss.
> - **Pitfall 4**: Failing to check $\det J > 0$ at *all* Gauss points (not just the centroid) for a distorted element.
> - **Pitfall 5**: Forgetting that **Q4 elements cannot represent curved edges** — they always map to straight-edged quadrilaterals. To get curved boundaries, use Q8 or Q9.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 — Isoparametric Element Requirements | KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision**

- ✅ An **isoparametric element** uses **identical shape functions** for both geometry mapping and field variable interpolation.
- ✅ The **parent (master) element** is the unit square $(\xi,\eta) \in [-1,1]^2$ in 2D and cube in 3D.
- ✅ The **five mandatory requirements**: Completeness, Compatibility, Jacobian Positivity, Adequate Integration, Patch Test.
- ✅ The **Jacobian matrix** $J = \partial(x,y)/\partial(\xi,\eta)$ is the *bridge* between natural and global coordinates.
- ✅ **$\det J > 0$** is the *sine qua non* for a valid, non-inverted isoparametric mapping.
- ✅ $\det J$ represents the **local area ratio** between $dx\,dy$ and $d\xi\,d\eta$.
- ✅ The strain–displacement matrix in natural coordinates: $B = \tilde{B} \cdot J^{-1}$.
- ✅ Element stiffness in iso-form: $K^e = \int B^T D B \det J\, t\, d\xi\, d\eta$.
- ✅ **Full integration** rule: $n_g = p+1$ per direction; **Reduced integration** uses $n_g = p$ per direction (introduces hourglass modes — *one-point reduced Q4 is rank deficient by 1*).
- ✅ **Partition of unity** $\sum N_i = 1$ ensures the element can represent a uniform translation.
- ✅ **Patch test** (Irons, 1963) is the strongest practical convergence test for any element.
- ✅ **Q4 → 2×2 Gauss**; **Q8 / Q9 → 3×3 Gauss** (full integration).
- ✅ For 3D: **H8 (brick) → 2×2×2 Gauss**; **T4 (tet) → 1 point**; **T10 → 4 points**.
- ✅ Distortion limit (rule of thumb): avoid interior angles $> 180^\circ$ and aspect ratios $> 4:1$ for Q4 elements.
- ✅ **Subparametric**: low-order geometry; **Superparametric**: high-order geometry; **Isoparametric**: same order.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy and Mesh Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accuracy and Mesh Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy in FEA** is the quantitative measure of how closely a finite element solution (displacement, stress, temperature, etc.) approximates the exact (analytical) solution of the governing differential equation. **Mesh Distortion** refers to the geometric deviation of an element's actual shape from its ideal, parent-element reference shape during isoparametric mapping, which directly degrades solution accuracy, convergence, and conditioning of the global stiffness matrix.

In the KTU 2024 framework for PEPET632, the term *accuracy* is bound to two inseparable ideas:
1. **Discretization Error** — the difference between the FE solution and the true continuum solution, controlled by mesh density ($h$) and polynomial order ($p$).
2. **Geometric Fidelity** — the preservation of element shape after mapping from the parent (natural) coordinate system $(\xi, \eta, \zeta)$ to the global Cartesian system $(x, y, z)$.

The **fundamental theorem** of FEM accuracy states:

> As the **characteristic element size** $h \rightarrow 0$ and the **polynomial degree** $p \geq 1$, the finite element solution $u^h$ converges to the exact solution $u$ in the energy norm at a rate proportional to $h^p$.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:
$$\|u - u^h\|_{E} \leq C \cdot h^{\min(p, \lambda)}$$

where $\lambda$ is the regularity of the exact solution and $C$ is a constant dependent on element shape, material properties, and boundary conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretchy Grid" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are a cartographer drawing a map of Kerala on a rubber sheet. If you stretch the sheet near Kochi, the squares you drew become long, thin rectangles (distorted). The distances you measure on those rectangles will be wrong, even if you originally drew the grid perfectly. The **mesh** in FEA is that rubber grid, and the **distortion** is the stretching. The **accuracy** of your "map" (the FE solution) depends on how faithfully each grid cell retains its original square shape.

A more practical engineering analogy:

| Domain | Ideal Shape | Distorted Shape | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Civil — Dam Mesh | Square 4-node quad | Long, thin quad (AR > 10) | Stress oscillations, hourglass modes |
| Mechanical — Turbine Blade | Hexahedral brick | Warped/wedge | Spurious zero-energy modes |
| Thermal — PCB Heat Sink | Triangular prism | Skewed triangle | Poor heat-flux prediction |
| Aerospace — Wing Skin | Quad/tri hybrid | Inverted (concave) quad | Negative Jacobian → **simulation crash** |

## 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard FEA Quality Metrics (Industry Defaults — ANSYS/ABAQUS/HyperWorks):**
> - **Aspect Ratio (AR):** Ideal = **1.0**; Acceptable ≤ **5.0**; Critical > **10.0**
> - **Jacobian Ratio (J):** Ideal = **1.0**; Minimum acceptable = **0.2**; Negative = **FATAL**
> - **Skewness Angle ($\theta_{skew}$):** Ideal = **0°**; Maximum = **45°**; Critical > **60°**
> - **Maximum Corner Angle:** Ideal = **90°**; Range = **(45°, 135°)**
> - **Convergence Tolerance (Energy Norm):** **0.1 %** for structural, **0.001** for thermal
> - **L2-Norm Displacement Tolerance:** **0.5 %** of total deformation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Error in Finite Element Analysis

A complete error budget in any FEA simulation arises from **four** distinct sources. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for the KTU board exam.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of FEA Error:**
> 1. **Discretization Error ($e_d$)** — Caused by coarse mesh or low polynomial order.
> 2. **Geometric Error ($e_g$)** — Caused by approximated CAD geometry (faceting of curves).
> 3. **Modelling Error ($e_m$)** — Wrong boundary conditions, material law, or element type.
> 4. **Numerical Error ($e_n$)** — Floating-point round-off, ill-conditioned matrix, integration order.

The **total error** in a measured quantity (e.g., von-Mises stress) is bounded by:
$$e_{total} = \vert e_d \vert + \vert e_g \vert + \vert e_m \vert + \vert e_n \vert$$

## 2.2 Convergence Theory — A Priori Estimates

The classical **Céa-like lemma** in FEA states that the FE solution is the best approximation in the energy space:
$$\|u - u^h\|_E \leq C \cdot \inf_{v^h \in V^h} \|u - v^h\|_E$$

Using interpolation theory, for a $C^0$ Lagrange element of polynomial order $p$ on a mesh of size $h$:
$$\|u - u^h\|_{L_2} \leq C_1 \cdot h^{p+1} \cdot \vert u \vert_{H^{p+1}}$$
$$\|u - u^h\|_{H^1} \leq C_2 \cdot h^{p} \cdot \vert u \vert_{H^{p+1}}$$

where $H^k$ denotes the **Sobolev space** of functions with square-integrable derivatives up to order $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The convergence rate in **displacement ($L_2$) norm** is $O(h^{p+1})$, while in **strain-energy ($H^1$) norm** it is $O(h^p)$. This is why stress (derivative of displacement) converges **slower** than displacement — a cornerstone result.

## 2.3 The Isoparametric Mapping and Jacobian Determinant

The isoparametric concept uses the **same shape functions** $N_i(\xi, \eta)$ to interpolate both geometry and field variables:
$$x(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta) \, x_i, \qquad u(\xi, \eta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(\xi, \eta) \, u_i$$

The **Jacobian matrix** $J$ connects parent and global coordinates:
$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} \\ \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} \end{bmatrix}$$

The **Jacobian determinant** is:
$$\det(J) = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} - \frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** If $\det(J) \leq 0$ at any Gauss point, the element is **inverted** and the simulation will fail. If $\det(J)$ changes sign across the element, it is **non-convex**, violating the isoparametric validity condition.

## 2.4 Mesh Quality Metrics — The Five Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Standard FEA Quality Metrics (Memorise for KTU Board):**

### (a) Aspect Ratio (AR)
$$AR = \frac{L_{max}}{L_{min}} \quad \text{(edge lengths of the element)}$$

### (b) Jacobian Ratio (JR)
$$JR = \frac{\det(J)_{min}}{\det(J)_{max}} \quad \text{over all Gauss points}$$

### (c) Skewness
$$\theta_{skew} = \max \left[ \frac{\theta_i - 90°}{90°} \right] \quad \text{with } \theta_i \in [0°, 180°]$$

### (d) Taper
$$\text{Taper} = \min \left[ \frac{A_i}{A_{ref}} \right] \quad \text{where } A_i \text{ is local area}$$

### (e) Warpage (for 3D shells)
$$\theta_w = \max \vert \theta_{diag,1} - 90° \vert + \vert \theta_{diag,2} - 90° \vert$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy-norm error | $\vert\vert e \vert\vert_E = \left[\int_\Omega B^T D B \, d\Omega - \text{terms}\right]^{1/2}$ | Used in convergence plots |
| 2 | L2 convergence | $\vert\vert u - u^h \vert\vert_2 \leq C h^{p+1}$ | Displacement error |
| 3 | H1 convergence | $\vert\vert u - u^h \vert\vert_1 \leq C h^p$ | Stress/gradient error |
| 4 | Jacobian determinant | $\det(J) = x_{,\xi} y_{,\eta} - y_{,\xi} x_{,\eta}$ | Must be $> 0$ everywhere |
| 5 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = L_{max}/L_{min}$ | Ideal = 1.0 |
| 6 | Skewness | $\theta_{skew} = \max(90° - \theta_{min}, \theta_{max} - 90°)$ | 0 = ideal |
| 7 | Zienkiewicz–Zhu Error | $\eta = \left[ \sum_e \int_{\Omega_e} (\sigma^* - \sigma^h)^T D^{-1} (\sigma^* - \sigma^h) d\Omega \right]^{1/2}$ | A posteriori estimator |
| 8 | Effectivity Index | $\theta_{eff} = \eta / \vert\vert e \vert\vert_E$ | Should approach 1.0 |
| 9 | Richardson Extrapolation | $u_{exact} \approx u_1 + \dfrac{u_1 - u_2}{r^p - 1}$ | $r = h_2/h_1$ |
| 10 | Locking-free condition (CST) | $\vert J \vert$ non-zero and bounded | Avoids volumetric locking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **automotive crashworthiness**, mesh distortion directly controls the predictive fidelity of crumple-zone folding patterns.
> - In **geotechnical FEA (KTU Civil electives)**, distorted elements near tunnel faces predict incorrect plastic-zone sizes.
> - In **additive manufacturing simulations**, severely distorted hexahedral elements cause **non-convergence** of the thermal solve.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy-Norm Error Bound for a Linear Triangular Element (CST)

We wish to derive the rate at which a constant-strain triangle (CST) approximates the displacement field.

**Step 1: State the Interpolation Error.**
For a linear triangle of size $h$, the interpolation error in $L_2$ norm is bounded by:
$$\|u - \Pi_h u\|_{L_2(K)} \leq C_1 h^2 \vert u \vert_{H^2(K)}$$

**Step 2: Use the Bramble–Hilbert Lemma.**
Bramble–Hilbert lemma states that for a quasi-uniform mesh of triangles, the interpolation operator $\Pi_h$ satisfies:
$$\|u - \Pi_h u\|_{H^1(K)} \leq C_2 h \vert u \vert_{H^2(K)}$$

**Step 3: Apply Ceá's Lemma.**
The FE solution is the **Ritz projection** of $u$ in $V^h$, so:
$$\|u - u^h\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \leq C_3 \inf_{v^h \in V^h} \|u - v^h\|_{H^1(\Omega)}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Interpolation Error.**
Taking $v^h = \Pi_h u$ in the infimum:
$$\|u - u^h\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \leq C_3 \, \|u - \Pi_h u\|_{H^1(\Omega)}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Bramble–Hilbert Result.**
$$\|u - u^h\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \leq C_3 \, C_2 \, h \, \left( \sum_{K \in \mathcal{T}_h} \vert u \vert_{H^2(K)}^2 \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 6: Final Result.**
$$\boxed{\|u - u^h\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \leq C \, h \, \vert u \vert_{H^2(\Omega)}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the famous $O(h)$ convergence for a **linear** element. Notice that the exponent equals $p = 1$. For a quadratic ($p = 2$) triangle (LST), the result becomes $O(h^2)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jacobian Determinant for a Distorted Quadrilateral

Consider a 4-node bilinear quadrilateral with global nodes:
- Node 1: $(0, 0)$, Node 2: $(a + \delta, 0)$
- Node 3: $(b, c)$, Node 4: $(0, d)$

The shape functions are:
$$N_1 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1-\eta), \quad N_2 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1-\eta)$$
$$N_3 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)(1+\eta), \quad N_4 = \tfrac{1}{4}(1-\xi)(1+\eta)$$

**Step 1: Express the Mapping.**
$$x(\xi, \eta) = N_1 \cdot 0 + N_2 \cdot (a+\delta) + N_3 \cdot b + N_4 \cdot 0$$
$$x = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[(a+\delta)(1+\xi)(1-\eta) + b(1+\xi)(1+\eta)\right]$$

**Step 2: Compute Partial Derivatives.**
$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \xi} = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[(a+\delta)(1-\eta) + b(1+\eta)\right]$$
$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \eta} = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[-(a+\delta)(1+\xi) + b(1+\xi)\right] = \tfrac{1}{4}(1+\xi)\left[b - (a+\delta)\right]$$

Similarly for $y$:
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \xi} = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[(0)(1-\eta) + c(1+\eta) - 0 - d(1-\eta)\right] = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[c(1+\eta) - d(1-\eta)\right]$$
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial \eta} = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[-(0)(1+\xi) + c(1+\xi) - 0 - d(1-\xi)\right] = \tfrac{1}{4}\left[c(1+\xi) + d(1-\xi)\right]$$

**Step 3: Construct the Jacobian Matrix.**
$$J = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} (a+\delta)(1-\eta) + b(1+\eta) & (1+\xi)[b-(a+\delta)] \\ c(1+\eta) - d(1-\eta) & c(1+\xi) + d(1-\xi) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at Centre $(\xi, \eta) = (0, 0)$.**
At the centre, $(1 \pm \xi) = 1$ and $(1 \pm \eta) = 1$:
$$J(0,0) = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} (a+\delta) + b & b - (a+\delta) \\ c - d & c + d \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Determinant.**
$$\det(J) = \frac{1}{16}\left[(a+\delta+b)(c+d) - (b-a-\delta)(c-d)\right]$$

Expanding:
$$\det(J) = \frac{1}{16}\left[ (a+\delta+b)(c+d) - (b-a-\delta)(c-d) \right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{16}\left[ (a+\delta+b)(c+d) - (b-a-\delta)(c-d) \right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{16}\left[ (a+\delta)c + (a+\delta)d + bc + bd - bc + (a+\delta)c + bd - (a+\delta)d \right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{16}\left[ 2(a+\delta)c + 2bd \right]$$
$$\det(J) = \frac{1}{8}\left[ (a+\delta)c + bd \right]$$

**Step 6: Condition for Validity.**
For the element to be valid:
$$\det(J) > 0 \iff (a+\delta)c + bd > 0$$

If $\delta \to -a$ (i.e., Node 2 collapses toward Node 1), then:
$$\det(J) \to \frac{bd}{8}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** A parallelogram with $a = b$ and $c = d$ has $\det(J) = ac/4$. Any departure from this proportion reduces $\det(J)$, and for $\delta$ very negative or with crossed edges, $\det(J)$ becomes negative — signalling element inversion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — A Posteriori Error Estimate (Zienkiewicz–Zhu)

**Step 1: Define the FE Stress.**
The stress at any point is recovered from nodal displacements:
$$\sigma^h = D \cdot B \cdot u^h$$

**Step 2: Project to a Smoother Field $\sigma^*$.**
Using the $L_2$ projection or SPR (Superconvergent Patch Recovery) technique:
$$\sigma^* = N \cdot \tilde{\sigma}$$

where $\tilde{\sigma}$ are the smoothed nodal stresses obtained by least-squares fitting on a patch of elements surrounding each node.

**Step 3: Define Element-wise Error.**
$$e_\sigma^{(e)} = \sigma - \sigma^h \approx \sigma^* - \sigma^h$$

**Step 4: Compute Energy Norm of Error.**
$$\|e_\sigma\|_E = \left[ \sum_{e=1}^{N_{el}} \int_{\Omega_e} (\sigma^* - \sigma^h)^T D^{-1} (\sigma^* - \sigma^h) \, d\Omega \right]^{1/2}$$

**Step 5: Define Effectivity Index.**
$$\theta_{eff} = \frac{\|e_\sigma\|_E}{\|u - u^h\|_E}$$

A well-posed estimator yields $\theta_{eff} \to 1.0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Zienkiewicz–Zhu estimator is the **most-cited** a posteriori error indicator in commercial FEA codes (ABAQUS, ANSYS). It is the workhorse of **adaptive mesh refinement** — driving $h$-refinement where the error is high.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hourglass Energy for Under-Integrated Quad

A 4-node quad with **1-point reduced integration** (URI) exhibits an hourglass mode $\gamma$ with shape:
$$\Gamma_i = h_x \cdot h_y \cdot \frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \xi}\frac{\partial N_i}{\partial \eta}$$

The **hourglass stiffness** is:
$$K_{hg} = \frac{1}{12} E \, t \, \Gamma^T \Gamma$$

and the energy stored in hourglass modes is:
$$U_{hg} = \frac{1}{2} q^T K_{hg} q$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $K_{hg} = 0$ (e.g., linear materials, parallelogram mesh, **distorted to a perfect rectangle**), the element has a **zero-energy mode**, leading to spurious kinematic oscillations that destroy accuracy. This is why URI elements require **artificial hourglass control** or **distortion-aware stabilization**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Convergence vs. Mesh Refinement — The Famous FEA Log-Log Plot

The following Mermaid block renders the convergence behaviour for $h$-refinement and $p$-refinement, which is the **single most important concept** in this module.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Initial Coarse Mesh] --> B[Run FEA Solver]
    B --> C[Compute Error Norm]
    C --> D{Error within Tolerance?}
    D -->|No| E[Refine Mesh: h halve or p increase]
    E --> B
    D -->|Yes| F[Converged Solution]

    G[Linear Element p=1] --> G1[Slope = 1 on log-log]
    H[Quadratic Element p=2] --> H1[Slope = 2 on log-log]
    I[Cubic Element p=3] --> I1[Slope = 3 on log-log]

    G1 --> PLOT[Convergence Plot]
    H1 --> PLOT
    I1 --> PLOT

    PLOT --> X[log h: characteristic mesh size]
    PLOT --> Y[log error: energy norm]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Adaptive FEA Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CAD Geometry Import] --> B[Initial Mesh Generator]
    B --> C[FEA Solver Module]
    C --> D[Post-Processor]
    D --> E[Error Estimator Zienkiewicz-Zhu]
    E --> F{Error Threshold Met}
    F -->|NO| G[Adaptive Refinement Engine]
    G --> H{h-refine OR p-refine}
    H -->|Mesh split| B
    H -->|Order increase| C
    F -->|YES| I[Final Accurate Solution]
```

## 4.3 Taxonomy of Mesh Distortion — Visual Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Mesh Distortion Types] --> A1[Aspect Ratio Distortion]
    ROOT --> A2[Skewness Distortion]
    ROOT --> A3[Warping Distortion]
    ROOT --> A4[Jacobian Distortion]
    ROOT --> A5[Taper Distortion]

    A1 --> A1A[Long thin elements cause ill-conditioning]
    A2 --> A2A[Non-orthogonal angles degrade interpolation]
    A3 --> A3A[Non-planar nodes break shell theory]
    A4 --> A4A[Negative Jacobian equals element inversion FATAL]
    A5 --> A5A[Stepped element edges bias gradient recovery]
```

## 4.4 Isoparametric Mapping Flow — Parent to Global

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Parent Element in xi-eta Space] --> SH[Shape Functions N_i of xi-eta]
    SH --> MP[Mapping x = Sum N_i x_i]
    MP --> JJ[Jacobian Matrix J = partial x, partial y over partial xi, partial eta]
    JJ --> JD{det J Computation}
    JD --> VALID{Positive det J at all Gauss points}
    VALID -->|YES| OK[Element Acceptable]
    VALID -->|NO| FAIL[Simulation Aborted - Inverted Element]
    JJ --> INV[Global Stiffness Assembly]
    OK --> INV
```

## 4.5 Solution Path: From Mesh Distortion to Loss of Accuracy

```mermaid
graph LR
    MD[Mesh Distortion] --> IM[Ill-conditioned Jacobian]
    IM --> NCM[Numerical Conditioning Matrix]
    NCM --> RoundingErrors[Floating Point Errors Amplified]
    RoundingErrors --> SP[Spurious Stress Oscillations]
    SP --> LSA[Loss of Solution Accuracy]
    LSA --> NG[Non-Convergence of Iterative Solvers]
```
